data.title: Time is running out to save Britain’s pharma industry
data.description:
The timing could hardly have been worse. Britain’s pharmaceutical industry was already struggling with arguments over the prices that the NHS pays for its medicines, attacks on vaccines, rising competition from China and restrictions in the crucial American market.

On Friday, Donald Trump slapped 100pc tariffs on imports into the US. An industry once regarded as the jewel in the crown of the British economy is facing a crisis – and time is running out to save it.

The president’s tariff wars may have settled down across most industrial sectors. But life sciences is the one exception.

Trump announced that from the start of next month, pharmaceutical imports into the US would face 100pc tariffs unless the company making them was already building a factory in America.

The UK, which exports more than £5bn of medicines to the US every year, is likely to be especially badly hit by that, and the share prices of all the major companies in the sector were down sharply on the news.

It is not as if the industry was in great shape to start with. Lord Vallance, the science minister, argued this week that the NHS would have to increase its spending on medicines if it were to prevent an exodus of manufacturers from the UK.

The American giant Merck this month decided to scrap a £1bn investment in the UK, arguing that the health system was not doing enough to support innovation in the sector, while AstraZeneca, Britain’s largest company by market value, put a £200m investment in Cambridge on hold, following its decision to cancel an investment in Liverpool at the start of the year.

In the background, there have been a whole series of attacks on vaccines, especially from Robert F Kennedy, Trump’s controversial health secretary. And, although it is not often discussed, like many Western industries, it faces a hyper-competitive China that is starting to lead the world in innovation and technology.

China has already overtaken the United States in the number of pharma patents filed, and it is also leading in AI-driven drug discovery. If you have the patents, it will not be long before you have world-leading companies, since controlling the technology is the key to long-term success.

It will become increasingly difficult for a relatively small economy such as the UK to remain competitive. Indeed, the UK’s share of global R&D investment in healthcare has fallen from 7.3pc to 5.7pc over the last three years alone, while the UK has fallen from 4th to 10th place in the global rankings for the number of Phase III clinical trials hosted. The days when global pharma was a British/American/Swiss industry are long in the past.

That matters. The UK is no longer a world leader in many globally significant industries, but life sciences was definitely one of them. We are home to two of the global leaders – GSK and AstraZeneca – and we have a thriving industry of smaller companies and start-ups, along with the investors to back them, that feed new ideas and technologies into the pipeline.

The industry directly contributed £17bn of value added to the UK economy, according to the industry’s trade body, the ABPI, and an additional £45bn in R&D spillover revenues. There is very little else that comes close to it in its importance.

Sure, the very biggest companies may well be able to get around the president’s latest round of tariffs. The global giants already have plants in the US or can build them if necessary (indeed, AstraZeneca has already pledged to spend $50bn (£37bn) on US factories and may even move its listing to New York).

Ireland, a low-tax offshore manufacturing hub for the US giants, will be hit more severely than the UK. However, it will be far harder for smaller companies and biotech start-ups to establish themselves. After all, it is already terrifyingly expensive to develop new medicines, and adding an American factory to circumvent the tariffs will typically prove unaffordable.

Likewise, the global giants have sales all over the world and don’t depend on orders from the NHS. However, it is far harder for smaller companies and start-ups. There are very few successful global industries that do not have a thriving domestic market, and the UK no longer offers that.

That has to change. There are two big things that need to happen.

First, Britain needs to cut a deal with the Trump administration on tariffs. The president likes the UK – that much was clear from the lavish state visit this month – and we don’t have a huge surplus in medical supplies. A deal could be struck whereby any drug with global sales exceeding $1bn would be manufactured on American soil, while smaller companies would be granted tariff-free access to the US market. That would preserve the start-up culture that the industry needs to thrive.

Next, the NHS budget is always under pressure, but it has also been significantly increased over the last few years and is likely to rise again in the November Budget. Instead of spending all the cash on higher salaries and even more generous pensions for its staff, more of the money should be allocated to buying drugs. That is the only way the British industry can thrive and generate the tax revenues the NHS needs to survive.

Along with finance and arguably aerospace, the pharmaceutical sector was one of the few areas of the British economy that competed on a global stage. It generated a huge number of well-paid jobs. However, it is currently steadily slipping down the global league, and if the Government does not offer it some assistance, it may soon be too late to save it.

data.title: Bankers get bonuses faster as Reeves pressures regulators to axe red tape
data.description:
The Bank of England has halved the time City bankers must wait to get their bonuses as Rachel Reeves pressures regulators to find ways to boost growth.

Senior bankers must now wait four years instead of eight to receive their full performance awards in a loosening of the rules introduced in the wake of the 2008 financial crisis.

The overhaul is aimed at promoting “responsible risk-taking” in the City of London. The changes come into force on Thursday in time for the round of bonuses this winter.

The changes go further than expected. The Bank previously suggested that the time limit should be cut to five years. The central bank has already scrapped a cap on bankers’ bonuses.

The loosening comes as the Chancellor pressures Britain’s regulators to slash burdensome rules to increase growth in Britain’s economy. She warned last year that regulation had gone too far, with institutions now “regulating for risk, but not regulating for growth”.

Ms Reeves has pressured watchdogs to loosen rules governing the City, planning and competition. Earlier this year the chairman of the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) was ousted and replaced with a veteran Amazon executive after ministers were underwhelmed by the watchdog’s pro-growth proposals.

Sam Woods, the chief executive of the Prudential Regulation Authority, said: “These changes are the latest example of our commitment to boosting UK competitiveness.

“These new rules will cut red tape without encouraging the reckless pay structures that contributed to the 2008 financial crisis.”

‘Working faster and smarter’

The timing of the changes may give the Chancellor a boost in November’s Budget if the Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR) decides to score the change as a positive contributor to growth. The fiscal watchdog will deliver an assessment of the economy’s prospects alongside Ms Reeves’s statement.

The Chancellor has announced a flurry of changes to planning rules in recent days as she pressures the OBR to take a more positive view of the outlook.

Sarah Pritchard, the deputy chief executive of the Financial Conduct Authority, said: “We’re working faster and smarter to support growth by letting firms apply the changes to this year’s pay cycle.

“The new rules also mean senior managers will continue to follow our high standards and remain on the hook where poor decisions affect consumers and markets”

The limits on bankers bonuses, including a mandatory eight-year “deferral periods”, were first introduced in 2014 in line with similar rules introduced by the European Union.

The wait times give banks the option to claw back bonuses from bankers if their investment decisions end up causing problems. The restrictions were aimed at curtailing the excessive risk-taking that contributed to the 2008 crash.

The Bank of England lifted an EU-imposed cap on bankers bonuses in 2023, which blocked banks from paying out awards worth more than two-times an employee’s salary.

Andrew Bailey, the Bank’s Governor, has previously cautioned that regulators must be wary not to scarp too many rules in pursuit of growth.

Earlier this year he said: “We must not forget the lasting damage done by the global financial crisis. There is no trade-off between economic growth and financial stability.”

data.title: The lesser-known financial benefits of joint accounts
data.description:
Joint bank accounts have traditionally been used by couples to pool their money to pay for household bills and other shared expenses.

However, research by digital wealth manager, Moneyfarm, shows that almost a third (31pc) of British couples in long-term relationships now refuse to open one at all. Four in 10 view joint accounts as outdated, old-fashioned or even sexist.

Yet despite their declining popularity, joint bank accounts still offer a number of financial benefits – and not just for couples. You can open one with a friend, family member or housemate, too – and the potential benefits go far beyond making sure you’ve got enough for your next household bill.

Here, Telegraph Money sets out some of the more surprising financial benefits of opening a joint bank account.

  • Easing probate
  • Inheritance tax advantages
  • Helping to manage finances
  • Advantages for expats
  • What to watch out for

Easing probate

Money held in a joint account automatically passes to the survivor if one of the account holders dies. This can be invaluable if the joint account holder is responsible for covering essential expenses while an estate is going through probate – a process that can take many months. During this time, accounts from the deceased will be locked until probate is granted, but with a joint account the surviving account holder can continue to access joint funds to cover essential expenses.

Duncan Bailey, of law firm Brabners Personal, said: “The surviving partner will need to show a death certificate to the bank to have the account transferred solely into their name. Once this is done, the surviving partner can typically continue using the joint account or regain full access very quickly, without waiting for a grant of probate. This can be a major practical advantage for paying ongoing bills or funeral costs.”

Inheritance tax advantages

For couples who are married or in a civil partnership, transfers between account holders are exempt from inheritance tax.

However, joint accounts aren’t limited to your partner, as you can also open one with a child, other relative, or a friend. In some instances, this arrangement could offer inheritance tax advantages, although it’s not guaranteed and it is essential to seek professional financial advice first.

Mr Bailey said: “Whether inheritance tax applies depends on the specific circumstances, which can sometimes be complex. The crucial factor for inheritance tax purposes is beneficial ownership, which is who actually owns and contributed to the funds in the account.”

One example Telegraph tax expert Mike Warburton recently considered was a reader asking if they could avoid inheritance tax by opening a joint bank account with their daughter. HMRC guidelines suggest that if you simply paid money into this joint account, it would still be ‘your’ money and would form part of your estate on death.

But if you did this and made a declaration that the beneficial ownership rests entirely with your daughter, this would count as a lifetime gift to her at that time. If you were to survive seven years, the account would fall outside your estate under the seven-year rule. If you died sooner it may incur a reduced tapered tax rate – assuming it is not covered by the tax-free nil-rate band.

Because the rules are complex, you should keep clear records of all deposits and withdrawals to show who contributed what, in case HMRC makes enquiries later.

Helping to manage finances

Some people use a joint bank account to help manage a relative’s finances, such as paying bills and direct debits, which can be useful in instances where they struggle with online shopping or banking, for example, but still have capacity to be in charge of their own money.

But while this can work informally, it is not a legal substitute for a lasting power of attorney should they need something more formal to be set up, warns Stephen Horscroft, of law firm Birketts.

He said: “A bank will typically freeze a joint account if one of the account holders loses capacity. This is because joint accounts rely on the consent of both parties, and the bank has a duty to protect the vulnerable account holder and prevent potential misuse or fraud.

“While essential transactions may still be allowed at the bank’s discretion, non-essential withdrawals are usually blocked until a registered LPA or Deputyship Order can be provided.”

Advantages for expats

A joint account could also prove useful if you have a friend or relative living overseas who is unable to open a UK account in their sole name due to residency issues.

Mr Horscroft said: “By holding a joint account with a UK-based relative, it can be more straightforward to access UK banking services, pay bills, or receive transfers without the requirement for a UK address.”

However, he also points out that banks may have restrictions on non-resident account holders, and anti-money laundering regulations must still be followed.

What to watch out for

Despite their many advantages, joint accounts come with risks. Most importantly, you should only ever open a joint account with someone you trust. Each person has equal rights to access and withdraw funds, so if things go wrong, it could be very difficult to recover lost money.

Emma Davies, of law firm Laytons, said: “If either party finds themselves in financial difficulty (for example, bankruptcy or debt), the funds in the joint account will be considered. It is often the case that ownership is presumed to be equal, and it can be difficult to prove that this is not the case.

“In this case, it is possible that joint accounts could be frozen temporarily which would limit access to both/all owners – even those who are not involved in the dispute.”

Another important consideration is that opening a joint account creates a financial association on your credit files. If the other person has poor credit, this association could affect your ability to borrow.

Finally, although a joint account can help with access to money after death or incapacity, it shouldn’t replace formal arrangements like a will or lasting power of attorney. Always seek professional advice before making significant decisions.

data.title: GSK chief to step down after nine years in charge
data.description:
Dame Emma Walmsley will step down as GSK’s chief executive at the end of the year, concluding nine years at the helm of Britain’s second-largest pharmaceuticals company.

The £62bn FTSE giant announced on Monday that Dame Emma will be replaced by Luke Miels, the chief commercial officer, who joined in 2017 and is responsible for overseeing vaccines and medicines globally.

He will take charge of the business in January, at a time when the industry is under pressure from Donald Trump.

The US president has threatened 100pc tariffs on drugmakers if they aren’t investing in America.

Dame Emma, who was the first woman to run a major pharmaceutical company when she became boss of GSK in 2017, is stepping down after struggling to boost the company’s share price.

Since her appointment, the company’s valuation has fallen by roughly 10pc. GSK shares rose by more than 3pc following the announcement.

Dame Emma said: “2026 is a pivotal year for GSK to define its path for the decade ahead and I believe the right moment for new leadership.

“As CEO, you hope to leave the company you love stronger than you found it and prepare for seamless succession.”

During her time as chief executive, Dame Emma spun off GSK’s consumer brands division to create Haleon, listing the business on the London Stock Exchange after rejecting a £50bn offer from Unilever in 2022.

This followed heightened scrutiny over the company’s strategy as critics claimed it lacked direction. At the time, GSK was selling products ranging from Sensodyne toothpaste to hepatitis vaccines.

Since then, analysts have claimed the drugmaker has struggled to develop an exciting pipeline of new medicines, something Dame Emma said was her biggest priority when taking charge.

In particular, investors are concerned that GSK has made no headway in fast-growing areas like obesity drugs, while being overly reliant on vaccines where demand is weakening.

Dame Emma is one of London’s highest-paid chief executives, commanding a pay package of £10.6m last year. This was down from £12.7m the year prior.

Sir Jonathan Symonds, the GSK chairman, said: “On behalf of the board, I want to thank Emma and acknowledge her outstanding leadership in delivering a strategic transformation of GSK, including the successful demerger of Haleon.

“GSK today is necessarily very different to the company she was appointed to nine years ago and has a bright and ambitious future.”

data.title: Wall Street bank scrambles to contain fallout from private credit implosion
data.description:
Bosses at a US banking giant are scrambling to restore confidence in their business after getting caught up in a $12bn (£9bn) bankruptcy case that has sent tremors through Wall Street and beyond.

Jefferies, an investment bank worth $10bn, has been forced to reassure investors about its financial resilience following the collapse of car parts maker First Brands Group. A business partly owned by Jefferies had extended hundreds of millions of dollars in loans to First Brands before its bankruptcy.

Major US investors including Morgan Stanley and BlackRock have all reportedly tried to pull money from the Jefferies-backed fund in recent days as panic about the crisis spread.

Jefferies’ shares have plunged more than 20pc so far this month. Other US bank stocks have also dropped amid fears that there could be further financial landmines lurking in the opaque $3tn world of private corporate debt.

In a letter issued on Sunday, Rich Handler, Jefferies’ chief executive, and Brian Friedman, the bank’s president, railed against “inaccurate or conflated allegations or assertions” and insisted the bank could cope with First Brands’ collapse.

“We believe there has been an impact on our equity market value and credit perception that is meaningfully overdone, and we expect this to correct as soon as the facts and range of outcomes are better understood,” the letter said.

Shares in the bank were up around 1.8pc in pre-market trading.

The bank is hoping to prevent a race to pull money out of its part-owned unit Point Bonita Capital, a $3bn specialist trade-finance fund that has $715m of exposure to First Brands.

Jefferies said Point Bonita would meet redemption requests next year through four quarterly payments, giving the fund time to raise money for the payouts.

“We take this matter extremely seriously and will do everything in our power to recover the money and assets that are rightfully owned by our co-investors in Point Bonita,” the letter said.

The bank said its own direct exposure to any losses came via a 5.9pc stake, worth $43m, in Point Bonita’s financing of First Brands, plus $2m of loans to the defunct company through Apex, a Jefferies-owned debt vehicle.

“Relative to the scale of Jefferies, we are confident that any losses or expenses from these investments or otherwise in respect of First Brands can readily be absorbed and do not threaten our financial condition or business momentum,” the letter said.

Shadow banking fears

Swiss bank UBS has also been caught up in the collapse of First Brands. The involvement of top tier banks has spurred fears of wider problems lurking in the $3tn “shadow banking” sector, as the private credit market is known. The collapse of a subprime car finance company, Tricolour, has added fuel to the fire.

Private credit involves companies borrowing from specialist funds, often run by private equity, rather than banks. The market is lightly regulated and not very transparent. It has expanded 50pc as the prospect of turbo-charged returns has drawn in pension funds and other more traditionally conservative lenders and investors.

The Bank of England has in the past raised concerns about the potential issues in the market given its rapid growth.

Ratings agency Fitch recently highlighted the sector’s “bubble-like attributes, including rapid growth and financial innovation, spread compression, heightened competition, growing retail participation and rising borrower leverage”.

However, Fitch said private credit was still “a relatively small portion of the overall financial system”.

Jefferies argued that the issues with First Brands were specific to the company.

Executives said the collapse was “the result of decisions and actions at First Brands, including possible fraudulent or otherwise improper activity that is under investigation by the First Brands’ chief restructuring officer and reportedly under investigation by the United States Department of Justice.”

Patrick James, First Brands’ founder and chief executive, resigned on Monday. A spokesman for Mr James told the Financial Times he “always prioritised the company’s interests above his own, and he remains deeply committed to the success of the business and the maximisation of value for the benefit of its customers, suppliers, employees and lenders”.

First Brands employs 26,000 people and counts $5bn a year in sales, supplying auto parts to most of America’s biggest retailers.

It filed for bankruptcy in late September, amid questions over its use of trade finance, where invoices for money it was owed were used as collateral for loans. Corporate regulators are now investigating.

data.title: Western executives who visit China are coming back terrified
data.description:
“It’s the most humbling thing I’ve ever seen,” said Ford’s chief executive about his recent trip to China.

After visiting a string of factories, Jim Farley was left astonished by the technical innovations being packed into Chinese cars – from self-driving software to facial recognition.

“Their cost and the quality of their vehicles is far superior to what I see in the West,” Farley warned in July.

“We are in a global competition with China, and it’s not just EVs. And if we lose this, we do not have a future at Ford.”

The car industry boss is not the only Western executive to have returned shaken following a visit to the Far East.

Andrew Forrest, the Australian billionaire behind mining giant Fortescue – which is investing massively in green energy – says his trips to China convinced him to abandon his company’s attempts to manufacture electric vehicle powertrains in-house.

“I can take you to factories [in China] now, where you’ll basically be alongside a big conveyor and the machines come out of the floor and begin to assemble parts,” he says.

“And you’re walking alongside this conveyor, and after about 800, 900 metres, a truck drives out. There are no people – everything is robotic.”

Other executives describe vast, “dark factories” where robots do so much of the work alone that there is no need to even leave the lights on for humans.

“We visited a dark factory producing some astronomical number of mobile phones,” recalls Greg Jackson, the boss of British energy supplier Octopus.

“The process was so heavily automated that there were no workers on the manufacturing side, just a small number who were there to ensure the plant was working.

“You get this sense of a change, where China’s competitiveness has gone from being about government subsidies and low wages to a tremendous number of highly skilled, educated engineers who are innovating like mad.”

High-tech transformation

It’s also a far cry from the cheap “Made in China” goods that many Westerners have associated with the “workshop of the world” in the past, underscoring how much cash has been poured into upgrading China’s industrial processes.

Far from being focused on low-quality products, China is now viewed as a leader in rapidly-growing, high-value technologies such as electric vehicles (EVs), batteries, solar panels, wind turbines, drones and advanced robotics.

A big part of that transformation is down to the country’s focus on automation – which has been encouraged by the ruling communist government and heavily supported with state subsidies, grants and local government policies.

Figures recently released by the International Federation of Robotics (IFR) show this has led to a dramatic and high-tech transformation of China’s industrial base over the past 10 years.

Between 2014 and 2024, the number of industrial robots deployed in the country rocketed from 189,000 to more than two million.

These can typically include everything from robot arms used for welding, assembly and loading, spider robots used for high-speed “pick and place” movements and overhead gantry robots for precision tasks such as 3D printing.

The overall number of robots added in China last year was 295,000, compared to 27,000 in Germany, 34,000 in the US and just 2,500 in the UK.

And while it would be easy to put this disparity down to population size alone, China also blows its western rivals out of the water when it comes to robot density. It now boasts 567 robots for every 10,000 manufacturing workers, compared to 449 for Germany, 307 for the US and 104 in the UK.

More automation is seen by many as good for productivity, the all-important measure of how much an economy gets out of what it puts in.

Many analysts also note that China’s growing share of worldwide manufacturing gives it increasing leverage over global supply chains – and would make it a formidable opponent in a war.

But alongside Beijing’s stated desire to dominate industries of the future, Rian Whitton, an expert at Bismarck Analysis, says increased automation is also an attempt to mitigate the impact of the country’s ageing population.

“China has quite a notable demographic problem but its manufacturing is, generally, quite labour-intensive,” he says.

“So in a pre-emptive fashion, they want to automate it as much as possible, not because they expect they’ll be able to get higher margins – that is usually the idea in the West – but to compensate for this population decline and to get a competitive advantage.”

As part of its so-called Made in China programme, local authorities have offered large tax breaks that reimburse firms for a fifth of their spending on industrial robots. This is under a policy known as “jiqi huanren” – which translates to “replacing humans with machines”.

Western manufacturers in trouble

But this technology, coupled with the vast output of Chinese manufacturers, spells serious trouble for traditional Western brands.

The most visible sign of this upheaval is on our roads, where Chinese-made electric and hybrid cars are taking a growing share of sales.

In Britain, Shenzhen-based BYD multiplied its September sales by a factor of 10 this year – overtaking far more established brands such as Mini, Renault and Land Rover.

But unlike the “tragic” cars once mocked by Jeremy Clarkson and his colleagues on Top Gear, BYD’s recent efforts have been praised for both their low prices and their well-appointed interiors.

“The most striking thing about their automotive industry is the pace and the speed with which it operates,” says Mike Hawes, the chief executive of the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders (SMMT).

“They can develop and execute models in probably half of the time most European car makers can.”

Sander Tordoir, the chief economist at the Centre for European Reform, a think tank, says Europe and Britain must try to boost their own deployment of robotics if they want to keep up with the pace of innovation in China – while also keeping their manufacturing industries alive.

“Robotics, if deployed well, can lift the productivity of your economy greatly. And if China is extremely good at it, then we should try to catch up because, like China, a lot of Europe is ageing,” he says.

“The second reason to care is because the robotics sector is high value and has spillovers for the military industrial sector, so the fact that China may be ahead is also significant from a security standpoint.

“I think the debate is about how to use industrial policy to build competitive markets, and that will inevitably include some support to offset China’s distortions and advantages, which are not all market-driven.”

Britain is falling behind

The risk, however, is that “we don’t create the new, or we trap workers in the old instead of trying to leap forward,” Tordoir warns, pointing to the tendency of politicians to prevent ageing steel and car factories from closing instead of encouraging the creation of newer, high-tech jobs for workers to move to.

But Britain’s record on robots is poor and it has struggled to add more than a few thousand per year, despite already having less than half as many as France.

Last year, UK robot additions fell by 35pc.

Whitton, at Bismarck Analysis, argues that Britain, which has lagged other countries in productivity growth, should focus on trying to improve its competitiveness by incentivising the adoption of more robotics as well as machine tools.

He says this would have a bigger impact than past tax-breaks designed to boost research and development spending and plant machinery adoption.

“It doesn’t appear that dilly dallying around tax changes is doing a hell of a lot,” says Whitton.

“But I see the Government throwing billions of pounds each year at completely speculative rubbish like green hydrogen or to fulfil renewable [energy] obligations contracts and I just think, ‘Well, why not five billion a year in grants for capital equipment?

“That would arguably get a bigger bang for our buck than a lot of the energy-related industrial policies we pursue.”

Counter-intuitively, Whitton says countries which had more automation during the first “China shock” of the 2000s – which flooded the world with cheap goods – managed to hold on to a greater share of industrial jobs.

“People talk a lot about how automation will lead to job losses,” he adds. “But actually, the job losses are going to be disproportionately in the countries that don’t automate.”

In other words, failing to modernise will almost certainly lead to more dark factories in the West. But the kind where no work at all is happening.

data.title: The Arab media giant accused of pushing Hamas propaganda
data.description:
As media outlets around the globe marked the second anniversary of the Oct 7 attacks on Tuesday, Al Jazeera’s editorial line was a notable outlier.

Emblazoned across the Arabic broadcaster’s English-language website was the headline: “Israel’s genocide continues across Gaza two years since start of war”.

The article brushed over the terrorist atrocities, in which almost 1,200 Israelis were murdered and 250 were taken hostage, referring to them as attacks carried out by “fighters” from Hamas’s notorious military wing, the Al-Qassam Brigades.

It instead focused on Palestinian deaths and America’s funding of Israel.

It is just one example of the fiercely partisan position taken by Al Jazeera, which is funded and run by Qatar’s ruling family.

For many critics, however, the bias goes much further.

The network has repeatedly been accused of acting as a mouthpiece for Hamas propaganda, while Israeli authorities have even alleged that some of its journalists are embedded in the terrorist organisation.

This is against the backdrop of Israel barring the majority of Western journalists from entering Gaza, making it difficult for publications of any kind to verify information.

Meanwhile, even after Israel and Hamas agreed to the first stage of a tentative peace deal and a ceasefire began, some question whether Al Jazeera will tone down its rhetoric.

Either way, there is no doubting the crucial role that Al Jazeera’s coverage has played in swaying opinion since Oct 7.

“I think Al Jazeera had a very significant impact in both Arabic and English, even though they have very different editorial standards,” says Omar Al-Ghazzi, associate professor at the London School of Economics.

“They amplified really what’s happening and thinking through a Palestinian perspective, which was very absent from Western media outlets.”

Accusations that Al Jazeera supports Hamas date back to the 2006 Palestinian elections, when the nationalist Fatah party accused the channel of incitement and called for a boycott.

In fact, Al Jazeera’s alleged support for Islamist causes has made it unpopular among most of its Arab neighbours, who fear Qatar is using it to sow unrest.

In 2011, Egypt ordered the closure of Al Jazeera’s bureau in the country, accusing it of encouraging the Arab Spring uprising, while Saudi Arabia previously banned the channel over claims it was promoting terror groups.

Qatar founded Al Jazeera in 1996 with an initial loan of more than £100m and has continued to bankroll it ever since. The broadcaster last month appointed Nasser al Thani, a member of Qatar’s ruling family, as its chief executive.

Since the start of the conflict, Al Jazeera has gained more prominence on the global stage by becoming one of the few non-Israeli media outlets to report directly from Gaza through a network of Palestinian reporters.

Immediately, however, there were signs of its allegiances.

Just hours after terrorists stormed into Israel, Al Jazeera Arabic broadcast statements from senior Hamas officials urging Palestinians to rise up across the West Bank.

This included a recording of Mohammed Deif, the Hamas military chief who has since been killed, calling on people to “kill, burn, destroy, and shut down roads”.

Al Jazeera also played a key role in circulating a propaganda clip that showed terrorists comforting captive Israeli children in an apparent effort to play down the scale of the atrocities.

As the conflict escalated, Al Jazeera’s English outlet published “multiple false and misleading claims”, according to the news rating organisation NewsGuard.

This includes stories accusing Israel of fabricating reports about the systematic rape of Israeli women during the Oct 7 attacks.

That is despite evidence uncovered by the UN and other media organisations, including the BBC and The Guardian.

Several Al Jazeera reporters celebrated the attacks on social media, while some have been accused of cutting off interviewees if they express criticisms of Hamas.

In one incident, a wounded Palestinian man was cut off after claiming on air that Hamas fighters were hiding among the sick in a hospital.

Hamas hotline claims

Such incidents have only served to undermine Al Jazeera’s reputation for impartiality. NewsGuard gives the outlet a rating of just 20/100, branding it “unreliable” because it “severely violates basic journalistic standards”.

Mohammad Daraghmeh, a Palestinian analyst, told The Telegraph earlier this year that Al Jazeera was “clearly” taking a side and was “not hiding it”.

“Al Jazeera’s editorial line is clear to everyone,” he said. “They definitely bring on guests who have the same view. If you say otherwise, they won’t host you again as an analyst.”

Al-Ghazzi describes the channel’s coverage of Hamas’s military operations in Gaza after Oct 7 as “upbeat”, though he points out that Arab news outlets in general are more likely to show violence on screen than their Western counterparts.

Al Jazeera’s English-language channel appears to be more balanced, with the broadcaster frequently interviewing Israeli politicians and analysts alongside Hamas officials.

Yet the links between Hamas and Al Jazeera are alleged to run much deeper than simply its editorial stance.

The Israel Defence Forces (IDF) claims to have uncovered documents showing that Hamas established a hotline with Al Jazeera and had given instructions to the broadcaster on how to cover its attacks.

Israel last year banned Al Jazeera on national security grounds, accusing the channel of “passing sensitive information” to Hamas.

The IDF has also alleged that six Al Jazeera journalists were Hamas operatives. This included the high-profile reporter Anas al-Sharif, who was killed in a targeted Israeli air strike in August alongside four colleagues.

The Israeli military accused al-Sharif of leading a Hamas terror cell and being involved in rocket attacks on Israel. Following the strike, the IDF said: “A press badge isn’t a shield for terrorism.”

The killings prompted widespread international condemnation from organisations including the UN and Amnesty International. Al Jazeera also rejected the terror claims and branded the attack a “desperate attempt to silence voices”.

Concerns for journalists

Meanwhile, the debate over Al Jazeera’s coverage, which did not respond to detailed questions about alleged bias inks to Hamas, has raged on against a backdrop of death and destruction.

At least 248 journalists have been killed in Gaza, more than in any other conflict in modern times, according to the UN. That includes 10 Al Jazeera staff members.

Campaign groups, including Reporters Without Borders and the Committee to Protect Journalists, have repeatedly raised the alarm over Israel’s killing of journalists, which they say is deliberate and constitutes a war crime under international law.

Critics of Israel describe the country’s labelling of journalists as Hamas militants as a smear campaign designed to justify attacks. In this sense, they argue, Al Jazeera is simply fighting back against Israeli propaganda.

Loreley Hahn-Herrera, of Soas University of London, draws comparisons with Donald Trump’s use of the phrase “fake news”, adding: “Framing it as Palestinian journalists are biased or unprofessional really undermines the work that is taking place.”

Al-Ghazzi agrees. “Unlike other conflicts, these are not activists. In the case of Gaza, these are trained professionals,” he says.

Israel has further exacerbated the issue by refusing to allow foreign journalists into Gaza – a media blackout that has been condemned by dozens of countries, including the UK.

As Israel’s bombardment of Gaza intensified, and public opinion began to shift, Al Jazeera won support from some parts of the international community.

“On the one hand, Israel continues to deny access to any international media and on the other, it kills with impunity local journalists who are the world’s only professional lens into the agony of genocide and famine unfolding in Gaza,” said UN experts Irene Khan and Francesca Albanese last month.

“Even as the journalists starve, lose family members, sleep in tents and get targeted by the Israeli military like the rest of Gaza’s population, they have continued to courageously bear witness to atrocities committed by the Israeli military.”

Meanwhile, as Israel and Hamas move tentatively towards peace – following a process mediated by Qatar – there are claims Al Jazeera may take steps to tone down its coverage.

Amit Segal, a journalist at Israeli news outlet N12, this week reported a “gradual softening” of Al Jazeera’s editorial line on the conflict, which he claimed was part of an agreement struck with the US. The White House declined to comment.

However, the broadcaster’s partisan framing of the Oct 7 anniversary on Tuesday suggests the prospect of a sea-change in editorial output is far-fetched.

While Al Jazeera has followed Arab countries’ position of welcoming the peace deal, it has been quick to temper praise of Trump and cast a cautious tone over whether Israel will hold up its side of the agreement.

Despite hopes of a lasting peace, it seems the propaganda war is likely to rage on.

data.title: Thames Water lenders prepared to take £7bn hit to avoid nationalisation
data.description:
A group of Thames Water creditors is prepared to effectively write off £7.5bn of debt in a bid to avoid nationalisation.

The consortium has proposed a turnaround plan that would cancel around a third of Thames Water’s near-£20bn debt pile and commit the group to investing in the company.

Outstanding fines would be paid – but the investor group, dubbed London & Valley Water, would also seek leniency on future penalties in exchange for investment.

Thames Water revealed over the summer that annual losses had hit £1.6bn as a soaring debt burden and continued sewage failures fuelled its financial crisis.

Bosses said it would take at least a decade to turn around Britain’s largest water company.

The company’s creditors are looking to secure backing for their plans to avoid the utility being put into a temporary special administration regime, which would effectively wipe out their investments.

Lenders said they would not pay a dividend while conducting their turnaround plans or until the company is listed.

They also committed not to sell the business before the end of March 2030.

The consortium said the “more ambitious” plan would increase investment in Thames Water by around £1bn compared to proposals they submitted to Ofwat in May.

The latest proposal was the result of “three months of detailed discussion” with the water regulator and would “support long-term financial resilience and is intended to help the company pay for outstanding fines”, it added.

Mike McTighe, a City veteran whose current roles include chairman of Telegraph Media Group, would become chairman of Thames Water under the creditors’ proposals.

He said: “There is a huge amount of work to be done to turn around Thames Water and deliver the improved service and environmental outcomes that customers and local communities deserve.

“From day one, we will inject billions in new investment, strengthen Thames Water’s balance sheet, transform the company for thousands of hard-working frontline staff and begin the delivery of an operational turnaround that puts 16 million customers and the environment first.”

Lenders of Thames Water’s “Class A” debt would receive at least 10pc of new equity in the utility company in exchange for writing off about 25pc of their stake, worth around £4bn.

Thames Water said it remains in discussions over the bid but any agreement would be subject to High Court approval.

Chris Weston, the chief executive of Thames Water, said: “Today is an important milestone in our ongoing work with creditors and stakeholders to secure a market-led recapitalisation that establishes the financial and regulatory foundations required to support the investment and performance improvements our customers expect.”

data.title: The Big Crash: Are we really heading for another 1929?
data.description:
It’s October 2027, and the poly-crisis that financial markets have long feared is unfolding at speed. Vladimir Putin has made strong gains in his war against Ukraine and has begun massing troops at Europe’s borders in readiness for invasion of one or more of the Baltic states.

In the Far East, China’s Xi Jinping is openly preparing for his long-promised assault on Taiwan, emboldened both by Putin’s success and Donald Trump’s drubbing in the congressional midterms, which have significantly reduced the US’s scope for effective retaliation.

In the Middle East, the fragile peace secured by Trump in 2025 has already fallen apart, plunging the region into renewed conflict.

And on the stock markets, it’s mayhem. Trillions of dollars of losses are being inflicted on the one-time boom sector of artificial intelligence (AI). With the global economy on the brink of recession and unemployment climbing sharply, it is clear that the market for AI’s services has been grossly overestimated.

Despite promises made by AI evangelists, productivity is flatlining. Far from improving output, reports suggest that AI is damaging many businesses where it has been most fervently applied. Some claims on which AI has been sold to investors and lenders even turn out to be overtly fraudulent.

Many of the mega-deals that characterised the latter stages of the great AI gold rush, with customers and suppliers incestuously taking cross holdings in one another, are fast unravelling in an orgy of litigation, broken promises and shattered expectations.

But that is not all. Overlaying the stock market crash is a debt crisis of monstrous proportions. Almost everywhere, bond market investors are on strike and interest rates are rocketing. Global governments are struggling both to refinance mountainous debts and to fund ballooning expenditures.

Private credit, a form of finance that has flourished outside the conventional, more tightly regulated banking system, is in meltdown, with the folly of lending to higher-risk enterprises unable to access credit elsewhere now cruelly exposed for all to see. Again, the losses are monumental.

So bad is the shock that the whole construct of competing fiat currencies, on which the world’s financial architecture is built, seems to be crumbling. Instead, states are hurriedly erecting financial border controls in a futile attempt to stop those investors who haven’t lost everything fleeing for safer shores.

But those investors are realising a dreadful truth: even if you can get your money out, there appears to be no safe place left to put it.

1929 redux

This is of course a wholly imagined, doomsday future. Yet finance is built entirely on trust and confidence and rarely has it looked quite so fragile as it does today.

Even the usually sleepy Bank of England and International Monetary Fund have now taken to warning about the possibility of a sharp, destabilising correction in equity markets, all puffed up as they are by fevered speculation over the potentially transformative powers of AI.

Valuations are stretched to breaking point, with the so-called “Shiller Cyclically Adjusted Price Earnings Ratio” – generally regarded as the most reliable indicator of where the market is relative to past peaks – close to the all-time high it recorded during the dot.com bubble and slightly higher than it was before the great crash of 1929.

The parallels with previous market manias are striking.

Is this 1929 all over again? Surely not. We are all far too clever, worldly-wise and conditioned by the disastrous consequences of this most famous of stock market crashes to let it happen again. It’s unthinkable. Or is it?

When a banking titan as seasoned as Jamie Dimon, chief executive of JP Morgan Chase, openly expresses his concern, it’s time to sit up and take notice. Dimon said in an interview last week that he was far more worried than others about the possibility of a serious market correction.

There were a “lot of things out there” creating an atmosphere of uncertainty, he went on, citing geopolitical instability, unaffordable state spending and remilitarisation.

AI hype

Yet if it is not clear which needle will burst the bubble, there is no doubt about the bubble itself. The AI hype is off the scale, with the big prize dominance not of the generative AI chatbots that we have already become accustomed to using in our everyday lives, but of so-called “general artificial intelligence” or computers with cognitive powers similar to a human being, only infinitely faster and more powerful.

The dangers of AI fever are not just in unhinged stock-market speculation. Such is the magnitude of investment in AI data centres, connectivity and supercomputing that it is said to have contributed nearly a half of all GDP growth in the US so far this year.

The economy is becoming almost wholly dependent on just a handful of tech titan “hyperscalers” chasing a dream of uncertain substance and return. Rarely, if ever, have the fortunes of the world economy depended so precariously on the judgment of such a small cluster of men – the bosses of Meta, Alphabet, Microsoft, Apple, X, Amazon and others chasing the supposed crock of gold at the end of the AI rainbow.

Tech giants say this time is different, that unlike the dot.com bubble – which was characterised by lots of aspiring wing-and-a-prayer enterprises of hardly any substance – the current investment boom is substantially led by a small number of well-established, cash-rich mega companies easily capable of absorbing the new industry’s heavy upfront costs.

What’s more, they claim, AI promises an economic miracle that will render current concerns about spiralling deficits and fiscal black holes almost wholly irrelevant, with stellar growth fast eroding national debt piles.

It’s true that all booms are different, with specific characteristics and transmission mechanisms. But the one constant is that they always end in a bust. People forget that the dot.com boom was not just about transitory fireflies. It also included heavy investment in enabling infrastructure which came close to completely bankrupting many of the established and seemingly solvent telecoms players that provided it.

Such is the scale of today’s investment outlay, and the fear of being left behind, that even the giants of tech are increasingly having to dip into credit markets to stay in the game. Existing cash flows don’t cover it.

So intense is the competition for first-mover advantage that it is almost bound to end up with over-investment, and a world awash with spare data capacity looking for a use.

Hubble bubble

This is what the likes of Amazon’s Jeff Bezos mean when they talk about a “good bubble”. Some may lose their shirts, but the end result is a fabulous infrastructure that serves society at large and eventually “ushers in an era of abundance” for all.

What’s more, Bezos claims, when the bubble pops, it will sort the wheat from the chaff, so that only the strongest survive. No doubt he counts himself among the likely survivors. This is what happened with all previous industrial and technological revolutions, Bezos suggests, and the same Darwinian process will occur with AI.

Up to a point, he is right. In the early stages of the auto industry, there were more than 2,000 car companies in the US. Only a handful of them survived the subsequent consolidation, but it spawned an era of mass motoring that our ancestors could scarcely have dreamt of.

But the Amazon boss’s panglossian way of looking at the world glosses over the carnage this involves and the reality of what happens when the losses from too much investment crystallise. There follows an almighty credit squeeze as finance attempts to recover its footing, and a subsequent investment drought that sends unemployment soaring and wider economic demand plummeting.

There is no such thing as a controlled and gradual climbdown from excess. The transition from one economic age to another is almost invariably a financially, politically and socially destabilising catastrophe.

What makes the current bubble look doubly worrying is that it coincides, as rapid technological change often does, with a growing number of other potential crisis points. If it were just one vulnerability, then the world economy might be able to cope with any consequent mishaps.

But today’s political leaders are besieged by challenges and their scope for evasive action has rarely looked more limited. It is this confluence of negatives that perhaps provides the biggest cause for concern.

The multi-crisis

When the global financial crisis erupted in 2008, it at least found the world in otherwise relatively stable condition, with ample stores of fiscal and monetary firepower to counter some of its worst effects.

It was far and away the worst financial crisis since the 1929 crash, which itself resulted in the Great Depression, plus a degree of political and international instability that ultimately culminated in the Second World War.

Economic policymakers were determined to avoid repeating the mistakes made back then and collectively threw the kitchen sink at the problem, with unprecedented fiscal and monetary countermeasures backed by an equally unprecedented degree of international co-operation.

Remember Gordon Brown’s “I saved the world” gaffe when convening a meeting of global leaders in London to fight the gathering crisis? Well he sort of did by galvanising a global response.

It is hard to imagine such co-operation today. When the next crisis rolls in, it will encounter a world divided by competing ideologies and interests as rarely before, and certainly one incapable of putting up a united front.

Like the 1930s, it is a world defined by mountainous debts, remilitarisation, political instability and rising social discontent. Even if the international resolve was there to act, it is no longer clear that the resources any longer exist for the sort of collective bailout that might be required.

Both the fiscal and monetary canon were exhausted fighting the last crisis. There is no ammunition left. With the national debt swollen to more than 100 per cent of GDP in all bar one of the world’s major advanced economies, there is little if any fiscal scope for dealing with another financial market meltdown by borrowing from elsewhere.

The same may also be true of monetary policy, where central banks would struggle to repeat the rampant money printing of the post financial crisis era. Today, the adverse consequences of quantitative easing in stoking asset price bubbles and rising wealth inequality is becoming far too apparent.

It is not an experiment that policymakers would want to repeat.

Doubting the dollar

As it is, many economies are already close to breaking point. The canary in the coal shaft is the price of gold, which has soared in recent months and is signalling a growing loss of trust not just in dollar assets, but in fiat currencies more widely.

Trump’s peace deal in the Middle East marks a dialling down in geopolitical tensions. For now, it has stopped the surge in the gold price in its tracks. But the interruption may be only temporary.

In remarks last week, Ken Griffin, the billionaire hedge fund founder of Citadel, said “investors are crowding not only into gold, but also into dollar alternative assets like Bitcoin. It’s hard to believe. It’s really worrisome that people view gold as a haven asset the way they once viewed the dollar.”

Hard to believe, perhaps, but easy to understand. Gold is the world’s oldest, universally accepted currency, with an almost mythical status in the history of money. Rarely have its attributes as a safe haven in troubled times been as much in demand as they are today.

First and foremost, it’s about growing loss of trust in the dollar, and the vast US treasuries market that underpins it. The US may still be a relatively dynamic and fast-growing economy, but it is increasingly sustained by ultimately unaffordable stimulus. Not to mention an AI investment boom that many now think cannot last.

With budget deficits of more than 6 per cent of GDP as far as the eye can see, there’s no sign of a reprieve in the onwards and upwards march of America’s national debt, and seemingly no political appetite for the budget trimming needed to restore sanity to the public finances.

Trump’s attacks on the integrity and independence of the Federal Reserve, his tariff blitz and his rejection of many of the other established norms of economic management have further shaken belief in the dollar as the default currency for international commerce.

‘The Roaring 2020s’

At any one time, financial markets are a mass of conflicting signals and the great irony here is that although international investors are increasingly running scared of US government debt, they cannot get enough of America’s leadership in the innovative industries of AI.

Trump recently claimed that his pro-business, tax cutting and deregulatory agenda had generated new investment commitments of some $17tn (almost £13tn), or more than half the size of US GDP. This is undoubtedly an exaggeration, but there is no quarrelling with the notion of an investment boom of mega proportions.

Here too there are parallels with the “Roaring Twenties” and the subsequent crash. The new industries that investors piled into back then were automobiles, consumer goods, radio, construction and chemicals.

When consumer spending cratered in the subsequent bust, all that investment was left high and dry with nowhere to go. We may be seeing something similar today. Trump’s headlong plunge into financial deregulation further increases the risks. After all, the global financial crisis of 15 years ago was directly connected to the deregulation and “light touch” financial supervision of the preceding decade.

While America burns too hot, Europe is sinking into a quagmire. Britain’s fiscal predicament looks precarious and France’s appears almost terminal. Gripped by political paralysis, there appears to be virtually no chance of the belt-tightening needed to put France back on a firm footing.

For now, our friends across the Channel hide behind the assumed protections of the euro and the German chequebook that underwrites it, but for how much longer can the defences hold? Eventually, without course correction, there will be a moment of all-embracing crisis.

Britain seems equally incapable of the required surgery, despite the apparent advantage of a government with an overwhelming majority and nearly four years to run before it faces re-election.

This ought to provide cover for difficult political choices, but Labour has proved incapable of the spending cuts needed to balance the books or of the measures required to lift the economy out of its low productivity rut.

Cursed by the highest interest rates in the G7, the strains are already evident in mushrooming debt-servicing costs that cut deep into the Government’s ability to fund pressing spending priorities such as defence.

Black October

For financial markets, October is a particularly inauspicious month. Historically, it’s been the autumnal backdrop for some of the worst stock market crashes: the Panic of 1907, the Great Crash of 1929, “Black Monday” in 1987, as well as the Global Financial Crisis of 2008, though this technically began with the collapse of Lehman Brothers the month before.

But crashes can and do happen at any time of year. Predicting precisely when is a mug’s game. Hardly anyone manages it consistently and even when they do get it right, it is more likely to be luck rather than judgment.

So for now, the fear of missing out continues to drive the headlong dash into the artificial intelligence boom. Everyone knows it’s out of control, but as Chuck Prince, the former chief executive of Citigroup, said just ahead of the 2008 financial crisis, “as long as the music is playing, you’ve got to get up and dance”.

This is the great, intrinsic curiosity of finance – that despite all the lessons of history, it never knows when to stop. Eternally condemned to a cycle of boom and bust, it invariably pushes the envelope of excess until the whole house of cards collapses in on itself.

Triggered

All the stars are now aligned for the mother of all financial meltdowns, but that doesn’t mean it’s going to happen soon. Market collapses require a trigger, or some kind of event that causes the presiding sentiment of greed to turn into panic and fear. We don’t yet see that. Rarely if ever do bull markets simply die of old age.

A case in point is Alan Greenspan, former chairman of the US Federal Reserve. He originally highlighted the dangers of what he famously described as “irrational exuberance” in stock markets as early as 1996, but the dot.com bubble didn’t finally burst until early 2000, some four years later. When markets finally crashed, it was in no small measure Greenspan’s fault.

There had been a mini-crisis in 1998, when a highly-leveraged hedge fund called Long Term Capital Management ran into trouble, threatening a chain reaction of cascading bankruptcies through the financial system. The Fed responded by organising a bailout, cutting interest rates and flooding the system with liquidity, thereby further fuelling the latter stages of the bubble. It was like pouring fuel on the fire.

Something similar could easily occur today. Interest rates are still relatively high by the standards of the post-financial crisis era, leaving plenty of scope for cuts in the event of limited market turbulence. Donald Trump will not want to see a stock market crash and accompanying recession under his watch. The Fed will be under particular pressure to act if and when the storm clouds threaten to burst.

This could keep markets ticking over for some time, but it will only make the final reckoning worse still once gravity eventually reasserts itself. Until then, as they might say on Strictly, we will just “keep dancing”.

data.title: Higher water bills are a shameless reward for failure
data.description:
On the face of it, the news that water suppliers won’t be allowed to ramp up bills by as much as they were hoping is cause for celebration.

At first glance, it looks like an all-too-rare rebuff for an industry that has been thumbing its nose at customers for far too long – which is, of course, precisely how this latest sorry charade is meant to be interpreted.

Ministers are desperate for us all to think that after decades of wrongdoing, it has taken a Labour government to finally get tough with the cowboys that pollute our rivers and charge us a fortune for the privilege, all while absentee shareholders and negligent executives line their own pockets with money that could’ve been spent on investment.

Yet, that isn’t what is happening here. Not even close.

Yes, bills won’t be increasing to the extent that suppliers were asking for – but the point is that an independent panel of “experts” has still sanctioned further rises beyond what Ofwat had approved and most objective observers thought those rises were already pretty steep.

So the idea that this is somehow a big victory for households and a defeat for water companies is profound nonsense. The opposite is true.

In fact, it is impossible to see further bill hikes as anything other than a reward for failure for an industry that has long treated customers and the environment with complete contempt.

Not that Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, seems even remotely prepared to acknowledge what is really happening here.

“We are laser-focused on helping ease the cost-of-living pressure on households. We’ve frozen fuel duty, raised the minimum wage and pensions and brought down mortgage rates, putting more money in people’s pockets,” she said – though hopefully not with a straight face.

This is gaslighting territory. It takes a special kind of shamelessness to boast about making people better off at the precise moment that millions of families are having a fresh round of bill rises imposed on them.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said on Thursday it is allowing five water companies – Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, Southern Water, South East Water and Wessex Water – to lift bills by up to 5pc over the next five years.

That might seem fairly tame if it wasn’t for the fact that it comes on top of an average increase of 24pc that industry regulator Ofwat had already approved in April.

As Mike Keil, chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), pointed out: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.”

In fact, two in five of the households these companies serve say they will struggle to afford the increases coming down their pipe, according to the CCW, which represents water customers across England and Wales.

What’s more, the CCW has conducted its own analysis showing there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs to cut bills by around £41 a year – “but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return,” Keil said.

However, you would’ve thought otherwise when reading Hardy’s hollow words on Thursday.

“I understand the public anger over bill rises – that’s why I expect every water company to offer proper support to anyone struggling to pay,” she said.

But as Keil said: “Three of these five companies also have no plans to end water poverty by 2030.”

The CMA doesn’t come out of this much better. It makes a big song and dance about how the additional funding will ensure “essential improvements”, such as more resilient supply and a reduction in pollution, are delivered “at reasonable cost”.

Most people will accept some level of inflation in their household expenses but I’m willing to bet that few regard a 27pc surge as reasonable – even if it is over five years.

Some customers are already grappling with rises close to, or more than, 50pc once inflation is added because suppliers have been outrageously front-loading increases to help pay for long-overdue infrastructure improvements.

The reality is that bill-payers are being asked to subsidise an era of playing catch-up because water suppliers failed in the past to keep pace with investment, maintenance and environmental duties.

The money was there but instead of being spent modernising the system, repairing antiquated pipes and ensuring sewage wasn’t pouring into our waterways, it was being lavished on greedy investors and weak-kneed bosses or vanishing straight out the door in the form of heavy debt repayments.

Research from the University of Greenwich has found that 35p in every pound spent on customer bills is swallowed up in debt and dividend costs.

With the system buckling, it is surely no coincidence that these are the highest bill increases since privatisation.

It is also no surprise that the suppliers that went crying to the CMA are also among the most debt-laden in a sector that has long been leveraged up to the gills.

Meanwhile, the competition policeman quietly concedes that alongside a much-trumpeted round of additional investment, bill increases will also help pay down debt and boost returns for investors – many of whom are the same faceless funds that presided over the current wretched state of affairs in the first place.

For all the talk of long overdue reform and a new era of genuine accountability from Labour, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that our broken water industry has effectively been handed a huge taxpayer-funded bailout.

data.title: London buses ‘infested with cockroaches’
data.description:
London buses are infested with cockroaches, drivers have claimed.

Cockroaches have been spotted aboard dozens of Transport for London (TfL) buses and in drivers’ rest areas, prompting safety campaigners to described the situation as “untenable”.

Transport bosses have launched an investigation after London bus safety campaigners raised the issue in a letter, more than a year after The Telegraph first revealed the scale of the problem.

Passengers have also noticed the infestation. This week, Matilda O’Flaherty, a 21-year-old student, told The Telegraph she had seen two cockroaches during a journey on a number 17 bus in August.

Describing how one had emerged from a hole in the seat before crawling up her leg, she said: “It was so gross, and it was about the size of my thumb.”

One driver on route 159 told the London Bus Forums group he had found a cockroach in his drink. In a video recorded inside the vehicle, he said: “I went to take a sip from my drink and felt something in my mouth. I spat it out and saw it was a cockroach. I felt sick and ended up vomiting.”

Elsewhere, the group named buses on routes 159, 345 and 55 as suffering infestations.

Kevin Mustafa, a London bus safety campaigner, said it was more than an “occasional nuisance”, with “many drivers describing infestations”.

He told the BBC on Monday: “The current state is untenable for drivers who already face demanding working conditions. London bus drivers are reporting persistent issues with cockroaches and unsanitary conditions onboard their vehicles.

“This is not a matter of occasional nuisance. Many drivers describe infestations, and the situation is starting to pose real health, safety and well-being risks.”

Cockroaches are typically associated with unhygienic environments, although earlier this year the insects were also reported to be plaguing NHS hospitals earlier this year.

Last year, Mr Mustafa, claimed TfL’s contracted bus operators were “not cleaning the buses properly” and that “at least once a week” he heard a report of insects aboard a vehicle.

A TfL spokesman said instances of cockroaches being reported aboard buses were “rare”, adding: “We are committed to working together with operators to provide a clean environment for staff and customers.

“All buses are also cleaned every night before entering service and reports such as these are rare. We take any report seriously, and are urgently investigating the specific incidents that have been raised.

“As with any reports, we will take appropriate action as part of the investigation, which can include taking buses out of service for inspection, deep cleans and treatment with specialist equipment if necessary.

“We would like to reassure staff that our operators would never take action against people raising concerns about welfare or safety and encourage any driver with concerns to contact their employer, their union, or contact us directly.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Thames Water’s rescue plan risks setting a dangerous precedent
data.description:
The latest version of a creditor-led rescue plan for Thames Water ticks multiple boxes. One can see why the company, ministers and the industry regulator might be minded to give it the green light in the coming weeks.

The pressure to hammer out a deal is intense. The threat of a multibillion-pound bill landing on Rachel Reeves’s desk before her Budget in November is concentrating Whitehall minds.

Thames is burning cash rapidly. A separate announcement from the company revealed that in August it called on the remaining £628m of a £1.5bn emergency loan.

It is further worrying evidence of the extent to which an organisation providing critical services to 13 million people continues to live hand-to-mouth after years of reckless ownership.

With the clock ticking, the “London and Valley” consortium of lenders has come up with a decent upgrade on the somewhat vague proposal published a month ago.

Then, the prospective new owners declared their undying commitment to “a new transparent and collaborative relationship with regulators”, before outlining a plan lacking in details.

The highlights of their latest attempt include a more generous portion of equity and a bigger debt write-off than previously envisaged. A commitment to not pay dividends for five years, along with the ambition of eventually returning Thames to the stock market are also welcome measures.

Yet all the hard work that has gone into answering many of the concerns of regulators, Government and long-suffering customers risks being overshadowed by what amounts to a fresh attempt to wriggle out of fines.

Having sensibly given up on trying to dodge outstanding penalties for pumping sewage into waterways, Thames’s wannabe owners are now trying to inoculate themselves against future punishment.

“The current regulatory framework for the water industry does not allow it to successfully turnaround its business,” London and Valley says.

The consortium won’t take “no” for an answer, it seems.

Steve Reed, the former environment secretary, told parliament in June that Thames must meet its statutory obligations, after being asked about possible “regulatory easements”.

But the very idea that an industry that treats Britain’s rivers as its dumping ground should face softer environmental targets – or “stretching but achievable incentives” in the consortium’s words – won’t wash.

It’s hard to square this with the consortium’s warm words.

Mike McTighe, the proposed future chairman of Thames – he is also chairman of the company behind The Telegraph – talks about “rebuilding public trust”.

The creditors themselves admit that Thames has presided over “a cycle of underinvestment and underperformance that has driven trust in Thames Water to an all-time low.”

Few would argue with that assessment. Yet any agreement that makes it easier for the company to pollute with greater impunity is an improbable basis for the restoration of Thames’s relationship with the public.

Consider that in an industry that has vandalised the environment, this is a chronic offender.

Thames’s record is getting worse, not better. Some 50pc more raw sewage was discharged into Britain’s waterways from Thames’s ageing system last year, compared with the previous 12 months.

In May, Thames Water was fined £123m by Ofwat – the biggest ever fine imposed by the regulator.

From a purely financial perspective, the creditors’ plea for clemency is understandable.

They expect future fines of more than £1bn if current regulations are applied and argue that something has to give if Thames is to receive the investment it needs.

This financial hole was of Thames’s own making, however. Its creditors bankrolled it as it hollowed itself out. To turn around now and demand that the rules are changed to make pumping effluent into rivers a less costly business is an affront to justice and risks setting a horrible precedent.

Perhaps even now, after months and months of negotiations, the hedge funds that swooped in to buy up debt on the cheap when Thames was on the brink, still don’t understand the extent of public and political anger.

Customer ire stretches to the whole gamut of misdeeds – rising bills, poor service, undeserved dividends and excessive executive pay.

But it is the epidemic of pollution that has attracted the most fury. That the creditors have chosen to attack on that front seems “courageous”, as they say in the Civil Service.

Indeed, I am willing to bet that the majority of people want to see stiffer fines, not softer ones, particularly if the current set-up has failed to act as a deterrent. Weaker rules would surely lead to more pollution, not less.

Meanwhile, the knock-on effect of granting Thames an easier ride would be that every other water company in Britain would be entitled to expect the same exemptions on the basis of fairness.

A reminder from the creditors that their offer is the only credible one left after private equity firm KKR abandoned its bid looks designed to focus minds. Ditto talks of how the plan “will avoid special administration”, and ensure that “Thames Water’s turnaround will not fall to the UK taxpayer”.

Decency, however, demands that Ofwat and the Government don’t allow themselves to be pushed around by a bunch of opportunistic hedge funds.

data.title: Peter Hitchens criticises New Statesman for using late brother’s image on gossip column
data.description:
When the Left-wing New Statesman magazine decided to name a new diary column after the late Christopher Hitchens, it was meant to be a tribute to a much-loved colleague.

Instead, the magazine – which chose to illustrate the column with a picture of Hitchens smoking – has been accused by his brother of insensitivity and poor taste because he died of oesophageal cancer.

Peter Hitchens, whose Right-wing views are at odds with the magazine’s editorial stance, was not consulted about the use of his late brother’s name and image and has expressed his “misgivings”.

The magazine’s deputy editor has defended the use of the picture, insisting that other members of the Hitchens family were consulted and gave their approval.

Christopher Hitchens, who died in 2011, was one of the country’s best-known liberal commentators and worked as foreign editor of the New Statesman, as well as writing 18 books on politics, religion and culture.

He talked about the fact that his heavy smoking and drinking were likely to have been factors in his illness, which cut short his life at the age of 62.

When his former magazine decided to launch a new gossip column, it chose to call it The Hitch, with a picture of a cigarette-smoking Hitchens.

Friends of Hitchens took to social media to describe the move as tacky and tasteless, and when Will Lloyd, the magazine’s deputy editor, hit back by saying he had the family’s permission, Peter Hitchens intervened, saying: “Well, you didn’t ask me.”

Peter Hitchens told The Telegraph: “The first I knew about it was when it popped up on Twitter. The thing that I most dislike about it is that he is portrayed as smoking, which is not necessary and a bit of a shame.

“I can say without hesitation that, if asked, I probably would have been pretty unenthusiastic. He was never a gossip columnist – it wasn’t really his kind of journalism.”

He said that, even after the row blew up, “no one from the New Statesman has contacted me by any means known to me. They might have sent someone with a cleft stick, but normal means of communication haven’t worked”.

Peter Hitchens and his elder brother had profound disagreements over politics and the existence of God, which led to a brief estrangement in the early 2000s, but were very much reconciled by the time of Christopher’s death.

Christopher was survived by his wife and three children, who are understood to have been approached by the magazine about the use of his image.

Mr Lloyd said: “We asked permission from the family to honour one of our greatest contributors by keeping his spirit alive – they said yes.”

The Telegraph understands that Peter Hitchens and Mr Lloyd had a cordial conversation on Wednesday afternoon, and that Mr Hitchens did not demand any changes from the New Statesman.

The magazine is now expected to hold an internal discussion about whether to remove the cigarette from the cartoon of Hitchens.
