data.title: ‘A huge housing development is planned near my new home. Why didn’t my solicitor warn me?’
data.description:
Do you have a legal question to put to Gary? Email askalawyer@telegraph.co.uk or use the form at the bottom of the page.

Dear Gary,

In July 2024, we had an offer accepted on a bungalow. The conveyancing process was slow and we finally completed in January 2025.

We love the property and the village we now live in. However, it has recently come to light that a development of some 90 dwellings is proposed for a field on the edge of the village, within a few hundred metres of our new home.

This development does not yet have planning permission. It is one of many proposed sites for our local council to meet their housebuilding target set by central government.

It is included in the Draft Local Plan for 2021-41. The “call for sites” ran for a year from October 2020 and in the draft plan dated February 2024, the site near us is included.

So, my question is this: because I – with my limited knowledge and everyday resources – have found this out, should not my solicitor or the company they used for the searches have also identified this as a potential large-scale development in the area?

While this information may not have put us off the purchase, it certainly could have affected the offer we made – and if the development goes ahead it could affect the resale value of our property.

– Miles, by email

Dear Miles,

I am sorry to hear all this, mostly because of your disappointment that information important to you was not obtained and made known to you, but also on behalf of the legal profession, who generally try to do their best in sometimes high-pressure situations.

Also, I regret to say this precise situation (a possible housing development in the locality where you have just bought coming to light after the purchase) is not an unfamiliar tale.

To my mind, this is because there is a disconnect between what people expect standard conveyancing searches to reveal and the information such searches actually disclose in practice.

The good news is that the development you are concerned about does not yet have planning permission. As the saying goes, “there is many a slip between cup and lip” – so there is every chance the development will not proceed.

If it does and the development is prestigious, it may not be bad news as it could make your village more desirable and push up prices. Your pessimism about your bungalow being devalued may be misplaced.

But I do understand you feel there was information out there that should have been disclosed to you in order for you to make an informed choice before buying.

The answer to whether your solicitor should have picked up on this information is, as it sometimes is in law, that “it depends”.

When you buy a property, your conveyancer should commission a bundle of legal searches on your behalf. These typically include the local authority search, which checks planning matters regarding the property itself as well as whether the roads are adopted, if it’s in a conservation area and so on.

The crucial point is that a standard local authority search does not automatically include information about future developments in the surrounding area, particularly those still at the “proposed” stage and merely included in, for instance, a Draft Local Plan.

To uncover that kind of information, you would need a more detailed planning report. These are offered on a commercial basis to buy. They dig deeper into current planning applications and Local Plan allocations that could affect not just the property which the report is centred on but also its surroundings.

The issue, which you are now all too aware of, is that these enhanced reports are not included as standard. This is because mortgage lenders do not insist on them as part of the standard reporting they require, and this is what conveyancers adhere to.

My personal view is that you should have been offered the choice to get an enhanced planning report – not least because many conveyancers do recommend them these days, especially for homes in rural or semi-rural areas where the character of the setting is a key part of the property’s appeal and therefore its value.

I suggest you return to the initial paperwork (it might be called a “letter of engagement” or “client care letter”) you received from your conveyancer to see what standard searches were mentioned and if you were asked if you wanted to pay for any extra over the standard ones.

Then, get clear on what searches were actually carried out. Ask your conveyancer, in writing, whether any planning report was commissioned and if not, why not. You can ask if the Draft Local Plan was considered at all. It sounds like the land allocation was public at the time of your purchase, so ask why it was not mentioned.

The valid defence from your conveyancer may be that you were offered an extra search but did not take it. Even so, it seems you were not made aware of the significance of the question and, in that sense, it seems the client care process has not succeeded as it should have done.

If the response from your conveyancer is unsatisfactory, the next step is to use their internal complaints process.

Failing that, you could escalate your concerns to the Legal Ombudsman, which has the power to investigate complaints about service standards and could look at how the choice about searches was communicated to you.

That said – even if you prove the service was lacking – in any legal claim there would be a second hurdle to overcome, which is proving financial loss.

You say the development would not necessarily have put you off buying the bungalow but it might have led you to offer less and it may affect resale value. These appear to be reasonable points but quantifying that loss in a legal context is easier said than done.

It is deeply frustrating to find out about a large housing scheme just after moving into a home that promised peace and quiet.

For me, this situation also underscores the good sense of going beyond the basic conveyancing searches when buying property, especially in villages where the pressures of housing demand mean that the open fields of today are under constant threat of being the new-build site of tomorrow.

Ask a Lawyer should not be taken as formal legal advice, but rather as a starting point for readers to undertake their own further research.

data.title: We were promised super-intelligence. We got a mountain of AI ‘slop’
data.description:
“I hope Nintendo doesn’t sue us,” says Sam Altman as he appears on OpenAI’s new Sora app in a virtual field full of Pokémon.

Bizarre and lifelike in equal measure, the AI clip was just one example of the videos on the company’s new TikTok-style platform.

Released in the US last month, Sora is an AI video-creating app that has been catapulted to the top of download charts as users scramble to see how far they can push OpenAI’s creative guardrails.

Inevitably, this has led to a fresh barrage of tasteless AI clips appearing online, otherwise known as “slop”.

In one example, the late Stephen Hawking performs wheelchair stunts and wrestling moves, while another depicts well-known cartoon characters dressed in Nazi-style uniforms.

OpenAI says it has been removing videos featuring AI characters from its app feed, although it appears the technology has already developed a life of its own.

Sora, running on new advanced technology known as Sora 2, allows users to turn text into photorealistic, high-definition video clips, with OpenAI claiming it will empower users and “bring a lot of joy, creativity and connection to the world”.

However, the backlash has already begun.

‘Worst of social media and AI’

Ed Newton-Rex, a composer and the chief executive of Fairly Trained, an AI non-profit, labelled the app as “the worst of social media and AI” and “literally only slop, nothing else”.

It marks the latest escalation in an ongoing battle between Silicon Valley’s AI giants and the creative industry, fuelled by claims that original content is being stolen.

Whether it be images, videos or character creations, media giants have repeatedly accused AI giants of theft to create low-grade and meaningless imitations, whether that be Elon Musk’s Grok or Mark Zuckerberg’s Meta chatbot.

“AI is crowding out human creatives,” says Newton-Rex.

“OpenAI has already trained its models on the life’s work of the UK’s creatives. With Sora, they’ve gone even further, letting anyone make videos featuring copyrighted characters, exploiting people’s IP.”

In response, tech companies have claimed “fair use”, which allows them to use limited amounts of copyrighted material without asking permission.

It was against this tense backdrop that OpenAI unveiled Sora late last month.

Valued in recent weeks at $500bn (£375bn), the AI business insisted it had taken on board concerns around “doomscrolling”, “addiction” and “sloptimised feeds”.

Altman even claimed he would shut Sora down if it failed to improve users’ lives.

Yet in the days running up to Sora’s release, OpenAI also issued an ultimatum to the world’s media giants.

It warned them they would have to “opt out” to avoid having their creations appear in Sora.

That is in contrast to demands from creative companies, which have called for a right to “opt in” to have the final say on what can and can’t be used by generative AI.

As Sora debuted to the world, it was quickly filled with AI videos that featured artwork from the world’s most popular cartoons and TV shows.

Other users created clips of dead public figures, including the late singer Amy Winehouse.

Zelda Williams, the daughter of the late actor Robin Williams, issued a desperate plea on social media: “Please, just stop sending me AI videos of Dad.”

The biggest studios in Hollywood have also weighed in.

A spokesman for Warner Bros Discovery said: “We met with OpenAI to express our concerns about their approach to IP protection on Sora 2 and made clear that we believe the onus for upholding copyright protections lives with them and not content owners.”

The Motion Picture Association, which represents media giants including Warner Bros as well as Universal Studios, Disney and Sony, also demanded OpenAI “take immediate and decisive action” to stop alleged copyright violations.

WME, the talent agency, informed OpenAI that all its clients would “opt out” from Sora.

Meanwhile, the Creative Artists Agency went further by accusing OpenAI’s alleged misuse of technology of posing a risk to “societies globally”.

Copyright concerns

It was against this tide of growing criticism that Altman partially backtracked.

In a recent blog post, he conceded that OpenAI would soon change tack to allow rights-holders to “opt in” if they want to be part of Sora and offer “granular control” over how their characters behave.

He also claimed OpenAI would find a way to share revenue with creatives.

Varun Shetty, of OpenAI, said: “We’re engaging directly with studios and rights-holders, listening to feedback and learning from how people are using Sora 2.

“Many are creating original videos and are excited about interacting with their favourite characters, which we see as an opportunity for rights-holders to connect with fans and share in that creativity.

“We’re removing generated characters from Sora’s public feed and will be rolling out updates that give rights-holders more control over their characters and how fans can create with them.”

However, Gregor Pryor, a partner and digital media expert at City law firm Reed Smith, highlights the legal challenges facing rights-holders when it comes to AI, particularly as courts deal with the issue for the first time.

“Some are rejecting the technology and opting to remove their intellectual property from training models,” he says. “Others pursue licencing their works to gen AI companies as a proactive way forward.”

Meanwhile, in the UK, the Government has repeatedly delayed clarification of how copyright rules apply to AI as it tries to attract investment from Silicon Valley.

‘Cultural disempowerment’

Yet regardless of legal tension, Sora’s momentum is growing. Although its launch has so far been limited to the US, Sora claims to have hit one million app downloads in just five days, even faster than OpenAI’s ChatGPT.

Matt Navarra, a social media consultant, says Sora is “far better” than its rivals by boasting a “visual edge”.

However, he says questions remain over whether the AI-only app will keep users coming back or lead to “slop fatigue”.

Elsewhere, David Krueger, a former member of the UK’s AI Security Institute, argues that AI apps such as Sora pose a deeper threat to human creativity.

Similar to concerns that AI bots will lead to a loss of skills and jobs, he claims they also risk “cultural disempowerment”.

He said that apps such as Sora will, in the long run, leave humans “worse off, isolated and disempowered”.

Supporters of OpenAI stress Sora will do the opposite.

Vinod Khosla, one of OpenAI’s first investors, last week accused “tunnel-vision creatives” of being “snooty critics”.

“Let the viewers of this ‘slop’ judge it,” he said.

Perhaps tellingly, the most-liked comment on OpenAI’s social media feed is a picture of a scientist holding a test tube with the caption: “FINALLY – PURE SLOP.”

data.title: Millions of households to face higher water bills
data.description:
Millions of households are facing higher-than-expected water bills after the competition watchdog allowed five utility companies to increase their charges.

Following a provisional ruling by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, South East Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water will be able to increase bills by a collective £556m over the next five years.

While that is less than the £2.7bn requested by the companies, it will still lead to some households paying annual bills of up to £638 a year.

Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, said she understood the public’s anger over bill rises, but expected companies to offer support to those struggling to pay.

Between them, Anglian and Northumbrian have been allowed to raise bills by a further 1pc, while Southern, South East and Wessex have been handed increases of 3pc, 4pc and 5pc, respectively.

Together, they serve around seven million customers across the UK.

The ruling means that Anglian will charge an average bill of £599, while Northumbrian bill payers will pay £495. South East Water will increase bills to £322, while Southern will charge £638.

Wessex was handed the biggest increase of 5pc, meaning its bills will rise to £622. All had asked for larger increases.

Appeal to the CMA

It comes after the companies appealed to the CMA earlier this year, requesting permission to increase their bills higher than Ofwat, the water regulator, had previously ruled.

In defence of its decision, the CMA said the extra money would help reduce pollution and strengthen supply, although it also said it would help cover the companies’ rising debt costs.

Kirstin Baker, who chaired an independent group of experts appointed by the CMA, said: “We’ve found that water companies’ requests for significant bill increases, on top of those allowed by Ofwat, are largely unjustified.

“We understand the real pressure on household budgets and have worked to keep increases to a minimum, while still ensuring there is funding to deliver essential improvements at a reasonable cost.”

Thames Water, Britain’s biggest water company, had also appealed, but its proceedings have been put on hold while it battles to stave off nationalisation.

Ms Hardy said efforts were being made to restore the public’s trust in water companies.

She said: “We’ve made sure that investment cash goes into infrastructure upgrades, not bonuses, and we’re creating a tough new regulator to clean up our waterways and restore trust in the system.”

It comes amid growing anger over the state of the water sector, which has been responsible for a rise in sewage failures in recent years.

Mike Keil, the chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), said: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.

“CCW’s own analysis suggests there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs, cutting bills by around £41 a year, but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return.

“There is a danger the customers of these companies will end up paying more, without seeing any additional improvements in return.”

Joanna Elson, the chief executive of Independent Age, a charity, said higher bills would be “extremely damaging to people on low incomes living in these areas”.

She said: “The people in later life we support tell us the drastic lengths they are taking to reduce their water costs, including only washing a couple of times a week, not flushing the toilet and cutting down on laundry. This shouldn’t be happening in modern Britain.”

Ms Elson added: “We urge the UK Government to protect customers on low incomes from rising water bills by establishing a national water social tariff. This will end the postcode lottery.

 “It’s not right that your address dictates how much financial support you receive.”

data.title: AstraZeneca stokes fears of London exit with New York listing
data.description:
AstraZeneca has announced plans to list shares on the New York Stock Exchange, stoking fears that Britain’s biggest drugmaker could quit London.

In a shake-up announced on Monday, the FTSE pharmaceuticals giant said it would upgrade its US listing to ensure it is equally as important in New York as it is on the London Stock Exchange.

Bosses said the move would make it easier for the company to raise money from US investors, describing it as “a global listing for global investors in a global company”.

As part of the announcement, the company said it would maintain its London listing and remain headquartered in the UK.

However, this move will stoke fears over AstraZeneca’s future in London. Analysts at AJ Bell said it hinted “at the possibility of a more dramatic shift at some point in the future”.

Mark Kelly, the chief executive of corporate advisory firm MKP Advisors, said he expected AstraZeneca would leave the London market within the next two or three years, adding: “This is a halfway house, it’s potentially a backdoor route to an eventual full US listing.

“I don’t think anyone is necessarily surprised to see AstraZeneca going this way.”

Ketan Patel, a fund manager at AstraZeneca shareholder Whitefriars, said the risk of the company quitting London was a “concern for the UK market”. He said its exit would come as a “huge blow” to UK investors.

Such concerns will no doubt sound alarm bells for the Labour Government, which is currently trying to rally support among businesses at its party conference in Liverpool.

AstraZeneca, which was the UK’s most valuable listed company up until last week, has long been viewed as one of the businesses most likely to ditch its London listing.

Earlier this year, reports suggested that Sir Pascal Soriot, AstraZeneca’s chief executive, was keen to move the company’s main listing to New York – owing to growing frustration with the UK.

Sir Pascal described the drugmaker as a “very American company”.

The business was formed in 1999 through the merger of Sweden’s Astra AB and Britain’s Zeneca PLC. It remains headquartered in Cambridge.

Mr Kelly said: “AstraZeneca is only really here because when they did the original AstraZeneca merger, it made most sense at the time – London was a bastion of financial equity markets, and it was a wonderful place for a massive company to be listed. It’s difficult to make that argument in 2025.”

Mr Patel said: “There is a clear strategy towards a business that is US-focused, and I would not be surprised if a full exit from London is on the cards. The company already reports and pays dividends in US dollars.”

Currently, more than 40pc of AstraZeneca’s revenue comes from the US, making it the company’s largest market by a significant margin. China accounts for 13pc of revenues, while Europe stands at 21pc.

In recent months, AstraZeneca has been stepping up investment in the US – including a $50bn (£37bn) announcement – following demands from Donald Trump, the US president, for companies to invest more there.

At the same time, the pharmaceutical company has pulled a series of investments from Britain amid an ongoing row with ministers over high medicine charges.

Earlier this month, AstraZeneca halted a £200m investment in its Cambridge research facility after the Government walked away from talks to lower drug charges for medicines in the NHS.

This has fuelled claims that Britain is becoming “uninvestable” for drugmakers.

The Cambridge move followed an earlier decision by AstraZeneca to walk away from a £450m investment in a Liverpool vaccine factory in protest over the Government reducing funding for the site.

Sean Conroy, an analyst at Shore Capital, said the latest move from AstraZeneca could arguably be “a lever and a negotiation tactic to be able to demonstrate that it is cosying up to the US a bit more” – putting Britain under pressure at the same time.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: AstraZeneca threatens to stop investing in Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca’s UK chairman has threatened to halt investment in Britain unless the Government backs down over an NHS drug pricing row.

Shaun Grady warned that Britain was in danger of disappearing from a list of attractive global destinations unless it brings its “appalling” drug pricing regime into the 21st century.

Speaking at the Conservative Party conference, Mr Grady said the UK “fell down” on adopting innovation with patients being denied cutting-edge drug treatments as a result.

He blamed a lack of government support for new projects and an agreement that places a cap on how much the NHS spends on branded medicines for putting future investment at risk.

AstraZeneca announced last month that it was putting on hold a planned £200m upgrade of its Cambridge research site. The company had earlier axed a £450m expansion of its vaccine manufacturing plant in Liverpool after Labour cut funding for the project.

Mr Grady said: “We’ve [the UK] failed to keep pace on medicine investment over the years.”

Asked what it would take for the company to go ahead with the Cambridge investment, he said: “Unless the commercial environment changes, the UK is not going to be on the list.”

Mr Grady added that the company would “revisit and reflect” on its Cambridge plans as talks with the Government continue.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers this summer after failing to strike a new pricing deal on medicines supplied to the NHS. It is understood that talks are still ongoing, led by Lord Vallance, the science minister.

While Mr Grady said AstraZeneca remained a significant investor in the UK, he said it was missing out on vital investment because of an antiquated system used by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) to determine which treatments are available on the NHS that has largely remained the same since 1999.

This is determined by a formula which looks at drug effectiveness in terms of how much it would cost to give a person a year of healthy life.

“The thresholds have not been changed for 25 years, which is pretty appalling,” said Mr Grady.

He added that, unlike the UK, countries including Ireland and Singapore were offering companies significant subsidies or tax breaks to invest there. He also compared Britain with the US, where the company announced in July it would invest £50bn by 2030.

“This is a competitive global pitch,” said Mr Grady. “We announced a £50bn investment in the US. We met the governor of Virginia in our offices in London, we announced that £50bn investment 33 days later. We need to think about that speed and what truly is a concierge service.”

Speaking on the same panel, Lord Harrington – who led a review on investment under the last Tory government – said a lack of joined-up thinking was also stymieing growth.

He said: “There’s a room full of civil servants in the Department of Health whose job it is to basically kick the c--- out of pharmaceutical companies to get the price that the government pays for drugs as low as possible. They are not allowed to consider holistically the effect of those decisions on investment in a country, whereas a company clearly does.”

The decision by AstraZeneca, the UK’s largest listed company, means all of its £650m investment package announced in 2024 risks being shelved.

The Department of Health launched a review of so-called NHS rebates earlier this year under pressure from Donald Trump, the US president, who has demanded that pharmaceutical companies lower their drug prices in the US – where they are much higher than the UK and Europe.

A voluntary agreement currently places a cap on how much the NHS spends on branded medicines as part of efforts to keep costs down.

The total bill is allowed to rise by a certain percentage each year. If the NHS spends more than expected on branded medicines, it is able to claw some of that money back in rebates on drugs.

The Government raised the rate unexpectedly to almost 23pc for 2025 for newer medicines at the end of last year.

Pharmaceutical bosses have warned the scheme is preventing the launch of cutting-edge medicines in the UK. They have pressed the UK to cut the rate of rebates into single digits, a level seen elsewhere in Europe.

HS2 will almost double long-distance rail capacity between London and the West Midlands, the Government has said, and that this should in turn free up space on existing lines for more passenger and freight services.

It added that work is ongoing on possible changes to service pattern, infrastructure and rolling stock to eke out additional capacity, especially north of Birmingham, though there is no question of reversing the decision to cancel phase 2 of HS2

A spokesman for the Department for Transport said: “No decisions have been made on HS2’s future timetable, so any suggestion of reduced capacity is pure speculation.”

A Government spokesman said: “Through the Life Sciences Sector Plan, we are making the UK the destination of choice for business, investing up to £600 million in the Health Data Research Service with the Wellcome Trust, committing up to £520m to the Life Sciences Innovative Manufacturing Fund and welcoming billions of UK investment from companies like Moderna and BioNTech.

“We will always put patients and taxpayers first, encouraging investment and innovation whilst ensuring best value for money. 

“Having made an unprecedented £1bn through the Voluntary Scheme on branded medicines to industry, we will continue to engage.”

data.title: Hundreds of UK jobs at risk as wind energy giant plots deep cuts
data.description:
Troubled wind farm operator Ørsted has put hundreds of UK jobs at risk as part of a sweeping overhaul to cut costs.

In a restructuring announced on Thursday, the renewables giant confirmed plans to cut up to 2,000 jobs across its global workforce over the next two years.

This includes job losses in Britain, where it employs 1,300 workers across 13 offshore wind farms. An Ørsted spokesman was unable to say how many UK roles were at risk.

It comes amid a string of setbacks for the company, which is deemed essential to Ed Miliband’s plans to triple offshore wind capacity by 2030.

Earlier this year, Ørsted abandoned plans to build the giant Hornsea 4 wind farm off the east coast of Yorkshire over claims it no longer made economic sense, while the business has also encountered difficulties in the US.

Since re-entering the White House, Donald Trump has waged war on wind by attempting to halt work on some of Ørsted’s biggest projects.

This includes the $4bn (£2.9bn) Revolution windfarm off the coast of New England, where work has since resumed after Ørsted took the US administration to court.

Following Thursday’s announcement, Rasmus Errboe, Ørsted’s chief executive, said: “We need to reduce our costs for developing, constructing, and operating offshore wind farms to strengthen our competitiveness.

“This is a consequence of our decision to focus our business and the fact that we’ll be finalising our large construction portfolio in the coming years – which is why we’ll need fewer employees.

“We want to create a more efficient and flexible organisation and a more competitive Ørsted.”

Offshore wind struggles

The cutbacks could have serious implications for the UK’s own plans to expand offshore wind.

The UK has 3,000 offshore wind turbines with a collective capacity of about 16 gigawatts (GW), enough to power up to 14 million homes on a windy day.

Mr Miliband, the Energy Secretary, wants to triple this capacity by 2030, an expansion that means installing about 3,000 much larger turbines in just five years.

This can only happen if Ørsted and companies are investing.

A Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ) spokesman said: “We have a strong pipeline of projects to deliver clean power by 2030 and our mission-led approach ensures we can steer our way through global pressures and individual commercial decisions to reach our targets.”

To help stave off rising costs, Ørsted was recently forced to launch a $9bn rights issue to raise new funding.

Meanwhile, Ørsted currently has 9GW of offshore wind capacity installed or in planning in the UK, far greater than 1GW in Denmark. The next largest are Poland and Germany, in each of which Ørsted has roughly 2.5-3GW of capacity.

A company spokesman said: “As part of its updated strategy, Ørsted has also sharpened its geographical and technological focus and will primarily be directing it towards offshore wind in Europe and in select markets in the Asia-Pacific region.”

The company has invested £15bn in its UK operations and was planning to invest a similar amount more over the next decade.

As well as investing in offshore turbines, this cash will also be used to build floating wind farms, onshore wind projects and battery farms.

Asked how many UK jobs would be lost, the spokesman added: “We’re not sharing country-specific information at this time, but the organisational changes will be global and happen across all its functions.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Nestlé to cut 16,000 jobs as new boss swings axe
data.description:
Struggling food giant Nestlé is to slash 16,000 jobs over the next two years as its new boss ramps up cost-cutting efforts.

The Swiss conglomerate, which owns brands including KitKat and San Pellegrino, said the cuts will include 12,000 white-collar roles, driving savings of 1bn Swiss francs (£940m).

A further 4,000 jobs will be cut as part of an ongoing overhaul in the group’s manufacturing facilities and supply chain.

It is not yet clear how many jobs in the UK will be affected. Nestlé has offices in Gatwick, Welwyn and York, as well as a dozen factories across the country.

Shares in the Swiss-listed company jumped by more than 8pc as investors welcomed the overhaul.

The cuts, which represent around 6pc of Nestlé’s global workforce, mark the first shake-up by Philipp Navratil, who took over as chief executive last month after his predecessor Laurent Freixe was sacked for having an affair with a junior colleague.

The 63-year-old, who has not publicly commented on his departure, had been at the company for almost four decades but had been chief executive for just a year when the scandal emerged. His exit was followed just two weeks later by the surprise resignation of chairman Paul Bulcke.

Mr Navratil warned “the world is changing, and Nestlé needs to change faster” as he announced the company would ramp up its cost savings target to 3bn Swiss francs by the end of 2027, up from 2.5bn previously.

He added: “This will include making hard but necessary decisions to reduce headcount over the next two years.”

Nestlé said it was seeking to improve efficiency across the business, including through the increased use of automation.

Analysts at Barclays said the overhaul felt “like a big moment for Nestlé”. They added: “The new savings are the highlight and hard cost savings are welcome but we would still like to see it funding higher marketing spend.”

The job cuts come as Nestlé battles a slowdown in sales as the cost of living crisis prompts shoppers to either trim their household budgets or trade down to cheaper supermarket own-label ranges.

Mr Navratil, who has worked at the Swiss company since 2001 and most recently ran its Nespresso brand, has set out plans to restore growth through cost-cutting and investment in its most profitable areas. He said driving growth in sales volumes was “our number one priority”.

Analysts at Bernstein said the shake-up was “adding fuel to the turnaround fire”. On Thursday, Nestlé reported a 1.9pc decline in sales to 65.9bn Swiss francs for the first nine months of the year.

Nestlé is also under pressure to sell off some of its portfolio of more than 2,000 brands to help pay down its ballooning debt pile. The company has faced criticism for its recent foray away from food into areas such as health supplements and dog food.

Nestlé launched a strategic review of its vitamins business this summer, while it has already completed a spin-off of its water brands into a standalone business.

data.title: Nestlé to cut 16,000 jobs as new boss swings axe
data.description:
Struggling food giant Nestlé is to slash 16,000 jobs over the next two years as its new boss ramps up cost-cutting efforts.

The Swiss conglomerate, which owns brands including KitKat and San Pellegrino, said the cuts will include 12,000 white-collar roles, driving savings of 1bn Swiss francs (£940m).

A further 4,000 jobs will be cut as part of an ongoing overhaul in the group’s manufacturing facilities and supply chain.

It is not yet clear how many jobs in the UK will be affected. Nestlé has offices in Gatwick, Welwyn and York, as well as a dozen factories across the country.

Shares in the Swiss-listed company jumped by more than 8pc as investors welcomed the overhaul.

The cuts, which represent around 6pc of Nestlé’s global workforce, mark the first shake-up by Philipp Navratil, who took over as chief executive last month after his predecessor Laurent Freixe was sacked for having an affair with a junior colleague.

The 63-year-old, who has not publicly commented on his departure, had been at the company for almost four decades but had been chief executive for just a year when the scandal emerged. His exit was followed just two weeks later by the surprise resignation of chairman Paul Bulcke.

data.title: Starmer pledges to spend billions more on drugs in NHS overhaul
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer is preparing to boost NHS spending on drugs to prevent an exodus of investment from the UK.

The Government is set to overhaul a decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money. The move will allow the NHS to pay billions of pounds more for medicines.

Several drug companies have scrapped investments in the UK amid a row over how much the Government was spending on new drugs compared to other countries.

The pricing shake-up will appease drugmakers – but it also threatens to put Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, on a collision course with Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, over how to fund the multibillion-pound bill.

Lord Vallance, the science minister – who is negotiating a new deal with the industry on behalf of the Government – admitted last month that price increases were necessary to stop a wave of investment leaving the UK.

The Times reported that the Treasury is insisting that spending increases must be funded through existing departmental budgets, while NHS bosses are demanding extra financial support to pay for any deal.

The looming showdown comes as the Chancellor prepares to unveil another tax-raising Budget, with economists predicting extra levies of around £30bn to balance the books.

Ms Reeves boosted health spending at the expense of other government departments in her June spending review, with the department accounting for 90pc of the extra money for public services over the next three years.

It means the NHS and other health spending is now on course to account for half of all spending on public services by the end of the decade.

Meanwhile, a string of companies including Merck, the US cancer drug manufacturer, and Eli Lily, maker of obesity jab Mounjaro, have halted investment in the UK over the past year in protest against the NHS’s drug pricing regime.

This week, AstraZeneca’s UK chairman signalled that the company was ready to scrap investment in Britain unless the Government backed down on pricing.

Shaun Grady warned that the UK was in danger of becoming unattractive for companies unless it brings its “appalling” drugs regime into the 21st century.

Pharmaceutical companies are also under pressure from Donald Trump, the US president, who has escalated tensions by threatening 100pc tariffs on the sector if it does not invest in America.

The antiquated system used by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) to determine which treatments are available on the NHS has not been changed since 1999.

The system uses a formula which seeks to determine value for money based on a drug’s effectiveness.

Currently, Nice will approve medicines that cost less than £20,000-£30,000 for every extra “quality-adjusted life year”.

A new range of between £25,000-£35,000 per year is said to be one option under consideration.

However, the Association of the British Pharmaceutical Industry has warned that the threshold would be closer to £56,000 if it had climbed in line with the cost of living. It has called for a range closer to £50,000.

A government spokesman said: “The pharmaceutical sector and the innovative medicines it produces are critical to our NHS, our economy and the plan for change.

“Through our life sciences sector plan, we’ve committed to working with industry to accelerate growth in spending on innovative medicines compared to the previous decade.

“We’ve secured a landmark economic partnership with the US that includes working together on pharmaceutical exports from the UK whilst improving conditions for pharmaceutical companies here.

“We’re now in advanced discussions with the US administration to secure the best outcome for the UK, reflecting our strong relationship and the opportunities from close partnership with our pharmaceutical industry.”

data.title: It’s a buyer’s market, but these six tips will help your property sell
data.description:
Jo Eccles is the founder and managing director of Eccord, an award-winning property search company, which represents individuals and families looking to buy or rent in prime central London.

I’ve viewed thousands of properties over the past two decades as a buying agent and I’ve truly seen it all – from tiptoeing round bedrooms where teenagers are still asleep with duvets pulled up over their heads, to beautifully presented turnkey homes where clients have been ready to offer on the spot.

While it’s true that sellers are facing significant headwinds at the moment – thanks to cautious and price-sensitive buyers, and uncertainty over possible tax changes in next month’s Budget – getting the viewing right can make a huge difference, both in the speed of sale but also price achieved.

My strongest advice to anyone selling is to price competitively from the outset. Don’t be seduced by unrealistic expectations of your own or ambitious estate agent valuations – this will only force you to chase the market down with incremental reductions.

Not only are you likely to achieve less than if you had priced sensibly at the outset but data from Zoopla also shows that properties that have had to be reduced in price take an average of 2.4 times longer to sell.

Assuming your property is priced correctly, the next step is preparing for viewings.

How you do this can make the difference between your property languishing on the market for months (or even years) – and selling quickly. Here’s my advice on how to impress.

Freshen up, but don’t refurbish

If your property is looking tired, a simple repaint in a neutral tone can go a long way to helping it look bright and fresh. And if it’s unfurnished – for example, if you’re selling a buy-to-let investment and your tenant has already moved out – I would strongly advise staging the property. It’s an expense, but the photos and feel of the place will be completely transformed – plus it will detract from any visible signs of wear and tear.

If your property realistically needs a refurbishment, embrace it and consider reducing the price accordingly.

Don’t be tempted to do a quick refurbishment and try to sell it as turnkey. I viewed one family house recently where the seller claimed to have refurbished throughout. But while he had repainted and sanded down some of the floors, the bathrooms and kitchens had been left untouched and looked visibly old. The old ironmongery was brass while the new was chrome, and he had installed air conditioning cheaply with visible pipes and trunking.

The result was that no buyer was prepared to reimburse him for the money he had spent, as they deemed the property still needing to be refurbished. Not only that, the cheap work made them suspicious about the quality of the house overall. He would have been better off having left it as it was and saving himself the wasted expenditure.

Put the heating on

As the weather gets colder, it amazes me how many sellers don’t think to turn the heating on to ensure their house is warm – especially if it’s empty, which makes it feel even more unloved. It’s such a simple but often overlooked thing, and it makes a significant difference to how people feel about the property when they walk in.

I often do back-to-back viewings with a client and when we walk into a cold property, buyers want to leave as quickly as possible, rather than relax and take their time. It’s hard for them to imagine feeling warm and cosy there, and they may also question whether the heating and other services are working.

If you’re not living in the property, consider setting the heating on a timer before scheduled viewings or leave the thermostat on low and accept it as a marketing cost.

Expect cupboards – and fridges – to be opened

I would never view a property without opening the cupboards, drawers and storage areas – and if they’re overflowing with clutter, it can make buyers question whether the property has enough storage for day-to-day living.

Buyers want to be reassured there’s space for everyday items such as coats, shoes, scooters or school bags to be stored tidily, and somewhere else for large items that aren’t needed as often, such as Christmas decorations or suitcases.

We open fridges too, so make sure they’re clean and there’s nothing obviously past the use-by date.

If a declutter is needed then do it properly – don’t be tempted to hide belongings under the bed or pile them on top of wardrobes, as it only highlights the shortage of storage space. One seller I met admitted that she used to load up her car and drive around the block every time a viewing took place, in the hope that family buyers wouldn’t realise the house was lacking in storage!

Step back and let the agent do their job

It’s understandable to want to be present when strangers walk around your home but, from a buyer’s perspective, having the owners hovering – especially during a first viewing – is really off-putting.

No matter how friendly you are, buyers will feel like guests who must tiptoe around. They won’t feel they can relax and take their time, and out of courtesy, most won’t feel at ease opening wardrobes or discussing potential changes.

If buyers are serious, they will request a second viewing and your agent will advise if they think it would be helpful for the buyers to meet you to ask more detailed questions.

Remove pets from the equation

Your pets may be beloved members of your family but when you’re trying to sell your house they can be a distraction and even a deterrent.

I once accompanied clients to a property where a very aggressive dog barked relentlessly from behind the kitchen door, which was not only intimidating and stressful, but also meant we couldn’t enter one of the most important rooms in the house. Needless to say, we didn’t stay very long.

If you do have pets, especially dogs, remove them from the property during viewings to ensure everyone feels relaxed and no rooms are off limits.

Don’t try too hard

Contrary to popular belief, buyers are rarely swayed by the smell of fresh bread baking or coffee brewing and you don’t need to light every candle in the house or spend a fortune on fresh flowers.

Switching on lamps, dressing beds neatly with fresh linen and ensuring the property feels warm and welcoming will go a long way – but be aware over-staging can come across as unnatural and desperate.

Selling in today’s market isn’t about gimmicks and grand gestures; it’s about creating a feeling of order and enabling buyers to easily imagine themselves living there.

Get the basics right, be realistic on price – and you may find there are more buyers waiting in the wings than you expect.

data.title: The cracks are starting to appear in the shadow banking bubble
data.description:
It is lightly regulated. The size of the exposure is often unknown. And many of the loans are of remarkably low quality.

It remains to be seen whether we are in the middle of an epic financial bubble or not. We will only know for sure once we have the benefit of hindsight.

And yet one point is becoming increasingly clear: if we are, it is not a soaring gold price or the crazy valuations of anything connected to artificial intelligence that will bring it crashing down to Earth. It is the murky world of shadow banking – and the signs of stress are already becoming alarmingly obvious to anyone who cares to look.

With stock markets hitting record highs, and with the price of gold and its digital equivalent Bitcoin soaring, there are plenty of signs that the markets are going through a phase of what the former Federal Reserve chairman Alan Greenspan used to refer to as “irrational exuberance”.

And yet, the first cracks are also starting to appear. Investors have started selling off shares in some of the world’s largest money managers on fears that their vast holdings of “private credit” are an accident waiting to happen.

The value of giants such as Blackstone, KKR and Apollo have fallen by an average of 10pc or more over the last month even as the rest of the market hits fresh highs.

The explanation? Investors are quite rightly worried about the vast portfolios of loans they have built up. Indeed, the Financial Conduct Authority last week warned of the need to be “vigilant” over the issue, while the collapse of the American auto parts supplier First Brands, with liabilities that may run as high as $50bn (£37bn), has exposed the losses that may be lurking within the system.

Over the last decade, the private equity and asset management firms have pushed aggressively into shadow banking, offering big loans to companies. According to an analysis by Morgan Stanley, private credit has grown into a $3tn industry, with $1tn of that added in the last five years alone.

Some of the jargon is complex, but in simple terms, the asset managers have turned themselves into corporate bankers. They are lending out lots of money to businesses.

Sure, it is often very profitable, which explains why the industry has grown so dramatically over the last few years. Interest rates are often 10pc or more, with another couple of percentage points in “arrangement fees”.

It is not hard to make money on that if your cost of capital is only 3pc or 4pc. The trouble is, it increasingly ticks all the boxes that identify a classic financial bubble.

To start with, the size of the exposure is unknown. True, we have estimates of how much money has been lent out through the shadow banking system. But there is a clue in the word shadow. It does not have the transparency of mainstream commercial banking, and that means no one can know for sure how much is outstanding.

Next, it is very lightly regulated. It has grown out of the private equity industry, which never had much oversight in the first place, and one of the reasons it has expanded so quickly is because traditional banks have had to comply with far more stringent rules on how much they can lend and to whom. Private credit does not have to worry about that.

Third, many of the loans are of poor quality. We are witnessing that already with the demise of First Brands and we may well see many more times over the next year or two. A lot of the lending has been to private equity backed buyouts that are already loaded up with way too much debt, to commercial real estate developers, which are always high risk, or to start-ups that might have dazzling prospects but don’t yet make any profits.

Finally, and perhaps most importantly of all, there is lots of clever jargon to hide a simple fact. At its core, shadow banking is basically all about lending lots of cash to businesses that have weak balance sheets.

Of course, quite often you will have luck on your side, and the loan will be paid back in full, especially if the economy is expanding at a healthy enough rate. But quite a few of them will also turn out to be duds.

In reality, every asset bubble is characterised by excessive lending hidden somewhere within the financial system. In the 2010s it was sub-prime lending on mortgages that were repackaged to make them appear a lot safer than they really were.

In the 1980s, it was junk bonds, an industry that looks remarkably similar to private credit, which specialised in lending more and more cash to companies that were previously shut out of the market (and usually for good reasons).

In the 1970s, it was the Savings & Loans crisis in the US, and the secondary banking market in London.

The details always vary but the core of the story is always the same. Standards are relaxed, the regulators are kept in the dark, and while there is some easy money to be made at the start, it all turns ugly very quickly as soon as the economy starts to run into trouble.

Sure, we don’t yet know if shadow banking will cause a crash. The likes of Blackstone and KKR may be able to manage their positions. They certainly have enough smart people, and they may be clever enough to rein back on the lending before it runs out of control.

And yet every bubble has hidden losses. It is becoming clear that shadow banking is where they are located this time around – and no one who is familiar with the long history of asset bubbles should be in the least surprised if the market starts to collapse, leaving a trail of wreckage in its wake.

data.title: Waitrose posters tell staff not to steal
data.description:
Waitrose has told staff not to steal, warning that they could lose their jobs or face time in prison if they do.

Posters placed in the supermarket’s staff rooms advise staff to speak to managers for support if they are struggling financially.

The posters, seen by The Telegraph, say: “The first time you steal could be the last time you steal.

“You will lose your job and may receive a fine or be imprisoned.”

Staff claimed they had only noticed the posters appearing in recent months, although the retailer insisted that they have been in use for the past two years.

A spokesman for John Lewis said: “We employ brilliant partners who go the extra mile for our customers and anyone working in retail will be very used to seeing posters like this.”

A 2023 study suggested that employees accounted for as many as 40pc of retail theft incidents, with crimes occurring in stores and distribution centres.

At the time, Retail Economics, which conducted the research, said that two thirds of company chiefs who took part in the survey believed cost of living pressures had driven an increase in employee theft.

In the past, the John Lewis Partnership, which owns Waitrose, has sought to offset higher employee costs. It gave full-time staff a £500 cost of living payment in 2022.

In the run-up to Christmas in 2021 and 2022, it also offered staff free food. It scrapped the free meals in 2023, but said non-seasonal workers at the company would still be able to access John Lewis schemes, such as hardship grants and interest-free loans.

Impact of inflation

Official data published last month showed inflation hit a fresh 19-month high of 3.8pc in August, with Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, admitting on Thursday that more needed to be done to tackle inflation.

This week, the International Monetary Fund (IMF) said prices in Britain were rising at the fastest pace in the G7, and even faster than in some developing economies, including Peru and Senegal.

Research commissioned by the Mayor of London last year found that a 10pc increase in the cost of living correlated with an 8pc rise in crime.

Retailers have been battling a shoplifting epidemic, with nearly three thefts per minute reported to police.

Shoplifting is estimated to have cost retailers £2bn last year, adding the equivalent of £133 to every household’s shopping bill.

John Lewis has upgraded its CCTV and installed locking trolleys to reduce crime levels.

data.title: Telegraph takeover may face national security investigation over China links
data.description:
The proposed takeover of The Telegraph by a consortium with links to China could face investigation as a potential national security threat, the Government has said.

Ministers were questioned in the House of Lords over the protracted attempt by RedBird Capital to engineer a £500m acquisition partly relying on the wealth of the United Arab Emirates royal family.

Asked whether the takeover would be investigated under the National Security and Investment Act, which grants ministers sweeping powers to intervene, culture minister Baroness Twycross said intervention “could be considered” if it was deemed to raise national security concerns. 

A RedBird spokesman said: “We are happy to work with any regulator or government body that wants to look into the proposed transaction.”

It threatens another hurdle for RedBird, which is attempting to push through a deal nearly two-and-a-half years after The Telegraph was first cast into limbo by the financial troubles of the Barclay family, its previous owners.

RedBird made a formal application to the Government last week to be allowed to begin the takeover. Its consortium is also expected to include stakes for the Daily Mail owner Lord Rothermere and Sir Leonard Blavatnik, the billionaire industrialist.

Under separate laws that apply only to newspaper and news website acquisitions, the Government already faces demands to investigate the impact on the public interests in plurality, free expression and accurate news. The risk of foreign state influence posed by RedBird’s plans for the UAE to become a minority shareholder are also open to regulatory scrutiny.

Baroness Twycross declined to confirm that fears of Chinese influence sparked by the longstanding links to Beijing of senior figures at RedBird would guarantee an investigation. But she added: “I’d like to reassure the noble Lord that any sale of The Telegraph will be subject to the regime.”

The Conservative Baroness Stowell said: “I do hope very much that the Secretary of State is able to say soon that she’s minded to involve the Competition and Markets Authority [CMA] and Ofcom in the scrutiny of this bid.

“Because I think it will be important, should she decide to give it the go-ahead, for our future confidence in The Telegraph’s editorial independence.”

‘They’re buying influence’

Baroness Stowell also raised concern that reliance on debt or an earn-out to the UAE could confer influence on top of its planned shareholding.

The £500m price, set in the deal between the Barclay family and the UAE, is significantly higher than most media analysts can justify using normal valuation approaches. RedBird has rejected claims it is overpaying.

Baroness Twycross replied: “We’re absolutely clear that the overall intention of the policy is that a foreign state should not have any control or influence, and this is what the Secretary of State considers carefully when she looks at the details of each case.”

RedBird, which is led by the former Goldman Sachs banker Gerry Cardinale, was involved in a prior attempt to take control of The Telegraph that was majority funded by the UAE royal family. It was blocked by the Conservatives in spring 2024 following a cross-party outcry over press freedom.

RedBird’s new consortium includes an expected 15pc stake for the UAE. The investors in what would be RedBird’s own controlling stake have not been disclosed.

However, the decades-long Chinese associations held by John Thornton, the firm’s chairman, have raised concerns that the fund may contain money under the influence of Beijing, such as billionaire family offices. Mr Thornton, another former Goldman Sachs banker, also sits on the advisory council of the China Investment Corporation, the country’s largest sovereign wealth fund.

No evidence is available to substantiate fears of Chinese money in The Telegraph takeover, but against the backdrop of the Westminster spying scandal, the Government and regulators are under increased pressure to investigate.

The Liberal Democrat peer Lord Fox said: “My Lords, as we know, a fund with Abu Dhabi money and probably Chinese money, is acquiring what in global terms is a small player, relatively small, but The Telegraph is significant in the UK.

“The best explanation for their motives that I can come up with is that they’re buying influence.”

‘No Chinese state funds involved’

A RedBird spokesman said: “A fundamental tenet of RedBird Capital Partners’ investment thesis for The Telegraph is editorial independence. Maintaining the highest standards of journalistic integrity is at the heart of RedBird’s plans to grow this iconic brand in the UK and internationally.

“There is no Chinese influence in RedBird Capital’s proposed acquisition of The Telegraph.

“There are no Chinese state funds being invested in this deal. There are no Chinese LPs in RedBird funds.”

In private equity, LPs stands for limited partners, the financial contributors to the funds that firms invest. They can be funds themselves, based anywhere.

Scrutiny of the new bid is being overseen by Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary. She claimed this week that “the last government was far too relaxed about the challenges and the threats posed by China”.

Luke de Pulford, executive director of the Inter-Parliamentary Alliance on China, which campaigns on the security threat from Beijing, said last week: “Unless Lisa Nandy intervenes, The Telegraph will be taken over by a company led by John Thornton, arguably the most pro-Beijing businessman in the whole of America.

“He even said to CCP leaders that they needed to ‘get in’ to English-speaking media. Mad.”

A group of free speech groups including Index on Censorship, Reporters Without Borders and Article 19 wrote to Ms Nandy in August, arguing that RedBird’s links with China “threaten media pluralism, transparency and information integrity in the UK”.

The Telegraph is currently effectively operating without an owner, overseen by independent directors. RedBird manages the large debt secured against it, which was intended to be converted into ownership in the original UAE-backed deal.

Special legal orders are in place to protect the newspaper’s independence of RedBird and the UAE while wrangling over its ownership continues.

Lord Fox asked: “Will the minister commit that her department will investigate any prima facie breaches of the orders protecting The Telegraph from influence by RedBird, breaches that may already have occurred? And will she commit to publishing the investigation findings?”

Baroness Twycross said she could not comment on a live takeover situation given Ms Nandy’s quasi-judicial role, which leaves her open to judicial review on any perceived breach of process. She said she would write to Lord Fox about publishing official findings on any alleged violations of the orders protecting The Telegraph.

Mr Cardinale, who is on the board of the Hollywood studio Paramount after RedBird provided $2bn for a takeover led by the billionaire Ellison family, recently said: “Don’t buy into a news organisation, whether it’s broadcast or print, if you want to influence it. That’s just bad business. Right?”

Last week he said: “RedBird is committed to partnering with the talented team at The Telegraph to help navigate this evolving landscape and ensure The Telegraph’s world class journalism and brand are positioned for success going forward.”

data.title: Ferrari cuts UK car supply over Labour’s non-dom tax raid
data.description:
Ferrari has cut the number of cars it sells in Britain over fears that Labour’s non-dom tax raid has driven wealthy buyers away.

The luxury carmaker started scaling back deliveries to the UK six months ago, around the same time that Rachel Reeves abolished the non-dom tax regime. The regime had allowed residents whose main home is overseas to avoid paying some taxes.

Benedetto Vigna, Ferrari’s chief executive, blamed the decision primarily on the policy change, saying: “Some people are getting out of that country for tax reasons.”

Mr Vigna told the Financial Times that UK sales had stabilised since Ferrari reduced the number of cars it sells here in response to the policy change, claiming the company was focused on the “management of scarcity” to help cars retain their value.

Ferrari, which makes a £250,000 car called the Ferrari 296 GTB, saw its UK sales dip last year to around 940, down from 983 the previous year.

As well as the impact of abolishing non-dom tax rules, the Ferrari chief executive also said factors such as Britain’s right-hand drive cars – which are harder to resell in other markets – had prompted the decision to pare back deliveries.

The residual value of some of Ferrari’s vehicles has dipped sharply in recent months, according to Autotrader data. The residual price of its Purosangue dropped by 12.2pc between January and October, while the SF90 Stradale fell by 6.6pc.

It comes after the Chancellor’s tax rises have driven a number of wealthy individuals away from the UK.

There are fears this trend could worsen after next month’s Budget, after Ms Reeves stressed that individuals with the “broadest shoulders” should pay more to balance the books.

She also said that last year’s £40bn tax raid, which included scrapping non-dom status, was the result of “the right policies in the circumstances that we faced”.

Rishi Sunak’s Conservative government originally introduced a tightening of non-dom rules in early 2024, but Labour abolished the regime altogether after their election victory last year.

Ferrari’s share price has fallen by 20pc since last week, when the luxury marque warned of weak sales for the rest of the decade.

The Italian company has also radically scaled back its electric vehicle plans amid slowing demand from drivers.

The carmaker said only about 20pc of its line-up would be electric from 2030, down from 40pc it had initially forecast.

It will also double the production of traditional internal combustion engine vehicles.

data.title: GSK boss says US is ‘biggest priority’ as Trump pressures drugmakers
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drugmaker has said the US is its “biggest priority” amid pressure from Donald Trump to slash prices for US citizens.

Dame Emma Walmsley, who will step down from GSK at the end of the year, praised the US and said the group is working with the Trump administration after it launched a campaign to address high drug prices.

Shares in pharmaceutical companies surged on Tuesday after rival pharma group Pfizer struck a deal with the White House to lower prices for some of its medicines. Mr Trump has also demanded greater investment in the US from drug companies after introducing a 100pc tariff on branded drugs made abroad.

The Pfizer agreement, which averts tariffs for three years, triggered a relief rally that sent shares in AstraZeneca up 11.2pc, as it reclaimed its place as Britain’s most valuable company. GSK shares jumped by 6.2pc.

Dame Emma admitted GSK was “betting big on the US” after she pledged during Mr Trump’s UK state visit last month to invest $30bn (£22bn) in America over the next five years.

She said: “Absolutely GSK is engaging constructively. The markets also view it this way, as it’s about constructive dialogue between the industry and the Government.

“The US is by far the biggest priority market for our industry because the US is the best country in the world to discover and develop innovation.”

As the country’s largest drugmaker, GSK has become a national champion for the UK pharmaceutical sector, despite generating only a small proportion of its revenues in the UK.

Britain represents just 2pc of the £65bn drugmaker sales, compared to 52pc in the US.

Despite this, Dame Emma said the group would not ditch its listing on the London stock market for New York. GSK’s shares have fallen overall in the eight years since she took over in 2017.

“I’m not getting distracted by the listing questions that everyone keeps asking about,” Dame Emma told Bloomberg’s Women, Money & Power conference.

“Our listing location doesn’t distract from where we allocate our capital in terms of investments. There are no plans for that.”

She also said there had been “zero engagement” on a potential merger with any other UK pharmaceutical company.

AstraZeneca fears

Her comments come days after AstraZeneca stoked fears that Britain’s biggest drugmaker could quit London after it announced plans to list shares on the New York Stock Exchange.

In a shake-up announced on Monday, the FTSE pharmaceuticals giant said it would upgrade its US listing to ensure it is equally as important in New York as it is on the London Stock Exchange.

Bosses said the move would make it easier for the company to raise money from US investors, describing it as “a global listing for global investors in a global company”.

Analysts at AJ Bell said the decision hinted “at the possibility of a more dramatic shift at some point in the future”.

data.title: Green levies to blame for rising energy bills, say suppliers
data.description:
Household energy bills are on course to rise by hundreds of pounds this decade because of ballooning green levies, the country’s biggest gas and electricity suppliers have warned.

Senior executives from Octopus Energy, British Gas owner Centrica, E.On, EDF and Ovo urged ministers to urgently address levies that pay for net zero-related costs, which they said are one of the main factors driving bills higher.

Speaking to MPs on a Parliamentary committee on Wednesday, Rachel Fletcher, director for regulation at Octopus Energy, said current trends suggest green levies, along with the costs of running the system and building the power grid, could add around £300 to a typical household’s electricity bill by 2030.

This meant that even if the wholesale cost of electricity was halved, bills would still rise by around £150 overall.

The warning will be seen as a blow to Labour’s Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, who has promised to cut bills by £300 by the end of the decade and has put green energy at the heart of his plan to achieve the target.

Just hours before energy executives spoke to MPs, Mr Miliband claimed the high gas prices were to blame for the UK’s sky-high electricity prices.

But energy bosses warned that net zero levies were becoming so expensive that they threatened to push household bills higher, even if gas and power prices fell dramatically.

Ms Fletcher, of Octopus, said: “If we continue on the path that we’re on right now, in all likelihood, electricity prices for a typical customer are going to be 20pc higher in four or five years’ time than they are now.

“And that’s even if wholesale prices halve. So we completely support the decarbonisation of electricity that will begin to bring wholesale prices down, but the non-commodity costs are adding about £300 of pressure onto a typical bill.”

“We have got to do something radical to address that,” she added.

Green levies and other policy costs are paid for by households and businesses directly through their energy bills, rather than through general taxation.

Under the Government’s net zero plans, the money raised from levies is being funnelled towards a major overhaul of the electricity system to adapt it for a future in which renewable energy sources such as wind and solar farms provide the bulk of Britain’s power.

For example, network charges are rising to pay for an upgrade of the national grid, while policy costs currently include support for wind and solar farms, nuclear power and more nascent technologies such as green hydrogen production or carbon capture.

Ms Fletcher said “a lot of good stuff” was being funded this way, but also claimed that the Government had allowed too many costs to be added in without fully considering their cumulative impact.

She told MPs: “There is no budgetary control of this, and yet it all ends up on household bills or in contributing to making our electricity some of the most expensive in the industrialised world.

“We need to get the growth of this burden under control with some proper budgetary control like we have over other taxes.”

Chris Norbury, chief executive of E.On UK, agreed. He said: “Some of the modelling that we have suggests that you could get to a position by 2030 where, if the wholesale price was zero, bills would still be the same as they are today because of the increase in those non-commodity costs.”

He pointed to “legacy policy costs”, such as the climate change levy and the renewables obligation, which help to fund wind and solar farms. Removing them from electricity costs “would help bring bills down”, he said.

However, neither he nor the other executives could agree on how these should be funded.

Piling costs

Ms Fletcher suggested moving some costs to gas bills, but Chris O’Shea, the boss of British Gas owner Centrica, warned this risked piling higher costs on to poorer households who were likely to be using traditional boilers for longer.

He said Centrica supported moving them on to general taxation in principle, but given this looked unlikely owing to strain on the public finances, “the next best thing is that they’re on electricity bills”.

Mr O’Shea said: “If you move them onto gas bills, that might be attractive because it makes electricity cheaper and makes gas more expensive but everything shows you that because the cost of electrifying means those that are better off will electrify first.

“So what you’ll find is it’s a subsidy from the poor to the rich if you move these costs from electricity onto gas and a further distortion of the market.”

“In the absence of these costs [going into] general taxation, they’re in the right place in electricity bills. They are, however, costing consumers a substantial amount of money,” he added.

In a separate report, the Resolution Foundation urged the Government to move the cost of green levies onto general taxation to save poorer households £110 a year.

This think tank, which is influential in Labour circles, estimates the move would also save middle-income households £40 a year, while the wealthiest 10pc in society would pay more than £300 a year extra.

On Wednesday, campaign group End Fuel Poverty Coalition described the warning from energy bosses as “highly concerning” and called on ministers to set out how they would cut levies on bills.

Maurice Cousins, campaign director for Net Zero Watch, said the “damning admissions” showed that net zero was “driving permanent structural inflation into energy bills”.

Mr Miliband’s department dismissed the warnings from energy companies as “speculation”.

A spokesman added: “Wholesale gas costs for households remain 75pc higher than they were before Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022, and the main reason energy bills remain high.

“The only way to bring down energy bills for good is by making Britain a clean energy superpower, which will get the UK off the rollercoaster of fossil fuel prices and onto clean, homegrown power that we control.”
