data.title: How market jitters could turn to disaster for investors
data.description:
As sure as night follows day, the stock markets wobbled last week after a period of strong growth. The most immediate cause was yet another eruption of Trump’s tariff wars, with China as this month’s target.

Then again, no one should have been surprised, as equity investors were clearly getting a bit ahead of themselves. The mention of stock market melt-ups has accelerated, and one well-known analyst, tentatively it must be said, even suggested that the US benchmark index, the S&P 500, could top 9000 at some point in 2026, assuming a bubble scenario. One driver of said bubble could be the increasingly frenzied AI theme.

All of which brings us nicely to another topic vexing many on Wall Street and beyond: private credit. A widely followed tech commentator, Paul Kedrosky, has recently been making waves with an analysis of how the big AI companies are funding their data centres. As an aside, I’ll return to the subject of data centres and how actually to power them later, but this week let’s focus on what Kedrosky and others have been worrying about, namely the business of financing them – and why it might matter to you.

As I’m sure you can figure out all by yourself, building lots of shiny new data centres requires a lot of money, not least to buy all those expensive, shiny new Nvidia chips, which will probably need replacing in a year or two because they’ll be out of date. The sheer quantum is enormous – in the hundreds of billions per annum – and, as is always the way, the bigger the number, the more creative the funding mechanism.

Some of the funding is coming via rather creative “partnerships”, such as Nvidia taking equity in its customers’ businesses. Another slug of money is coming from the massive cash flows of the major tech leviathans, which are funnelled into new data centres.

But even that may not be enough, especially as capex is becoming an increasing drag on said leviathan’s cash flows and raising alarm bells on Wall Street. Traditional debt financing, such as corporate bonds, has a role to play, but that debt weighs directly on the corporation’s balance sheet, increasing debt leverage ratios.

Wouldn’t it be much better (assuming you were an accountant at a tech leviathan) if this capex binge were being funded by what are called off-balance sheet entities, such as special purpose vehicles that own data centres? In this structure, the parent tech company wouldn’t necessarily assume the debt. It would be transferred to another entity that would pay higher interest rates.

As Kedrosky and others have noted, this is proving especially alluring to many insurance companies (gulp) and private equity shops, both of which are desperate for above-average income yields. At this point, we encounter the two scariest words in the world of central bankers: private credit. You may have seen this term bandied around a fair bit, but let’s first explain what it is, and what it is not. Let’s assume you run Global Tech Leviathan Inc. At the top of the debt stack is corporate credit, which is probably relatively cheap in terms of yield because, if all else goes wrong, lenders (probably banks) can seize those highly profitable trading assets.

These huge outfits don’t need to make much use of the next big chunk of credit-debt – high-yield credit – but smaller, listed businesses, some in the tech space, might, with higher yields, hence the term high-yield. One point worth noting is that the spread between high-yield credit, which used to be called junk, and low-risk corporate debt has been narrowing for some time (gulp).

Next, we have leveraged loans and structured debt, including lots of vehicles with acronyms, many of which tend to be the preserve of private equity-controlled firms. These loans tend to be packaged up and sold on to a syndicate of lenders. Last but by no means least, we come to private credit, which can look and feel a bit like structured finance but often involves looser restrictions on the borrower and just one lender.

What keeps central bankers awake at night – and, let’s be honest, there’s plenty to keep them awake – is the fear that this massive web of complex lending structures could all go wrong. Yet keeping tabs on the opaque structures is incredibly difficult.

The good news, for central bankers at least, is that the vast majority of this private credit is not on bank balance sheets. Still, the bad news is that many, if not most, of the major private credit houses are involved with private credit, and these big beasts are looking more and more like banks in disguise. So if a truly massive PE house has problems, then the system-wide impact could be huge.

Then there are those insurance companies I previously mentioned. Insurance companies have been actively involved in the US private credit market, and the potential here is for yet another AIG: a giant insurer takes a hit because of poor lending decisions and helps kick off the subsequent global financial crisis, as AIG did with the last one.

So, how does this affect the world beyond those worried central bankers – investors like you and me? The first thing to say is that, touch wood, it’s a long way from being a crisis yet. The big tech companies are fabulously profitable, and if their profits take a year or two to take a hit to sort out some of this off-balance-sheet jiggery-pokery, it’s not the end of the world.

And who knows, maybe all that spending on AI and data centres will pay off. But it is another reason to worry about those tech stock valuations, which are already a tad extended. And remember that all those data centres involve expensive chipsets that have to be rapidly accounted for in the books via depreciation. That’s prompted many commentators to wonder whether most shiny new data centres will need to be completely ripped apart and replaced within just a few years, incurring yet more costs.

As for the UK, I’ve seen a few investors fret about the handful of listed lending and private equity funds that are involved in debt financing. Again, I’d counsel caution and avoid confusing fairly established forms of leveraged or structured finance with private credit. There’s a world of difference between data centre private credit, for instance, and say BioPharma Credit lending to fast-expanding biotech firms, or TwentyFour’s two lending funds (TwentyFour Select Monthly Income and TwentyFour Income), using asset-backed finance for large, well-established private equity-backed firms.

Outside of these stock market funds, there aren’t really many ways for private investors to access this broad private credit space, which is probably a blessing for mainstream investors.

But I would issue one final warning. Across the board, the yield gap between much less risky lending to G7 governments and most corporate lending is narrowing, to the point where in many countries, corporates can borrow more cheaply than their governments. That could be entirely rational given worries about government risk. Still, it does seem as though great chunks of the corporate fixed income and lending spectrum are looking a bit rich when it comes to yields, and that’s doubly true if inflation starts picking up speed again.

data.title: One Hyde Park flat on sale for £45m after Hong Kong tycoon goes bankrupt
data.description:
A London flat tied to a bankrupt Hong Kong property tycoon has been put on sale for £45m.

Located across an entire floor in One Hyde Park in Knightsbridge, the 9,000 sq ft apartment is on the market after it was seized from the wife of Pan Sutong, formerly one of Asia’s richest men.

Equipped with a private wine cellar, five bedrooms, six bathrooms and a cinema room, the flat was bought by an offshore company controlled by Liu Jianming in 2011 for £60m.

Sotheby’s has been hired by Ms Liu’s lenders to oversee the sale of the flat, which was built by billionaire property developers Nick and Christian Candy as part of the One Hyde Park development. Details of the lenders are unknown.

Opened in 2011, the apartment block has spas, swimming pools, golf simulators and room service provided by the Mandarin Oriental Hotel Group, with floor space costing up to £7,500 per sq ft.

Residents have at times included Kylie Minogue and the Candy brothers themselves, while also housing Rinat Akhmetov, one of Ukraine’s richest men, and Rory Carvill, an insurance entrepreneur.

Nick Candy’s lavish penthouse was priced at £175m in 2021, making it the most expensive flat ever listed in the UK at the time.

The flat owned by Mr Pan’s wife has been repossessed amid global bankruptcy proceedings.

The tycoon was declared bankrupt in Hong Kong in 2022 after he failed to repay loans relating to his property empire. His debts are thought to total more than £14.2bn.

His business empire included investment conglomerate Goldin Group, through which he owned vineyards in France and California, as well as a horse-breeding and training ground in Australia.

Horse-racing was among Mr Pan’s hobbies, who over the years splashed vast sums on thoroughbreds such as Akeed Mofeed, an Irish colt that went on to win the 2013 BMW Hong Kong Derby.

Mr Pan’s neighbours in Hong Kong included tycoon Li Ka-Shing, Hong Kong’s richest man.

His downfall stemmed from his heavily indebted property empire, which included a partially built skyscraper in China that was twice the size of The Shard. The tower sat within a wider project to build a business district in the area.

Mr Sutong cashed into One Hyde Park as part of a flood of overseas investment in London.

It previously emerged that the majority of the 86 flats in the building had been bought by companies based offshore.

The status of such companies not only protected the anonymity of the property buyers but also allowed them to avoid paying inheritance tax and capital gains tax.

Mr Pan and Sotheby’s declined to comment.

data.title: Tax on rental income: What is it, calculations and how to pay
data.description:
In the same way that workers must pay tax on their earnings, landlords also have to pay tax on the income they receive from tenants.

While buy-to-let investors used to pay tax on rental income “net of mortgage costs”, they can now only claim a 20pc tax credit on their mortgage interest payments, which has eaten into profits.

Here, Telegraph Money explains how tax on rental income is calculated, and what it means if you have multiple properties and rentals overseas.

  • What is tax on rental income?
  • How is tax on rental income calculated?
  • Rental income tax on multiple properties
  • Rental income tax on properties overseas
  • Rental income tax FAQs

What is tax on rental income?

Tax on rental income refers to income tax, which is payable on the money you receive from tenants. The rates, thresholds and tax-free allowances are the same as for any other type of income, whether it’s from employment, or a pension income.

You can also use certain costs incurred as part of being a landlord to reduce your tax bill – but since the rules changed a few years ago, you can now offset less of your mortgage costs.

Chris Etherington, of accountants RSM UK, said: “For residential lettings, there are special provisions for mortgage and other loan finance costs. Relief for these is given as a reduction in tax, rather than set against rental profits, at a rate of 20pc.”

How is tax on rental income calculated?

Tax on rental income works differently depending on whether you are taxed as an individual, or as a company, if you have incorporated the ownership of the property.

If you are taxed as an individual, the income tax will work in much the same way as the tax you would pay on income earned from employment.

Rachael Griffin, of wealth manager Quilter, said: “It is taxed at 20pc, 40pc or 45pc, depending on your tax bracket. The main difference is that you would need to complete a self-assessment tax return and calculate your profits.”

The amount you need to pay is determined by subtracting any tax-free allowances and expenses from your total rental income – resulting in your taxable profit. 

As mentioned above, this profit is then taxed according to your income band.

For the 2025-26 tax year:

  • Basic-rate taxpayers pay 20pc on income between £12,570-£50,270
  • Higher-rate taxpayers pay 40pc on income between £50,271-£125,140
  • Additional-rate taxpayers pay 45pc on income over £125,140.

While most taxpayers pay nothing on earnings up to £12,570 – the personal allowance – this reduces once you earn more than £100,000; for every £2 extra you earn, you lose £1 of the tax-free allowance. By the time you earn £125,140, you’ll have no personal allowance at all. 

As the UK has a progressive tax system, be aware that you need to add any rent from letting a property with any other taxable income to determine the tax rate that may apply.

Ms Griffin added: “For example, if you were earning £35,000 from employment and earned a further £20,000 from rental income, you would be pushed into the higher rate of income tax and would therefore pay the higher rate of 40pc on £4,730.”

Why do you pay tax on rental income?

  • Rental income is considered taxable earnings, like a salary or business profit
  • Paying tax on rental income is a legal obligation
  • While other investments can benefit from being held in an Isa (individual savings account) where growth is tax-free, the Government does not incentivise investing in property in the same way
  • Even though the property tax rules have been put together by different governments over time, a key reason for the current tax rules could be due to the lack of housing supply. The rules in place at present could potentially deter many would-be landlords from starting – or continuing – rental businesses, in theory increasing the number of properties available for owner-occupiers.

Rental income tax on multiple properties

If you have multiple properties, you will typically need to pool together all of the profits and treat them as income from one business. You then deduct allowable expenses for each property to calculate your total taxable profit.

Nicholas Mendes, of broker John Charcol, said: “For example, if one generates a profit and another operates at a loss, the loss from one property can offset the profit from another – potentially reducing your overall tax liability.”

To make this process easier, it is highly advisable to keep separate, detailed records for each property, covering both income and expenses.

Mr Mendes added: “This ensures accuracy and simplifies the process of filing your tax return.”

Note though, that there are some exceptions to the guidelines set out above.

Mr Etherington said: “This includes rental income from properties held in partnership which would need to be declared as a separate rental business.”

Rental income tax on properties overseas

Be aware that profits from an overseas rental property can still be taxable in the UK, even if tax has been paid overseas as well.

Mr Mendes said: “Owning a property overseas adds an extra layer of complexity to your tax obligation. Fortunately, the UK has double taxation agreements with many countries, allowing you to claim relief and avoid being taxed twice on the same income.”

But as navigating the specific tax rules in both jurisdictions can be challenging, it’s worth seeking professional advice to ensure compliance – and also to make sure you don’t end up overpaying on tax.

When to pay rental income tax

The following are the deadlines for the 2024-25 return:

  • 31 October 2025: paper Self Assessment return deadline
  • 30 December 2025: deadline to submit online if you want HMRC to collect tax through PAYE code (for under £3,000 owed)
  • 31 January 2026: deadline for online Self Assessment return and balancing payment for 2024–25, plus first payment on account towards 2025–26
  • 31 July 2026: second payment on account for 2025–26

Failure to declare rental income can result in fines, penalties and in some cases legal action. By staying compliant, you can ensure your tax affairs are in order.

If you let a property and earn income from it, take care to stay within the rules. You will need to calculate profits and file a self-assessment tax return.

Expenses and tax allowances

Expenses:

Allowable expenses are costs which are incurred “wholly and exclusively” for the purposes of renting out a property. They can be deducted from your rental income to reduce your taxable profit. These include:

  • Utility bills
  • Property repairs
  • Service charge costs
  • Letting agent fees
  • Landlord insurance.

Ms Griffin said: “These expenses can help to reduce your taxable income when completing your self-assessment tax return, so it is important to keep a detailed log of all expenses.”

Allowances:

There are a host of property-related tax-free allowances for individual landlords. These include:

  • the property allowance, which can exempt up to £1,000 of property income a year from income tax
  • the rent-a-room scheme, which can allow an owner-occupier to receive up to £7,500 of rental income a year tax-free – provided it is received from a tenant living in their home.

Tax on rental income FAQs

Do you pay tax on rental income if you have a mortgage?

Yes, you still pay tax on rental income, even if you’re paying off a mortgage.

Having a mortgage on your rental property can significantly affect how much tax you pay.

Previously, landlords could deduct the full amount of mortgage interest from their rental income, but in recent years, tax relief for landlords has been reduced.

Mr Mendes said: “Now, if you let a property, you can only claim a 20pc tax credit on your mortgage interest payments.”

This can mean that landlords who are higher-rate or additional-rate taxpayers will not receive full tax relief on their rental income.

Mr Etherington warned: “In some instances, landlords can make a loss on their rental business but still end up with a tax liability.”

How much can I let my house for without paying taxes?

This depends on your other income. If, as a landlord, you don’t have any other earnings – other than rental income – you might have no income tax to pay. This is because the rent you get may fall within your personal allowance. This allows you to earn a certain amount of income tax-free.

Most individuals get a personal allowance of £12,570 (frozen at this level until April 6 2028) – and will not usually have a tax liability if their taxable income is below this.

Additionally, landlords may benefit from the £1,000 property allowance. This can be used as an alternative to claiming actual expenses if it is more beneficial.

Do I pay tax on rental income if it’s my only income?

Yes. Rental income is taxable, even if it is your only source of income.

That said, as mentioned above, if your total income – including rental income – is below the personal allowance (of £12,570 for the 2025-2026 tax year), you won’t owe any tax.

Mr Mendes said: “Despite this, you are still required to declare the income to HMRC to ensure your records are accurate and up to date. Equally, if your rental income does exceed the personal allowance, then you will be required to pay tax on the amount above the threshold.”

data.title: Sky boss vows to protect news amid funding threat
data.description:
The boss of Sky has vowed to protect the broadcaster’s news business in response to fears about future funding from its US owner.

Dana Strong, the Sky chief executive, told staff during a recent meeting that the media giant would support Sky News for the long term regardless of whether parent company Comcast continues to provide funding.

Comcast guaranteed to keep funding Sky News for 10 years as part of its $39bn (£29bn) takeover of the British broadcaster in 2018.

There have been growing concerns that the US company will pull the plug on the heavily loss-making channel when this commitment expires.

Sky News operates on an annual budget of £100m and is thought to be making losses of as much as £80m. Comcast has already written down its investment in Sky by $8.6bn.

The US conglomerate this week began cutting jobs at NBC News as part of a broader shake-up that will see it spin off its declining cable networks such as MSNBC and CNBC. Sky News last month struck a deal to show its global news coverage on MSNBC.

Sources stressed that Ms Strong’s comments related to Sky’s commitments to its news business, rather than any guarantee of funding from Comcast. The US company has been contacted for comment.

Nevertheless, the assurance is likely to reassure anxious journalists at Sky who have been fearful of job losses.

‘Sky News 2030’

David Rhodes, executive chairman of Sky News, announced a major overhaul of the channel earlier this year in an effort to reduce its reliance on dwindling advertising revenue and generate new sources of income such as subscriptions.

The move will see Sky News focus less on live and breaking news and instead funnel resources into areas such as analysis, as well as podcasts and newsletters.

The shake-up – pointedly dubbed “Sky News 2030” – has already led to several high-profile departures, including long-serving business anchor Ian King and breakfast host Kay Burley, who was part of the original team that launched the channel under Rupert Murdoch in 1989.

Sky News this month launched its new-look flagship breakfast show starring Sophy Ridge and Wilfred Frost, the son of British TV legend Sir David Frost.

Speaking at the industry conference RTS Cambridge last month, Mr Rhodes argued that trusted independent news was not dying but rather “shapeshifting”.

However, he acknowledged that modern audiences were more inclined to trust individual journalists and creators than traditional news brands.

The uncertainty over the future of Sky News comes amid broader job cuts at the broadcaster as it navigates the shift to the streaming era.

Sky, which employs around 23,000 people, last month launched a consultation that is expected to lead to 600 job losses. It is the third round of lay-offs in under two years.

The British media giant is grappling to shore up its position in the market amid a decline in its traditional satellite TV business.

Bosses have outlined plans to scale back investment in new technology after a string of expensive product launches that included the Sky Glass Smart TV and a streaming set-top box.

The company will instead focus on improving its existing products as it battles for subscribers with the likes of Netflix and Disney.

A Sky spokesman said: “We are committed to the long-term success of Sky News. Sky is proud of the award-winning, independent journalism it delivers every day, and of the strong progress being made on the 2030 strategy unveiled earlier this year, which includes investment in digital, video and premium journalism.”

data.title: PG Tips owner under pressure to invest in struggling tea company
data.description:
The owner of PG Tips is under mounting pressure to pump more money into the struggling drinks giant, as fears mount over the rapid build-up of risky private loans in the global financial system.

CVC Capital Partners has been warned that its drinks company Lipton Teas, whose brands include PG Tips, faces “a liquidity shortfall or possible debt restructuring over the next 12 to 18 months”.

Ratings agency S&P said in a recent note that “we view the debt burden as unsustainable”.

Lipton has been battling a sharp fall in sales, shrinking market share and dwindling cash resources, increasing the chance of the company defaulting on its debts, S&P said.

Lipton is sitting on a €3.2bn (£2.8bn) debt pile, roughly equivalent to 10 times last year’s pre-tax earnings of €320m.

S&P’s analysts have a CCC+ rating on Lipton’s parent company Cuppa Bidco, putting it several notches below investment-grade status.

Separately, Moody’s has also sounded the alarm with a recent downgrade that pushed Lipton further into junk territory.

Unlike investment-grade status, which is deemed safe and reliable, so-called junk debt is issued by companies that have a greater chance of defaulting.

Private equity debt bomb

S&P’s warning has been made against the backdrop of growing concerns about a debt bomb waiting to go off in the private equity industry.

Market jitters are already being stoked by the emergence of several cracks in the $3tn (£2.2tn) shadow lending market, which has ballooned on the back of a flood of private equity money just as a new era of higher interest rates took off.

Among policymakers and regulators’ concerns is the interdependency at the heart of the private credit industry.

The International Monetary Fund (IMF) estimates that around two thirds of private credit loans sit with private equity-sponsored companies.

Critics also cite a lack of transparency over whether loans are being properly devalued by private lenders when it becomes clear that a borrower may struggle to service its debts.

Moody’s decision to downgrade Lipton was down to “weak operating performance and continued cash burn, which is straining liquidity”, its analysts said in a note published last month.

Lenders to the company are said to be dismayed at the sudden deterioration in its financial state, with one City source claiming bondholders were “gunning” for CVC over the matter.

Lipton’s senior loans were trading at 80 cents in the euro on Wednesday – an all-time low.

CVC paid €4.5bn to buy the company from Unilever in 2022.

The deal was financed with €2.5bn of debt – much of it provided by a consortium of investment banks including Goldman Sachs, Barclays, Bank of America Corp, Citigroup and Deutsche Bank.

However, the banks quickly offloaded the vast proportion of their loans at a steep discount to some of Wall Street’s most aggressive vulture funds, including Elliott Management.

Though it’s not clear which of these still owns the debt, their presence among Lipton’s cohort of major creditors would be likely to complicate any attempt at a financial restructuring.

Earnings going backwards

According to Moody’s, the company has drawn up a series of cash-raising measures, including improvements in working capital, VAT recovery and disposal of non-core assets, which Lipton expects to generate around €160m.

However, even if these initiatives are successful, the capital structure is likely to remain unsustainable because earnings are going backwards, it warns.

With Lipton estimated to be forking out between €250m and €270m in interest payments annually, S&P warned that its financial headroom had “diminished considerably” and the company “may depend on additional funding sources”.

As of June, Lipton had just £17m left of a £375m borrowing facility.

Its strained balance sheet has limited the company’s ability “to withstand any unexpected operating shocks or additional working capital volatility”, S&P added.

CVC ended three decades as a private company last year when it joined the Amsterdam stock exchange.

The €15bn flotation crystallised an estimated €4.1bn paper fortune for senior CVC figures, the bulk of it shared among its three co-founders Donald Mackenzie, Rolly van Rappard, and Steven Koltes. Mackenzie reportedly sold shares worth approximately €163m.

CVC declined to comment.

data.title: Ed Miliband is facing an open revolt from the energy industry
data.description:
Another day, another broken promise from this hapless Government.

Well, not quite yet perhaps, but Ed Miliband’s pledge to bring down household energy bills is as good as dead after it was rubbished by some of the industry’s most influential figures in devastating fashion.

It seems set to join a growing list of unfulfilled, exaggerated and unachievable manifesto promises made by Labour in its desperate bid for power, whether that be a commitment to smash the boat gangs, targets on NHS waiting lists or welfare reform.

The party made scores of manifesto pledges, too many for the vast majority of voters to be able to keep count.

But for a whole host of reasons, everyone remembers Miliband’s guarantee to reduce energy bills by £300 by 2030, not least because it was one of Labour’s flagship promises.

A typical family forked out a wallet-busting £1,740 to heat and light their home last year, unsurprisingly making Labour’s promised £300 saving extremely meaningful for millions.

Indeed, for the many who have been struggling to pay their bills – two fifths of UK households fall into that category, according to charities – it’s the sort of incentive that wins votes.

However, Miliband’s promise of lower energy costs has been on the rocks for months as it becomes ever more apparent that far from being the solution to eye-watering bills, his green energy scramble is actually part of the problem.

Much to his embarrassment, Miliband’s pledge was given a thorough roasting in Parliament on Wednesday at the hands of folk who understand how the energy market works better than anyone.

As a slew of energy bosses queued to warn of higher bills, the hearing highlighted the absurdity of Labour’s vow to magically make bills more affordable by building yet more wind turbines.

The revelations from the stinging energy select committee session leave Miliband’s credibility in tatters – if it wasn’t already, of course.

Among the many marmalade-droppers to come out of the session was that from Rachel Fletcher, the director for regulation at Octopus Energy.

She said that, contrary to Miliband’s rash talk of lower bills, households are likely to be spending considerably more on energy by the end of the decade.

This isn’t because of those much maligned fossil fuels that Labour is attempting to eradicate at light speed either, but because of a slew of green policy costs that help to bankroll the Energy Secretary’s obsession with yet more renewables that the grid can’t cope with.

Almost comically, it turns out Miliband was right to be banding around a figure of £300, just not in the way he anticipated. Instead, that’s how much Octopus calculates will be added to bills in the coming years as a result of net zero-related charges, including green levies.

In fact, green levies are becoming so exorbitant that they threaten to push household bills higher, even if gas and power prices fall dramatically, MPs were warned.

This means that even if the wholesale cost of electricity halved, bills would still spike by around £150 overall, Fletcher claimed.

At which point, Miliband should have gone for a lie down in a field full of solar panels – at least, until the Government can find someone less likely to lead this country down the path to financial ruin.

Instead, the testimony was greeted by Miliband’s department with the sort of response that sounded like it came from a parallel universe or compiled by ChatGPT.

Dismissing the claims as nothing more than “speculation” – which they clearly weren’t – a spokesman trotted out the usual unfounded cut-and-paste propaganda that we are used to hearing by now: “The only way to bring down energy bills for good is by making Britain a clean energy superpower, which will get the UK off the roller-coaster of fossil fuel prices and onto clean, home-grown power that we control.”

It’s a stance that treats voters with contempt. Yes, an energy industry built on fossil fuels is intrinsically wired to display some level of resistance to a green revolution that requires an entirely new system to be constructed.

But to dismiss these concerns as nothing more than pure guesswork is plain wrong.

It’s not as if this were a bunch of frothing-at-the mouth oil and gas zealots out to trash renewables. On the contrary, “we completely support the decarbonisation of electricity”, Fletcher said.

True, bills are high because of the energy crunch triggered by Vladimir Putin’s invasion of Ukraine but that’s only part of the reason.

A big part of the increase is undeniably because households are being forced to shoulder the cost of upgrading a woefully underinvested grid that is in no fit shape to keep up with the pressures that Red Ed’s race to go green requires.

Massive upgrades are needed because we have a fanatical Government whose war on gas boilers and petrol cars means increasing numbers of electric vehicles and heat pumps are set to overwhelm the system further.

Labour is also offering extraordinarily generous price guarantees to new wind farm manufacturers, and we are all about to be landed with a huge bill for the construction of Sizewell C nuclear plant in Suffolk.

And Fletcher also pointed out that the Government has allowed too many costs to be added into the mix without fully considering their cumulative impact.

As she neatly put it: “There is no budgetary control of this.”

Meanwhile, in case anyone thought this was normal, Simone Rossi, the boss of EDF’s UK arm, made the point that the cost of serving customers in Britain is roughly double that of France.

But Miliband won’t be deterred. At a time when concerns about Beijing’s threat to national security have never been greater, ministers, it seems, are okay with the Chinese building an armada of floating wind turbines off the Scottish coast. 

Is nothing off limits in the pursuit of this green nirvana?

data.title: Britain warned of tight energy supplies this winter
data.description:
Britain will import electricity from Europe to keep the lights on this winter as grid operators warn of tight energy supplies over the coming months.

The National Energy System Operator (Neso), which manages the power system, said supplies would come under most strain in early December and mid-January, particularly on cold, calmer days, when output from wind farms falls.

But it also insisted the grid was “secure and resilient” with new gas power stations, batteries and interconnector cables with France and other countries helping to provide the biggest safety cushion since 2020.

In its latest “winter outlook”, Neso has forecast that the capacity margin – the amount of untapped power still available – during cold spells will be about 6.1 gigawatts this winter, or about 10pc of demand.

The forecast came as a separate report by National Gas said Britain would be more dependent than ever on imported gas for heating.

Dr Deborah Petterson, director of resilience at Neso, said: “A resilient and reliable energy supply is fundamental to our way of life.

“At Neso, we are looking at the upcoming winter and can report this year’s winter outlook sets out the strongest electricity margins in six years.”

However, the quango’s report warns that “there may still be some tight days when we need to use our full range of operational tools”.

It said these could be triggered by “periods of cold weather with low wind, low interconnector imports and high levels of generator outages”.

But Neso also added that grid operators “plan for such occasions and have a range of tools to balance the system should they occur”.

In particular, Neso is increasingly relying on power imports via electricity interconnectors with France, the Netherlands, Belgium, Norway and Denmark to back up the UK’s own power stations.

Interconnectors are high-voltage cables laid across the seabed between Britain and its neighbours. They are typically driven by prices, meaning power is pulled into the UK when prices are higher here than they are in France or elsewhere.

Through a backstop system known as the capacity market, where generators are paid to be on standby when supplies are tight, Neso said it expects “at least” 6.9 gigawatts of imports to be available.

That is up by about 300 megawatts compared to last year, following the commissioning of the Greenlink interconnector with Ireland.

On the other hand, the report warned that Norway – which is connected to Britain via the North Sea Link – was facing below-average water levels in its hydroelectric reservoirs following disappointing rainfall, suggesting it may have less capacity to export power this winter.

Some experts have raised concerns about the way Neso calculates its spare capacity figures, arguing it does not give enough weight to the risk of “dunkelflaute” – periods of cold weather when wind speeds plunge.

Last year, Neso issued an alert to capacity market generators in early January as wind output dropped to less than 10pc of nameplate capacity and grid operators scrambled to avoid blackouts.

Neso has always insisted that at no point were supplies close to failing and that it had been holding around 1.4 gigawatts of emergency reserves that went unused.

But Kathryn Porter, an independent energy analyst, accused the quango of overstating the available wind power and warned that relying on interconnectors to bridge the gap during cold spells was risky.

She said: “If you took the interconnectors off in their entirety, that’s pretty much the whole of Neso’s margin gone.

“If we had a dunkelflaute covering all of northern Europe, then all bets are off.”

Separately, a similar winter outlook from National Gas – which manages the gas transmission system – said gas supplies this winter were expected to be “secure” but that the amount of spare capacity in the system had shrunk by a third.

National Gas blamed declining gas supplies from the North Sea and said the UK was increasingly dependent on pipeline supplies from Norway and shipments of liquefied natural gas (LNG) from abroad.

data.title: BBC to show less current affairs
data.description:
The BBC is poised to reduce the amount of current affairs programming it broadcasts in the evening as it battles an exodus of licence fee payers.

The public service broadcaster has asked Ofcom to scale back existing television quotas, warning it will be forced to air more repeats in primetime slots after cutting spending on new shows.

Under existing Ofcom rules, the corporation must broadcast at least 450 hours of current affairs programming across BBC One and BBC Two. Of this, 106 hours must be shown during peak time – between 6pm and 10.30pm – including a minimum of 45 hours on BBC One.

The BBC has asked the regulator to replace this peak-time quota with a simplified requirement to air 70 hours of original current affairs programming. The overall quota of 450 hours would remain unchanged.

The broadcaster argues that it would be forced to increase its use of repeats if the quota were maintained in its current form – which it said would not be in the interests of viewers.

It has also said that there will be no reduction in original programming, insisting that it views quotas as a minimum rather than a target.

Bosses also argue that setting quotas for primetime slots is becoming increasingly redundant as viewers instead opt to tune into flagship shows such as Panorama on iPlayer.

Ofcom said it was minded to accept the change, noting that quotas needed to remain relevant in an era of streaming.

Sweeping cutbacks

Nevertheless, the proposed cuts highlight the impact of sweeping cutbacks at the BBC as it grapples with a decline in licence fee funding. The broadcaster last year outlined plans to cut 155 jobs from its news operations in an effort to save £24m, or 4pc of its current budget.

The overhaul led to the BBC scrapping shows such as its HardTalk interview programme – reflecting the growing shift towards more live and breaking news.

It also closed the Asian Network news service and made cuts to news bulletins on radio stations, including Radio 5 Live and Radio 2.

Deborah Turness, the chief executive of BBC News, said at the time that the cuts were required to “balance the books”.

It forms part of wider efforts by Tim Davie, the BBC’s director general, to save £700m to help plug a black hole in the corporation’s finances.

The BBC’s licence fee income dropped by 30pc in real terms between 2010 and 2020 after a series of freezes and cuts to the levy. It has also suffered a steady decline in licence fee payers, with 300,000 households ditching the fee last year alone.

But the cutbacks have left the BBC increasingly reliant on repeats to fill its schedule. Outside of news and current affairs, the broadcaster said it will spend £150m less on new shows this year as production costs also increase.

BBC executives are currently exploring alternatives to the licence fee after discussions with ministers over the future of the funding model. The broadcaster’s current Charter period is due to expire at the end of 2027.

Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, has ruled out funding the BBC through general taxation. However, BBC bosses are resisting calls for a Netflix-style subscription model, saying this would be incompatible with its public service mission.

A BBC spokesman said: “These proposals reflect changing viewing habits and aim to focus on delivering new, high-quality content while achieving greater value for money for licence fee payers.

“We remain committed to providing high-impact current affairs programming, as shown by our recent Panorama investigation into Charing Cross police station.”

data.title: Spain at risk of fresh net zero blackouts
data.description:
Spain’s electricity grid operator has warned that the country is at risk of net zero blackouts just months after similar issues left millions without power.

Red Eléctrica has requested urgent changes to the way it operates to help it cope with sudden fluctuations in solar energy, according to a document published by its regulator.

It warned that without urgent changes to the way voltage is managed, security of supply would be at risk.

Spain, and much of the Iberian peninsula, was gripped by blackouts in April, triggering a raft of investigations into the cause and disagreements over the extent to which renewables were to blame.

Experts said that the country’s heavy use of solar power left Spain vulnerable to blackouts because of differences in the way renewable generators work compared with traditional power plants.

Madrid’s socialist government has repeatedly denied that renewable energy cause the power cuts, instead blaming bad planning by Red Eléctrica.

Last week, a “factual report”, produced by European Network of Transmission System Operators for Electricity (Entsoe), found that the chain reaction that led to the mass blackouts began when several solar and wind farms in southern Spain suddenly disconnected from the grid.

At least five deaths were linked to the power outage and the incident provoked worldwide scrutiny of Spain’s power system, amid concerns that the issues could occur in other countries, including Britain, that are rapidly shifting to renewable energy.

In the UK, a report by the National Energy System Operator (Neso) said the same problem was unlikely to occur in Britain because generators were already required to help manage voltage.

Red Eléctrica’s latest warning will renew scrutiny of Spain’s growing reliance on net zero energy sources.

On Thursday, the National Commission of Markets and Competition (NCMC) said it was rushing new rules into place to protect “security of supply”.

In a consultation document, it warned that the grid had become less stable because of the rising prevalence of solar farms that tend to adjust their voltage in “step changes” rather than a smoother fashion.

Rooftop solar panels also mean many homes and businesses need to draw less from the national grid on sunny days. This can lead to sudden changes in voltage – for example when a cloud blots out the sun – that have an outsized impact on the broader system.

The NCMC proposed temporary tougher limits on voltage control and stricter scheduling of power output to prevent sudden changes catching grid operators off-guard.

Additionally, the regulator will consult on the plans for five days before introducing them, with the new rules initially planned to stay in place for 30 days.

‘Less secure and prosperous’

The Spanish blackout earlier this year prompted fresh warnings about Britain’s race to net zero, led by Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary.

Mr Miliband wants to make Britain’s electricity system 95pc “clean” by 2030, a shift that would see the country rely almost entirely on renewable energy – such as wind, solar and nuclear – to keep the lights on.

However, critics say more time is needed to ensure the grid is resilient and prepared for the huge shift.

At the Conservative Party conference in Manchester this week, shadow energy secretary Claire Coutinho also accused Mr Miliband of making Britain “less secure and less prosperous” with his policies.

The Government has previously insisted the UK has “one of the most reliable electricity systems in the world”.

In a blog published last month, Craig Dyke, director of system operations at Neso, also insisted Britain’s energy system was “well-protected against the sequence of events that led to April’s blackout” in Spain.

He added: “Our experts are working day and night to ensure nothing of that nature happens here.

“Neso has taken action to mitigate many of the risks seen in Iberia and must continue to build on existing planning, testing, and system monitoring capabilities, particularly in voltage management.”

data.title: Thames Water crisis threatens London super-sewer
data.description:
Thames Water’s debt crisis threatens to trigger millions of pounds in losses for London’s £4.5bn super-sewer.

Tideway Tunnel, which owns the project, has sounded the alarm ahead of a looming handover later this year – with bosses fearing Thames could withhold cash in its scramble to avoid nationalisation.

The sewer owner said it is closely monitoring Thames’s plight and has sought assurances from the Government to ensure the project will not be threatened if the company collapses into special administration.

In its latest annual report, Tideway said: “We continue to note Thames Water’s evolving financial position and this has led to concern that their situation might change.

“There is a possible risk that Thames Water, given its current financial position, or a special administrator may choose not to pay an element of Tideway’s revenue.

“This would not be consistent with Thames Water’s licence and would be expected to lead to a breach and enforcement action.”

As part of its contingency plans, Tideway also considered the possibility of Thames failing to support the sewer’s operations, warning that this “could affect our ability to deliver our investment programme on time and on budget.

“If Thames Water does not comply with the revenue agreement, it could have a financial impact,” the company added.

Concerns over Thames Water’s future come as the company struggles under the weight of a £17bn debt pile.

The company admitted last month that it could enter a taxpayer-backed special administration regime if a rescue deal led by more than 100 creditors fails to succeed.

Thames’s financial crisis is in stark contrast to the completion of Tideway, which has been hailed as a rare infrastructure success story.

The 25km tunnel under the River Thames, which became fully operational earlier this year, represents the biggest improvement to London’s sewage network in 150 years.

Buried 67m deep, the concrete pipe is designed to ease pollution pressures by redirecting sewage away from the river and into a treatment works in east London.

While Thames will not own Tideway, it is expected to be charged an annual fee for its use of the sewer. This will be paid to Bazalgette, a company bankrolled by a group of banks and pension funds.

A Tideway spokesman said: “Tideway is a completely separate, independent company set up to deliver the Thames Tideway Tunnel project.

“This vital new infrastructure is now operational, and has already diverted away nearly eight million tonnes of sewage that would have historically spilled into the Thames.

“We remain confident that Tideway’s revenues are well protected given water industry legislation and the licence obligations that apply to both Thames Water and Tideway. We continue to work effectively with Thames Water to deliver the benefits of the project.”

A Thames spokesman said: “Thames Water collects revenue from customers on Tideway’s behalf and passes it on to Tideway on receipt. Tideway’s revenues are well protected given water industry legislation and the license obligations that apply to both Thames Water and Tideway. We continue to work effectively with Tideway to deliver the benefits of the project.”

data.title: Miliband refused to discuss North Sea with me, says Ratcliffe
data.description:
Sir Jim Ratcliffe was stonewalled by Ed Miliband when he asked the Energy Secretary to discuss his policies on net zero and the North Sea, the industrialist has said.

The billionaire owner of chemicals giant Ineos said Mr Miliband refused to engage in a discussion about Labour’s North Sea drilling ban in a call with industry bosses.

Sir Jim said: “I was on a video call with him and I asked him whether he could explain his policy on energy and the North Sea, but he wasn’t prepared to engage. He said, ‘I’m sorry, but that’s my policy. I’m not prepared to talk about it’.

“I haven’t tried to meet him again – there’s no point in wasting my time.”

Mr Miliband’s policies are under growing scrutiny after data showed Britain’s businesses continued to pay the highest energy prices in the world for the second consecutive year.

The Energy Secretary has been accused of driving up electricity costs by adding green levies on to bills to fund renewables.

This week Ineos said it had cut a fifth of jobs at its Hull chemical plant, blaming cheap Chinese imports and energy costs linked to so-called carbon taxes.

The company has warned its Grangemouth chemicals plant also faces closure after shutting its oil refinery on the same site last year.

Mr Miliband has repeatedly stressed that net zero was not to blame for soaring prices. However, critics have warned his plans to switch Britain’s power system to 95pc clean energy by 2030 risks driving up prices even further.

Sir Jim said it was time for the Government to start listening to business or risk deindustrialisation in Britain.

He said: “I am calling on politicians to make an 11th hour intervention and save the chemical industry.

“Carbon taxes are costing the Grangemouth plant £130m a year – the plant is stressed.

“The site is the size of the City of London and we need a lot of cash just to maintain it. But that money is going to the Government.

“If these carbon taxes remain they will eventually shut down all the chemical plants in Europe, and the products will be replaced by either American or Chinese imports.”

It came as economists warned Britain will lose most of its chemical industry by 2030 unless Mr Miliband lowers green levies on energy.

The report by Oxford Economics, commissioned by Ineos, found that high energy costs has led to a 30pc decline in UK chemical production since 2021, with investment spending by European firms at half the level of their US counterparts over the past five years.

“European policymakers face a critical decision: act decisively now to safeguard this vital strategic industry or risk its irreversible decline,” the report said.

“If European chemical production is replaced by imports from China and the US, total carbon emissions will rise. Chinese and US chemical industries emit 2-3 times more carbon for the same volume of output than those in Europe.”

Sir Jim added: “We urgently need to bring the continent’s energy and carbon taxes in line with the rest of the world and challenge one-sided tariffs. If this isn’t done, there won’t be a chemical industry left to save.”

The closure of Ineos’s Grangemouth chemical plant would also likely deal a major blow to the UK’s energy security.

The chemical plant is also the terminus for the North Sea Forties Pipeline system which brings ashore the oil and gas from about 80 offshore fields, about 40pc of the UK’s entire output.

Grangemouth’s “cracker” facility strips out the gas, feeding it into the UK transmission network and then pumps the oil to UK users or into tankers, meaning the pipeline would become redundant if the factory were to close.

A government spokesman said: “We know this is a tough time for our chemicals industry, who are paying the fossil fuel penalty, with wholesale gas costs remaining 75pc above their levels before Russia invaded Ukraine.

“Ministers regularly meet representatives of the chemicals industry including Ineos, and will continue to engage with the company and the wider sector to explore potential solutions that will ensure a viable industry in the UK.”

data.title: John Lewis to build Reading tower block after overcoming NHS opposition
data.description:
John Lewis has won approval for plans to build 170 rental flats in Reading after overcoming opposition from NHS officials.

The retailer received unanimous support for the £70m housing project at a planning committee meeting on Wednesday night, meaning it has now gained approval for all three of its flagship rental home schemes.

The green light for the Reading development – where it is replacing a vacant warehouse with new flats – follows earlier hold-ups at the site which pushed back the proposed opening date by a year.

These included opposition from various local groups, including NHS officials, who warned that it risked exacerbating pressures on local GP surgeries.

NHS officials in July said local health services were already over capacity, with the new homes threatening to bring in an influx of new patients.

John Lewis later agreed to pay around £147,000 into a funding pot to help local healthcare services accommodate new patients from the proposed development.

It also scaled back the project, reducing the number of homes to 170 from 215.

Richard Bennett, of the Reading Conservation Area Advisory Committee, on Wednesday pointed to complaints from NHS directors who were “desperate for facilities” because their surgeries are in older buildings that are “not capable of suitable enhancements”.

Mr Bennett said he remained concerned over “the lack of modern surgeries able to deal with the thousands of new residents brought by all the developments”.

However, Micky Leng, a councillor who supported the John Lewis project, said that in the south of Reading – where he lives – “we don’t have that much development and we still haven’t got enough GPs”.

He said the retailer had “put the community at the centre” of its plans.

While local officials praised John Lewis for its “exceptional” engagement throughout the process, it also faced criticism over the level of affordable homes planned for the project.

Around one in six homes at the scheme will be affordable, which John Lewis increased from 10pc following renegotiations. Councillors said this still fell well short of Reading’s local target of 30pc.

Councillor John Ennis said: “Local people need affordability, so that’s disappointing.”

Katherine Russell, director of build-to-rent for the John Lewis Partnership, said: “We’re pleased to have the opportunity to invest further in the community by helping to deliver much-needed new homes.

“Our ambition is to take the reputation we have built for service and trust into housing, ensuring residents feel the same quality and care that has defined our brand for generations.”

John Lewis previously gained consent for 428 homes for rent above a new Waitrose in West Ealing, London, and 353 new rental homes above a rebuilt Waitrose in Bromley, London.

Anne Breen, global head of real estate at Aberdeen, said: “Build-to-rent, done well, could go a long way to transform the UK’s private rental stock – much of which is ageing – to become places people truly want to live and call home.”

data.title: The nightmare of living in a conservation area
data.description:
Have you found getting planning permission for a home renovation in a conservation area difficult? Get in touch at money@telegraph.co.uk*

In January 2023, Malcom Peebles and his wife, Amy, took on a project. The three-bedroom, red-brick, late Victorian semi in a leafy part of Surrey needed modernisation, and the couple bought it with the express intention of adding a dormer extension to the loft to create an additional bedroom and bathroom.

Yet this proved easier said than done. More than 18 months after submitting their pre-application enquiry in January 2024, they have made no further progress with their plans. Both their first and second planning applications have been rejected.

The Peebles have spent almost £12,000 on applications, architect and heritage consultant fees, and have even commissioned a Verified View design. This is where photography, survey data, 3D modelling, and CGI are combined to create a photomontage that shows what a development would look like in the real world. However, this has all been to no avail.

And the reason? They live in a conservation area.

First designated in the late 1960s, a conservation area is defined as “an area of special architectural or historic interest the character or appearance of which it is desirable to preserve or enhance”. Local planning authorities have a legal duty to heed this when deciding on applications for development.

There are around 10,000 conservation areas in England, according to Historic England, with the protections most likely to affect exterior changes or any trees on a property.

The beauty of a conservation area is often a key reason people want to move there in the first place, but many find that even sympathetic home upgrades are being routinely rejected.

“It’s galling – there seems to be no desire to accept any changes, even if they are in keeping with the conservation area. We have often wondered whether anyone is actually reading the application or simply says no as a matter of course. It feels very ‘Computer says no’,” says Peebles, 37, a lawyer, who lives in the East Molesey (Kent Town) conservation area, across the River Thames from Hampton Court Palace.

In addition to the delay and mounting costs, what has most frustrated Peebles is the lack of information from Elmbridge borough council regarding why the plans have been rejected – and how they could be altered to make them acceptable.

“Our first application was rejected on the grounds of ‘scale, massing and design’, and so we changed our plans significantly for our second application, and had respected heritage consultants review them and confirm there would be no impact,” he says.

“The crazy thing is that homes on the street have dormer extensions – one of which is significantly larger than our planned one and also has non-conservation windows. We’re planning to use all the right materials, which are more expensive but look in keeping with the area, and the streetscape won’t be affected. It feels completely illogical and unreasonable, and it shows significant defects in the planning process, which appear to go unchecked.”

Peebles, who has lodged an appeal against the latest rejection, also worries about the possible implications for his house price. “We bought on the basis that we would put a loft on,” he explains. “Without it, the house price doesn’t quite stack up.” Elmbridge borough council said it has no further comment.

Even highly experienced architects find themselves falling foul of planning officers in conservation areas. “Combating a puritan view of conservation is the greatest challenge to our practice,” says Richard Parr, of Richard Parr Associates. “Local authority conservation officers have, in my opinion, a default position of ‘No’ and that any alteration is harmful. Long discussions and pre-applications may win them over, but there is no certainty.”

The key battlegrounds

It’s not only large alterations such as extensions that can trip up homeowners in conservation areas. While each conservation area has its own specific rules, much of the legislation protects the property’s exterior decorative details, including mouldings, cornices, bricks, paint colours, front paths and stained and leaded glass. As such, replacing historic single-glazed windows in these locations is often one of the most common challenges.

“Railings, gates and door furniture all come under scrutiny,” says Jonathan Brandling-Harris, of House Collective estate agency. “And for good reason: it’s the uniformity and period charm that make these places so appealing.”

Another major stumbling block comes when attempting to improve the energy efficiency by installing heat pumps and solar panels. One homeowner, who wishes to remain anonymous and is trying to get an air source heat pump approved for her north London conservation area house, says: “There’s so much red tape when you’re just trying to do the right thing for the planet.”

Stefan Pitman must know this only too well. As the founder of the SPASE architecture practice and adviser to Historic Royal Palaces, Pitman has completed the restoration of numerous Grade I listed properties, which receive the strictest level of protection. These include making Dorset’s Athelhampton Manor, which is one of England’s finest Tudor manor houses.

That project involved fitting four ground-source heat pumps and 15 air-source heat pumps. But when it came to fitting a single air source heat pump to another client’s property in a conservation area in central London, obtaining planning permission was delayed by 11 months.

“This was due to noise concerns, even though this was one of the quietest heat pumps on the market,” Pitman says. “Homeowners frequently encounter hurdles such as conflicting permissions between local councils’ planning policies and central Government [emissions] targets.”

Trusts can add to the complexity

Some houses are situated within trusts as well as conservation areas, which makes doing work on them even more complicated. “It usually means a separate consultation process and separate applications for works, often with different criteria, timelines and processes from the local authority,” says Siwan Ifan, of Charlton Brown Architecture & Interiors.

Charlton Brown often works on buildings in north London’s Hampstead Garden Suburb, which is protected by the Hampstead Garden Suburb Trust. Set up in 1968 “to do all things possible to maintain and preserve the present character and amenities” of this architecturally significant area, the trust has its own design guide document and architectural advisers to deal with applications, conduct meetings and site visits.

For example, consent must be sought from both Barnet council and the trust for putting a room in the roof with dormer windows, renewing roof tiles, erecting a garden shed or installing CCTV cameras. A homeowner must also obtain permission from both parties to change the colour of their doors, gates, or window frames, put up or replace a fence, and even if they want to pave or deck an area of their garden.

Additionally, consent from the trust must be sought for removing or renewing a hedge or repaving a path with different materials.

Although an application can be made at any time to the local authority, the trust only meets 10 times a year, which is when the trust council makes its decisions. The trust offers free pre-application advice, and Charlton Brown normally secures consent – or at least positive feedback – from the trust before submitting any proposals to the local authority.

Pick your battles

While upgrading homes in conservation areas can be frustrating, it’s not impossible, points out Robert Cox, of ADAM Architecture. “The key is to approach changes with honesty, pragmatism and sensitivity to the building and the setting.”

Architects often have success replacing single-glazed windows with slimline double glazing from companies such as Histoglass. “The policies across councils not only vary hugely, but are often in flux, so what may not have been acceptable a couple of years ago in terms of double glazing your house might be consented to now,” Ifan says. Many local authorities are also releasing updated planning guidance that supports the installation of solar panels and heat pumps in conservation areas.

Maintaining good relationships with planning authorities and local trusts is also crucial, Cox adds. “The more people feel part of the process, including neighbours, the easier it is on everyone,” he explains. “Many proposals are rejected not because the work is inappropriate, but because no one has taken the time to engage the right people or explain the benefit clearly.”

Sometimes, however, succeeding is a question of picking your battles. Architecture for London recently completed the refurbishment and extension of a Grade II-listed Georgian house in the Barnsbury conservation area of Islington, north London, owned by an art historian and a museum curator.

While the architects succeeded in adding a modest side extension to the lower-ground floor and replacing a rear conservatory with a metal-framed extension, it hit a major hurdle with a non-original bay window on the first floor at the rear of the house.

“This bay window was built in the mid-20th century and, in our opinion, had no heritage value,” says Ben Ridley, of Architecture for London. “We applied for planning consent to replace this with a modern glass bay window, but the local authority refused to grant consent for this part of the works due to a perceived impact on the conservation area – and so the existing bay remains.”

For homeowners attempting to alter their conservation area homes, Ridley points out that rear extensions can often be built under permitted development, assuming the property is not listed. “Loft conversion works well; however, it usually requires planning consent, and therefore dormers will often have to be smaller than they would be if built under permitted development,” he says.

In general, it’s often easier to change the back of a conservation area property than it is the front, according to Parr, whose firm recently carried out a major renovation and extension of a house in Mapesbury, north-west London.

“Try changing the streetscape and there will be quite justifiable resistance, whereas alterations to the rear – which is usually plainer – should not be controversial,” he says. “My advice is that patience will get you there – but be prepared for the time it takes.”

data.title: Meet the high earners dedicated to spending as little as possible
data.description:
When we start making more money, we are naturally inclined to want to spend that extra £1 or £10 on the nicer things in life.

That might look like grabbing bits from Waitrose instead of lining up in Lidl every Sunday for the big shop, or trying a slightly more expensive moisturiser, or picking up the tab and not chasing your friends for the money the next morning.

There’s a name for that: lifestyle creep.

While often small, these extra expenses quickly add up. Throw in inflationary price rises, and before you know it your money is slipping through your fingers – despite you making more of it than ever.

It’s a common issue, but some high-earners take pains not to succumb to lifestyle creep, continuing with frugal habits regardless of how much they earn.

‘I want to accumulate money so it buys me time’

At just 24, Mia McGrath has invested more than £100,000. But she’s not planning to spend it any time soon.

She has been documenting her savings journey from £50,000 across her social media platforms. “When I saved my first £1,000, I was like, ‘Great. Now, can I save £5,000? Now, can I save £10,000?’ It was like a challenge for me,” she says. “I got my gratification from doing that as opposed to spending it.”

McGrath, who lives in London, recently quit her full-time job as a junior sales executive to focus on her social media side hustle and modelling jobs, since the money she was making from brand deals overtook her salary.

While she is making more than many of her peers, there are plenty of items they will throw money at that she refuses to. The first thing is takeaways – McGrath doesn’t even have the Deliveroo app on her phone. She also doesn’t often treat herself to coffee out, or to pastries or pre-packaged snacks between meals. Instead most of her meals are made at home, and rarely cost more than £2.

The same frugal mindset applies to what she spends on her appearance. “On average, I spend less than £30 on my appearance a month,” she says. “My favourite is a £1 Aldi face wash.”

Unless it’s something she deems worthy of splashing out on, McGrath will find a way to spend the least money possible. “Not because I don’t like spending money,” she says, “but because I don’t like spending money on things that I don’t like spending money on.”

There are bigger purchases she also refuses to touch. “Number one being cars because of the costs and the depreciating nature of them,” she says. “But I also live in Zone 2 so I don’t need to [drive].”

Another cost she avoids is pets. “I can’t really understand why people would have a pet and then not feel happy with where their finances are at.”

McGrath has been vocal online about her plans to “soft retire” in just 16 years’ time when she’s 40. By then, she hopes to be in a position where work is optional rather than a necessity to live.

“I want to have enough assets [to] pay me a passive income. I want to accumulate money so that it buys me more time, because time is really precious,” she says.

“What scares me more is retiring at 65 or later, when you have less control of your financial situation. We don’t even know what the state pension could be like in the future. It could be means-tested.”

This doesn’t mean she avoids spending entirely. McGrath says she has a very typical life for someone her age. “I’ve just been on holiday. I buy clothes, I go out with my friends. But if I made an extra £2,000 in a month from side hustles, I don’t see that as extra money to spend.”

McGrath uses the term “frugal chic” to describe her outlook. “When people hear the word ‘frugal’, it [suggests] you’re a penny pincher, you’ll reuse paper towels and try to save 7p on your milk,” she says. “I would say that I’m not like that. I budget. I consider every purchase. But I also live a very normal life.”

‘I get a buzz from saving’

Alexander Cobb, 29, takes home £3,500 a month after tax. Right now, he saves or invests £2,000 of this every month. He has already maxed out his Isa allowance for the year and is putting the rest of his money into Premium Bonds.

For Cobb, a stakeholder manager, his stringent savings habit is about resisting lifestyle inflation. “Every time you receive a pay rise or a bonus, you don’t need to be spending more of it,” he says. “Human psychology is, ‘I’ve earned it, I should just spend it.’ It’s never, ‘I’ve earned it, I could save it.’”

Cobb’s outgoings each month, including bills and his mortgage, total £890. “As my salary has gone up, all my bills haven’t gone up. I’ve been keeping them the same,” he says. “I’ve been really rigorous with my electricity and my water bill. I’m always changing tariffs, making sure I’m on the cheapest at any one time.”

Taking advantage of locking into fixed energy rates for as long as possible, Cobb keeps track in a Google Calendar of when deals are coming to an end and switches to the next cheapest deal.

He also keeps his mobile phone bill down to £10 a month by purchasing his phone outright and only uses a pay-as-you-go Sim card. “I don’t want to tie myself into mobile contracts that have in-contract price rises,” he explains.

Streaming subscriptions are shared between family members, with a WhatsApp group dedicated to coordinating who needs to log in and out, and when. Cobb also doesn’t see the need to pay for subscriptions like Spotify Premium, happily listening to the ads to save the extra £10 a month.

“People go, it’s only £10 a month... [that’s] £120 a year. That could be two date nights out,” he says. The small savings add up. “I paid £100 for return flights for two holidays, [to] Rome with Ryanair, and Copenhagen with Wizz Air. All of that was £100. That’s from not having Spotify Premium.”

Cobb will treat himself sometimes, but even then he will find a way to cut costs. “I’ll find things that are reduced, or ways around not having to spend money,” he says.

This might mean taking advantage of deals or discounts, or using loyalty points or first-time offers. He once used Tesco Clubcard vouchers to pay for a meal out at the pub with friends. “My friends had to pay, whereas my meal was free,” he says.

Cobb also earns extra cash from freelance work, which he channels into investments. “As soon as I get that extra money each month, I’m not thinking that’s an extra holiday or a new car. That is more money into the stock market,” he says. “I think it’s making your money work hard for you, rather than just be that you see it as a tap, and then that’s it gone.”

Saving, for Cobb, is also deeply satisfying: “It gives me a little buzz inside”. He also values working hard for his money and not having it handed to him on a platter.

“I can adjust how I live in the future and then reap the rewards, hopefully sitting on a yacht somewhere,” he adds.

data.title: Vodafone hit by massive blackout
data.description:
Vodafone has suffered a massive network blackout, leaving hundreds of thousands of mobile, broadband and landline customers across Britain without signal or internet for hours.

Complaints about the network problem began at around 2.50pm on Monday, with more than 130,000 people reporting issues, according to Downdetector, which tracks network disruption.

A Vodafone spokesman said its 4G and 5G services were being disrupted by a major network issue. The Telegraph understands the blackout is believed to be linked to a technical fault and is not initially being treated as a possible cyber attack.

The problem also took down the websites for Vodafone UK and VodafoneThree. The website of Vodafone Group, its global parent company, was operating as normal.

Customers of other mobile networks that piggyback on Vodafone’s technology, including Voxi, Asda Mobile, Talkmobile and Lebara Mobile, also reported problems.

Just after 5pm, some Vodafone services began to come back online, including the website for VodafoneThree.

While the cause of the disruption has yet to be confirmed by Vodafone, a UK security source said the incident was believed to be down to a technical glitch.

The disruption will be highly embarrassing to Vodafone, coming just months after it finalised a multi-billion pound deal to combine its UK business with that of rival Three UK.

In marketing to promote the deal, Vodafone had claimed “two networks are better than one” with promises to boost speeds for millions of mobile customers. In one press release confirming the deal, Vodafone promised to bring “vastly superior network quality to tens of millions of consumers”.

Shares in Vodafone fell 0.6pc on Monday.

The company completed a merger of its UK business with Three in June this year, forming a joint venture to create Britain’s largest mobile network with 28 million customers. It also has 1.6 million fixed broadband subscribers across its brands.

The long-awaited merger valued the combined business at £15bn. The combined company had pledged to invest £11bn on improving Britain’s mobile coverage after an investigation by the competition watchdog. The businesses have also updated their systems to allow millions of customers of Three and Vodafone to automatically use both networks.

Margherita Della Valle, chief executive of Vodafone Group, said that the deal would “transform the country’s digital infrastructure and propel the UK to the forefront of European connectivity”.

Jake Moore, a cyber security expert at Eset, said the problems could stem from an issue with Vodafone’s internet domain. Such a problem would “severely disrupt access to digital services” as it “prevents devices from viewing Vodafone’s domains, effectively blocking connectivity”.

He added:“Outages will always naturally raise early suspicions of a potential cyber incident, though current evidence points more towards an internal network failure.”

Data from internet monitoring group NetBlocks showed web traffic linked to Vodafone dropped to almost zero on Monday afternoon. Cybersecurity group Cloudflare said Vodafone had “effectively dropped off the internet”. Cloudflare said the disruption lasted around two hours.

A Vodafone spokesman said: “This afternoon, for a short time, the Vodafone network had an issue affecting broadband, 4G and 5G services. 2G voice calls and SMS messaging were unaffected and the network is now recovering. We apologise for any inconvenience this caused our customers.”

The network problems come amid long-running complaints over mobile not-spots and data speeds in Britain. The UK has suffered from years of underinvestment in its telecoms infrastructure.

The vast majority of UK mobile signal connections – 70.7pc – are still based on 4G, compared to faster 5G technology. Even when users can connect to a 5G mast, they suffer from some of the slowest download speeds in the G7.

data.title: Executives ‘swapping five-star hotels for Premier Inns’
data.description:
Cash-strapped companies are sending senior executives to stay in Premier Inns instead of five-star hotels, according to the boss of hospitality giant Whitbread.

Dominic Paul, the chief executive of the Premier Inn owner, said companies were trading down on hotel accommodation as higher taxes prompt bosses to hunt out cost savings.

He said: “Pretty much all businesses are looking at how they make their costs work harder for them at the moment.”

This includes companies “trading down from four or even five-star hotels to Premier Inn” when booking trips for bosses.

It comes as many businesses battle soaring costs, driven largely by the Government’s decision to launch a £25bn National Insurance tax raid in last year’s Budget.

However, the Institute of Chartered Accountants in England and Wales last week said that companies have begun cutting spending in anticipation of further tax increases next month.

It said business confidence was in “freefall”, as its recent survey revealed that six in 10 businesses cited the tax burden as a “growing challenge”.

Whitbread’s cost-saving initiatives

To capitalise on companies trading down, Mr Paul said Whitbread was working with most business travel agencies to boost sales.

This means businesses can book Premier Inn hotels through their internal systems when planning a work trip.

He said Premier Inn benefitted from “uncertain economic times, which is what we’re in right now”.

However, Whitbread has not been immune to rising labour costs.

Mr Paul said the company had been attempting to offset the National Insurance increase through automation.

This includes rolling out an army of robot hoovers to hire fewer cleaners in newly opened hotels.

On Thursday, Whitbread said it would seek to find cost savings of between £65m and £70m this year, up from previous guidance of £60m.

As a result, Mr Paul urged the Chancellor to resist raising business taxes again in her Budget as she attempts to plug a black hole of around £30bn in the public finances.

Mr Paul said further tax rises would force it to consider moving investment overseas.

It is currently planning to open a series of new UK Premier Inn hotels, which will mean 500 extra rooms by the end of the year.

Mr Paul said: “If we get big increases in cost, we will review the investment plans that we’ve got. We could potentially move investment to Germany, for example.”

Whitbread currently makes more than 90pc of its revenue from the UK, although it also has 63 Premier Inn hotels in Germany.
