data.title: China selling more than 1m electric cars a month
data.description:
China is selling more than one million electric vehicles (EVs) a month as the country’s car makers fight a brutal price war.

The world’s second-biggest economy sold nine million EVs in the first nine months of 2025, an increase of 24pc on last year.

That represented about 60pc of the 14.7 million cars sold globally during the same period.

Data experts Rho Motion said the figures, which include battery electric vehicles (BEVs) and plug-in hybrids, followed record monthly BEV sales in China in September.

It comes as Chinese electric car brands are battling for supremacy at home with aggressive price cuts, prompting warnings from president Xi Jinping against what the government has branded “disorderly” competition.

The price wars have helped to grow the market share of domestic brands and caused huge headaches for Western manufacturers.

But according to the International Energy Agency, the growing affordability of electric cars is one of the driving forces behind rising consumer uptake, particularly for small and medium-sized vehicles. Some 90pc of small cars sold in China last year were electric.

More interest in smaller cars

Best-selling models include Geely’s Xingyuan compact hatchback, Tesla’s Model Y SUV and BYD’s Seagull city car, according to Jato Dynamics. The Xingyuan has a starting price of about 68,800 yuan (£7,200).

Felipe Munoz, an automotive analyst at Jato, said: “We are used to seeing big cars leading the Chinese rankings, but lately we’ve seen more interest from the consumers in smaller cars.

“Geely’s Xingyuan is one example. It’s now even outselling the Tesla Model Y, which still has a very strong position despite its age and despite being a foreign brand.”

At the same time, central and local governments have provided massive state support to the electric car industry, in the form of grants, cheap loans, clean air mandates and charging infrastructure. It all comes as part of a broader strategy to dominate the technologies of the future.

Corrin Reilly, of consultancy New AutoMotive, said: “China has long been leading the way in the transition to electric vehicles seeing success in the up-take of both battery electric cars as well as plug-in hybrids.

“The scale at which China has managed to transition so far is testament to its unwavering commitment to the technologies of the future and stands as a lesson governments across the west must learn from when their resilience is tested.”

Europe also a ‘bright spot’

Rho Motion said Europe has also been another bright spot for EV sales so far this year.

Sales in Europe rose 32pc to three million in the first nine months of the year, boosted by a record month in the UK in September following the introduction of a new government grant scheme.

Germany is soon expected to benefit from a boost as well, with the government there set to launch its own incentives for buyers from next year.

However, US grew sales by just 11pc to 1.5 million after Donald Trump’s administration scrapped tax credits favouring EVs.

Overall, Rho Motion said September marked the first time that the global EV market had topped two million sales “driven by record-breaking demand across major markets.”

On the back of slowing EV sales in the US, General Motors announced a $1.6bn (£1.2bn) hit to its finances.

The company said the one-off costs was owing to consumer demand being weaker than previously expected.

Scaling back production capacity as a result would cost $1.2bn, GM said, with the rest of the costs related to scrapping contracts and settling commercial deals.

It came after the Trump administration last month eliminated a $7,500 EV tax credit and watered down vehicle emission standards – reducing the incentives for consumers to go electric.

“Following recent US government policy changes, including the termination of certain consumer tax incentives for EV purchases and the reduction in the stringency of emissions regulations, we expect the adoption rate of EVs to slow,” GM said.

data.title: Off-plan property: a guide to buying, risks, and rewards
data.description:
Buying a home is likely to be one of the biggest decisions any of us make. For those considering purchasing an off-plan property, the process involves committing to buying a home before it’s even been built.

Usually, investing in a property involves multiple visits and getting a survey done to check for any issues. You won’t need to bother when buying off-plan, as there’s nothing to inspect.

Around a third (31pc) of new homes last year were bought off-plan, according to research from Hamptons. Selling homes off-plan is essential for homebuilders’ cash flow, the estate agency notes, but is it a good way to buy property?

Here, Telegraph Money takes you through what you need to know about buying off-plan, and what to look out for.

In this article, we will cover:

  • What is buying a home off-plan?
  • The pros and cons of buying off-plan
  • How to buy an off-plan property: a step-by-step guide
  • Buying off-plan FAQs

What is buying a home off-plan?

Buying a home off-plan – whether it’s a house or a flat – means you make the purchase before the property is built.

This could mean buying it ahead of construction starting just based on the plans, or while building is ongoing.

However, you are unlikely to be making a purchase based on just blueprints. Developers will often have a show home – a finished version of what other planned properties will look like – that potential buyers can visit to get an idea of the look and feel of the property, and to see if they like the area.

Some developers will also offer packages which let buyers choose extra details for the property – such as a colour scheme or furniture – so the home comes furnished to your tastes and ready to move in.

The pros and cons of buying off-plan

The benefits

Buying a property before it is built means you can benefit from price appreciation during the construction phase and end up with a home worth more than you paid for it.

For example, if you buy the to-be-built home for £250,000 and property prices rise 10pc on average over the period it is built, you move into a house worth £275,000.

Many see buying off-plan as an opportunity to make the most of market movements when property prices are rising. In fact, data from Hamptons shows that off-plan sales are far more common in times when prices are rising quickly on the open market.

In addition, developers often offer discounts or incentives for buyers, such as paying stamp duty or a contribution to the deposit, lowering the overall cost of the purchase and making it an attractive offer for first-time buyers. However, Mr Mendes warns that these add-ons may impact how much you can borrow.

“Lenders may limit the amount of incentive they will accept, often around 5pc of the property’s value. If the incentives exceed this limit, the lender may reduce the mortgage amount accordingly,” he said.

While buying off-plan comes with risks, more protection is offered than if you were to buy a home second-hand.

Nearly all newly built homes now come with warranties for the property that protect you if something goes wrong as a result of the design or construction. Usually, these last for 10 years after the property is completed.

If you are getting a mortgage, lenders will often require a warranty to be in place and come with an accreditation such as one from NHBC or Premier Guarantee, which protects against structural defects and provides peace of mind to the buyer and lender.

The risks

Buying off-plan is often seen as coming with more risks than a home that already exists, even with the guarantees.

One of the biggest concerns is a developer going bust, meaning the building of the property cannot be finished. In this scenario, a liquidator will be appointed to deal with the company and may try to sell the contract on to another party who can complete the building work.

However, this is not always the case, and when your deposit has been used to fund the building, you are unlikely to get it back.

Hugh Wigzell, partner at law firm Farrer & Co, said: “The contract will usually include a long-stop provision that gives rise to a right of rescission if the development is not complete by the agreed date. This means you can reclaim your deposit and walk away from the transaction.

“Of course, this is contingent upon the deposit still being available (either held in a stakeholder account by the seller’s solicitor or protected under warranty). Sometimes the warranty provider will step into the developer’s shoes and build out the project.”

As such, it is worth clarifying how the deposit is being used and whether it is protected.

However, some warranties only protect part of the deposit, leaving buyers who have put down larger sums exposed.

Smaller, although still consequential, risks of buying off-plan include delays to the property being completed, and the finished product not matching your expectations.

If delays are an issue, it’s important to keep check on when your mortgage offer expires and whether it can be extended if the build is taking longer than anticipated.

Mr Mendes added: “Some lenders may assess the financial health of the developer to ensure there is a low risk of project failure. If a developer is considered financially risky, the lender may either decline the mortgage application or request additional security.”

There is also the risk that if your lender doesn’t place the same value on the property once it’s finished as it is being sold for then you may not be able to get a mortgage to cover the full amount.

Mr Mendes suggested keeping a contingency fund to cover a shortfall if necessary. And while it is possible to get a contract to buy the property that is contingent on you receiving mortgage finance, Mr Wigzell said he has never seen a developer agree to this in an off-plan transaction.

However, if it turns out the finished property is smaller than advertised, Mr Wigzell said the contract usually provides a mechanism for a proportionate reduction in price – so you should get money off.

How to buy an off-plan property: a step-by-step guide

In order to secure an off-plan property, typically, a specific timeline is followed. Generally, this can be broken down into five steps:

  1. Secure your finances
  2. Research the property developer and the area
  3. The reservation process
  4. Appoint a solicitor and exchange contracts
  5. Completion and handover

Step 1: Secure your finances

Before purchasing a property off-plan or otherwise, it is absolutely crucial to understand your budget.

By speaking to a mortgage broker (preferably one who specialises in new builds), you can acquire an agreement in principle (AIP), giving you a clear indication of what you can afford. It also demonstrates to developers that you are a serious buyer.

Step 2: Research the developer and the area

Not all developers are the same, and it is important that you investigate the developer’s track record to ascertain its credentials.

Look for past projects, read reviews from previous buyers, check for any history of disputes and ensure that they are registered with a warranty scheme such as the NHBC. Although the property will not have been built yet, make sure to visit the development site at different times of the day to get a feel for the location, transport links, and local amenities.

Step 3: The reservation process

Now that you’ve chosen the property of your dreams (quite literally), it’s time to put the home-purchasing wheels in motion. Typically, this starts with a reservation fee, which can range anywhere between £500 and £2,000. This takes the property off the market and fixes the price, eliminating the danger of last-minute price hikes.

You should then be presented with a reservation agreement, outlining the key terms of the sale, which is wise to have your solicitor view before signing.

Step 4: Appoint a solicitor and exchange contracts

Speaking of solicitors, you will need to appoint one, preferably one with experience in off-plan conveyancing.

They will need to conduct due diligence and review the contract, raising any valid concerns and enquiries along the way with the developer’s solicitors. Once these checks are complete and your mortgage offer is secure, you will sign the contract and pay the exchange deposit (typically around 10pc of the purchase price).

This is the point at which the agreement becomes legally binding.

Step 5: The completion and handover

Once the contracts are exchanged and the exchange deposit is paid, all that’s left is to complete on the sale and for the handover to be secured.

On completion day, the remaining balance of the purchase price is transferred over to the property developer and you become the legal owner. The developer will provide you with a notice to complete once the property has been constructed and is ready, usually around a fortnight before the move-in date.

Make sure you are able to attend a final inspection, where you can identify any minor issues that need fixing before you move in.

Off-plan property FAQs

Can you get a mortgage when buying off-plan?

While you can get a mortgage when you’re buying off-plan, lenders are more cautious when the loan is for new build properties, said Nicholas Mendes of brokerage firm John Charcol.

This is because new-build properties tend to come at a premium, and are “notoriously challenging to resell” so lenders are concerned about whether they will be able to recoup all of their money back.

As a result, you may be offered a mortgage with stricter lending terms than for other properties, Mr Mendes warned.

For instance, when Yorkshire Building Society launched a £5,000 deposit mortgage last year, the terms and conditions revealed it couldn’t be used to buy a new-build property.

“Many lenders will only offer mortgages on new builds from developers who are registered with specific bodies, such as the National House Building Council to ensure that the property is built to a reliable standard,” he added.

What is the difference between off-plan and on-plan?

An off-plan property has not been completely finished, whereas an on-plan property is fully completed and ready for you to move in.

Is buying an off-plan property a good idea in a falling market?

Not usually, as buying an off-plan property in a falling market can result in its value decreasing before completion, resulting in a loss on your investment. It is typically considered better to buy an off-plan property in a rising market due to the risks involved.

data.title: HS2 ‘will mean more traffic on clogged-up roads’
data.description:
HS2 threatens to add to the number of trucks on the road by worsening congestion on existing tracks, the boss of Britain’s biggest rail freight firm has warned.

Tim Shoveller, the chief executive of Freightliner Group, said that because it ends at Birmingham, HS2 is unlikely to ease pressure on the West Coast Main Line, a key route for cargo.

At the same time, the connection between HS2 and the West Coast Main Line at Handsacre Junction, Staffordshire, will force trains to slow, creating a bottleneck that will reduce capacity below current levels, Mr Shoveller added.

He said: “With HS2 running only from London to Birmingham it’s very difficult to see that any traffic will move from domestic rail.

“And, frankly, the connection at Handsacre will actually mean there’s a net capacity reduction on the railways because it’s a really suboptimal design.

“It’s a bit like building a new road alongside a motorway but still getting to the same roundabout at the end, with all the congestion that involves.”

Mr Shoveller said pressure was “rightly” mounting on the Government to review how to make the best of HS2 and avoid pushing shipments onto crowded roads.

HS2 was originally intended to shorten journeys between London and the North while freeing up capacity on the West Coast Main Line for slower passenger trains and freight.

However, following Rishi Sunak’s decision to scrap the northern reaches of the line in 2023, services to Manchester, Liverpool and Glasgow may have to stick to the existing route.

At the same time, trains that join the West Coast Main Line from HS2 at Handsacre Junction will have to use slow lines usually devoted to freight.

Modelling by economists at MDS Transmodal suggests that, at best, freight capacity to the North West and Scotland will be capped at current levels.

That could act as a barrier to the Government’s target of growing rail freight by 75pc by 2050 as part of its push for net zero.

Freight trains cut carbon emissions by between 76pc and 95pc per ton compared with road haulage, Mr Shoveller said.

Mr Shoveller also appealed to the Government to end a freeze in HGV fuel duty, which he said combined with higher track access charges had led to rail costs rising three times faster than for road over the past decade.

That has seen the distance over which moving containers by rail from southern ports is more competitive extended by 80 miles from Birmingham to Manchester, he said.

He said it is also vital that the Office of Rail and Road remain an independent arbiter as the Government nationalises passenger services and combines them with Network Rail – which sets charges and allocates train paths – to create Great British Railways.

The Government has been approached for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: In 60 minutes I saw 172 cyclists run these lights – it’s a matter of time before they kill someone
data.description:
Spend a single hour during the morning rush standing at a set of traffic lights circling Bank Junction in the City of London, and it becomes clear that, for many cyclists, red means go.

It means go for two women who whizz through on push bikes, with empty child seats behind them rather concerningly indicating a completed school run. It means go for the commuter singing loudly on his rental Lime e-bike; the chap in high-vis gear clutching two carrier bags precariously on his handlebars; the young schoolgirl in skirt and socks; the push biker who weaves around a pedestrian wearing a “Baby on Board” badge.

Between 7.30am and 8.30am on a Thursday, The Telegraph counted these cyclists among 172 who jumped a red light on King William Street. This equals a rate of nearly three a minute.

If each were fined the £50 fixed penalty notice (FPN), which can be issued for the offence of failing to stop for a red light on a bicycle (and technically, an amber, too), then £8,600 would be raised. That figure would be even higher if those riding on pavements, two-to-a-bike without holding the handlebars – all of which we observe, and are offences, too – were fined as well.

If you were to double that figure to account for the two hours of the morning commuter rush, and double it again for the same evening commuter period, you would muster £34,400 each weekday for those times alone. That is £8,703,200 during a regular work year. Not to mention, there are three additional sets of lights positioned around Bank Junction.

According to a new survey commissioned by e-bike provider Lime, more than half of London’s cyclists run red lights. In 2024, 1.3 million bike journeys were recorded per day in the capital, which makes for a lot of red-light jumping. Furthermore, 82 per cent admitted they recognised it is dangerous. The survey included general e-bike and regular bike users, and not just Lime customers.

Last month, it was reported that the City of London Police are so concerned by the proliferation of offenders, they are exploring the introduction of tougher penalties. These include Community Protection Warnings and Notices, which treat the behaviour as anti-social, and could result in court appearances and criminal convictions for repeat offenders.

The measures were discussed at a meeting of City of London Corporation’s Streets and Walkways Sub-Committee, following Tube strikes earlier this month. Those strikes saw further mayhem as usually infrequent cyclists swarmed the roads. The London Ambulance Service reported a 33 per cent rise in collisions involving cyclists during that time.

At Bank Junction, so many cyclists jump each red light – some at high speed without any hesitation – that you start to believe the practice is as much a commuting ritual as AirPods and clutched coffee cups. The dangers of red-light jumping, however, put it in a league of its own.

“A cyclist ran over my foot when I was crossing here, he rode straight through and yelled at me to ‘get out the way,’” says Sarah Beale, 39, a personal assistant who commutes from Chelmsford. “You have to cross with your wits about you,” she adds, looking like her head might swivel off as she glances both ways on repeat. “It’s scary. I was crossing at a green man, what did I do wrong? It’s often the people on rented bikes going through the lights.”

Are they ignorant of the rules of the road? “I think it’s that they don’t care.” Beale got away with bruising. “It’s not just a London thing, but it’s busier here. We get a fine as a motorist if we go through a red light. It might stop them if the fine was more.”

Just minutes later, I meet a second casualty. John Rowland, 42, a political consultant from Weybridge, Surrey, also has a story to tell as he waits to cross.

“I was hit by a cyclist who ran a red light when I was crossing at a green man near Buckingham Palace,” he says, removing his headphones. “It hurt. I mean, I was lucky. I was bruised and bashed on my leg. He was going at quite a pace. He came flying off his bike down the road. He got up and was really p----- off. It was a shock to him as well.” Fines should be enforced, he says vehemently.

The second The Telegraph stands by the lights and watches them turn red, readying to count, a cyclist shoots through. It is a Lime e-bike. Two more cyclists follow within the first minute. During that first 15 minutes, we see 13 whizz through.

The majority are clearly commuters in gym wear or office clothes. Around half wear helmets, and some are on collapsible bicycles designed for train journeys. Among them are a handful of what look to be illegal e-bikes – non-rentals that appear to travel faster than the legally permitted 15.5mph (above this, the power should cut out and riders must rely on pedalling). However, the overwhelming majority would not strike you as lawbreakers.

At times, up to nine cyclists stream through as amber turns to red. One young woman barely looks about her as she wobbles off nonchalantly through the red. Another, in high-heeled boots, stops and starts haphazardly.

Others go through, and then are forced to pause in the centre of the junction as double-decker buses turn. I witness some near head-on collisions between cyclists. On one occasion, a man cycles through a red, goes round the corner and almost crashes into a reversing police car.

On a number of occasions, even a green crossing man does not stop them winding around pedestrians. Strangely, everyone seems so accustomed to it I don’t witness one altercation. Pedestrians shrug. “Nightmare,” one mutters in my direction. Another, a cyclist himself, assures us he does it too but “elsewhere”, not here. That “would be dangerous”, he adds as he crosses the road, texting on his phone. His road safety proficiency on foot is almost as concerning as his cycling logic.

The City of London Police’s cycling response team regularly targets the issue on patrol. In July, it announced nearly 300 cyclists had received FPNs for going through red lights so far in 2025. Speaking to The Telegraph, Sgt Stu Ford, who leads the team, says that figure increased substantially during the last week of September when they issued 104 FPNs as part of a targeted effort, the majority for red-light jumping. “That doesn’t surprise me,” he says when we reveal how many jumpers we have counted.

Officers use their discretion when issuing fines. Capacity is not there to stop every single red-light jumper, but it is clear the police’s patience is wearing thin. “I find it hard to believe in this day and age that people don’t realise they have to stop at a red light,” says Ford. “Their answer a lot of the time is, ‘Oh, well, yeah, sorry’, or, ‘Oh, well, I thought it would be OK’. Cyclists need to understand the red light is for them as well.”

He is not convinced a £50 fine is enough of a deterrent. “It’s been £50 for quite some time. You have to ask if that’s [high] enough. Maybe not.”

Ford would like to issue prolific repeat offenders with Community Protection Notices which, if breached, would become a criminal matter. “We want people to take it seriously. It’s not about the money it’s making… What we want is for people to stop at those red lights,” he says.

At Bank Junction many do, but a shocking number do not. The pedestrians who scatter out of the way of a cyclist who jumps a red light with, inexplicably, a bicycle tyre strapped to his unwieldy rucksack, are thankfully braced to check both ways, and check again. And then once more.

data.title: Steve Reed lines up administrators for Thames Water
data.description:
Ministers have appointed City insolvency advisers to prepare for the potential collapse of Thames Water.

Steve Reed, the Environment Secretary, has selected FTI Consulting to help draw up contingency plans in the event that the struggling utility giant is placed into a taxpayer-backed special administration regime.

It comes as Thames Water, which has 16 million customers across London and the South East, teeters on the brink of collapse as it struggles under a £17bn debt pile.

Its future was cast into fresh doubt in June after private equity firm KKR pulled out of a £4bn rescue deal.

The company’s creditors have put forward an alternative £17bn rescue plan, but the bailout is contingent on regulators agreeing to waive historical fines.

Mr Reed has previously suggested this would not happen, making a government intervention appear increasingly likely. It is estimated that a state bailout would cost the taxpayer as much as £4bn.

The Environment Secretary in June said Labour was stepping up preparations to temporarily nationalise Thames Water. A government source said the move was a natural consequence of this.

The move to appoint FTI, which was first reported by Sky News, places the consulting firm in prime position to act as the company’s administrator should it collapse into insolvency, though the appointment of a special administrator is ultimately a decision for the court.

A Government spokesman said: “The Government will always act in the national interest on these issues.

“The company remains financially stable, but we have stepped up our preparations and stand ready for all eventualities, including applying for a special administration regime if that were to become necessary.”

Thames is currently locked in discussions with its creditors and regulator Ofwat over a new rescue deal for the company.

Creditors have been pushing for ministers to intervene to wipe out historical fines, saying a “regulatory reset” was required to ensure the utility is financially sustainable.

Thames was handed a record £123m fine in May and creditors say it could be in line for penalties of more than £1bn relating to environmental and pollution failings.

The £17bn rescue plan involves injecting £5bn in new equity and debt and writing off billions of pounds in existing debt.

A Thames Water spokesman said: “Our focus remains on a holistic and fundamental recapitalisation, delivering a market-led solution which includes targeting investment grade credit ratings and returning the company to a stable financial foundation.

“Constructive discussions with our many stakeholders continue.”

data.title: Hammersmith Flyover could be replaced with tunnel
data.description:
The Hammersmith Flyover in west London could be demolished and replaced with a tunnel.

The six-decade-old concrete bridge could be knocked down under proposals from Hammersmith and Fulham council.

Under the Labour-led council’s draft Local Plan, the flyover may be replaced with a “flyunder”, referring to a tunnel to carry the four-lane A4 Great West Road.

Councillors are expected to vote on whether to accept the plans on Monday, with the draft proposal claiming the demolition would release land for development while improving the town centre’s noise and pollution levels.

Papers scheduled to be presented to them say that demolishing the flyover “has the support of local people, the Mayor of London and TfL [Transport for London]”.

The vote on whether to build the tunnel comes despite the council spending millions on a refurbishment project, which TfL said would make the bridge “safe to use for decades to come”.

The flyover, which was opened in 1961, was intended to reduce traffic levels in Hammersmith.

However, the draft plan noted that it “had adverse consequences, cutting off Hammersmith Town Centre from the River Thames, severing the traditional Victorian street pattern and creating large amounts of traffic”.

The ageing bridge has required increasing amounts of money to keep it in a safe and usable state, with around £100m being spent on it in a project that finished 10 years ago.

Engineers had discovered that salty water from anti-icing treatment on the road surface had seeped through leaky waterproofing, reaching the steel beams inside the bridge that hold it together.

Those had to be stripped out and replaced, with the two-year project finishing in 2015.

The council’s draft plan says developers hoping to win the contract to demolish the flyover and build the tunnel should also be prepared to develop the site as well.

This should include “mixed-use redevelopment”, including housing “for local people across a range of tenures and affordability” as well as new hotels, shops, “cultural and leisure facilities” and “supporting infrastructure”.

The council also hopes to “return the Hammersmith gyratory to two-way working and improve the capacity of Hammersmith bus station”.

“Redevelopment of the site should explore ways to improve pedestrian connectivity between the District and Piccadilly line station, and Circle and Hammersmith & City line station,” the document says.

The council hopes its 15-year plan will be ready for adoption in November 2027.

data.title: North Sea giant makes fresh push to drill UK’s biggest oil field
data.description:
A North Sea developer has resubmitted plans to exploit Britain’s largest untapped oil field in a major challenge to Labour’s ban on new drilling.

Equinor said on Wednesday it was once again seeking permission to produce oil and gas at Rosebank, located north-west of the Shetland Islands.

The Conservatives had granted the state-owned Norwegian oil company permission to drill at Rosebank while in power, only for the High Court to overturn the decision earlier this year. The court also blocked production at Jackdaw, a gas field in the North Sea.

This was on the basis that the projects’ carbon emissions had not been properly accounted for.

It means that the question of whether to wave through Rosebank now falls to Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, who previously described the project as “climate vandalism” and vowed to ban all new drilling in the North Sea.

Labour has said the drilling ban only applies to new developments, with Sir Keir Starmer reassuring Equinor before last year’s election that approved schemes such as Rosebank would be allowed to go ahead.

The oil field is Britain’s largest undeveloped site and could contribute 4.5pc of the country’s gas supplies at its peak.

Equinor’s fresh application has been lodged as Mr Miliband prepares to water down Labour’s drilling ban over fears it is fuelling an exodus of developers.

This proposed about-turn follows growing criticism over Labour’s retreat from the North Sea, including from Donald Trump, who described the ageing basin as a “treasure chest” for the UK.

The Conservatives and Reform have both vowed to imitate the US president’s pledge to “drill, baby, drill” if they win power at the next election.

This is in contrast to Mr Miliband, who has repeatedly argued that extracting more oil and gas from the North Sea will not bring down energy prices.

Environmental campaigners urged the Government to block Rosebank’s latest application, calling it the “defining test of this Government’s credibility on climate change”.

Tessa Khan, of anti-fossil fuels lobbying group Uplift, said: “Rosebank is a very bad deal for the UK. It won’t lower bills and [would] do almost nothing to boost energy security, given that most of it is oil destined for export.

“The Government must stick to its word and invest in clean energy, which is powering growth, and stop propping up an industry that is now only viable with massive state support.

“It must not allow a declining oil and gas industry, or its cheerleaders in politics, to dictate UK energy policy. This Labour Government must do the right thing and reject Rosebank.”

A spokesman for Equinor said: “We remain fully committed to working closely with all relevant stakeholders to advance the Rosebank project. As long as there is a need for oil and gas, it matters where and how it is produced.

“Rosebank can help counteract the decline in domestic UK production with oil and gas that is produced with a much smaller carbon footprint than the average on the UK Continental Shelf.

“Rosebank is an important contributor to the UK’s energy security. It is a vital project for the UK economy and is already bringing significant benefits in terms of local investment and jobs.”

A government spokesman said: “We are unable to comment on individual projects.”

data.title: Tagged shoplifters to trigger alarm when entering banned areas
data.description:
Prolific shoplifters are being tagged for the first time to set off alerts when they go near stores they have previously targeted.

Officers can use the GPS technology to track shoplifters and ensure they are not breaching “exclusion zones”.

Police and shop owners receive instant alerts if a tagged shoplifter enters a banned area, which can cover entire towns, specific streets, or individual shops. Offenders face up to five years in jail for breaching the orders.

The technology is so advanced that police can also set a secondary 300-metre perimeter, allowing officers to warn nearby shopkeepers when a known offender is in the area.

The scheme could be expanded to map thefts and cross-reference them with the movements of tagged offenders to establish if they are linked to offences.

Police chiefs believe the initiative could be a “game-changer” in tackling shop crime, with the most prolific 10 per cent of offenders estimated to be responsible for up to 70 per cent of store thefts.

The pilot is being pioneered by Sussex Police, which has secured the first court-imposed criminal behaviour orders to tag two repeat shoplifters.

Victoria Hale, 50, a convicted repeat offender, has been ordered to wear a GPS tag for 12 months and banned from going within 30 metres of a Co-op store in Eastbourne.

A second order was imposed on Barry Farthing, 41, whose exclusion zone covers a Co-op in Hastings. He will begin wearing the tag when he is released from prison in January.

Katy Bourne, the Sussex Police and Crime Commissioner and the national policing lead on retail crime, said: “This could be a game changer. We know it’s a handful of individuals who cause the most harm. If we can change their behaviour, we can stop it.

“A success would be a reduction in reoffending for the individual, a drop in the number of thefts being reported and a change in their behaviour if they attend rehabilitation programmes.”

Shoplifting has hit a record high in the year ending in March 2025, with police reports of the offence up by 20 per cent in a year to 530,643, equivalent to nearly three thefts a minute. Some 29,000 prolific criminals are estimated to cost the public purse £5bn a year through repeat shoplifting, theft, and violence, a study revealed last month.

Tags are already being used to enforce geographical restrictions and curfews on sex offenders, domestic abusers, stalkers and burglars but they have not previously been used on shoplifters.

Det Insp Martin Harmer of Sussex Police, who is overseeing the tagging initiative, said the technology had become more sophisticated, enabling police officers to track offenders worldwide in real time.

The tags are tamper-proof and resilient enough for offenders to go swimming with them on. They could be programmed to vibrate if an offender went into an exclusion zone and only stop when they leave. Police could also send texts to offenders via the tags with alerts, such as warnings that their tag needed to be recharged.

Mr Harmer said the tags could be used with offenders who were homeless, as they could wear a portable power pack on their bodies to charge them. This would require them to come to the police station every few days to recharge the pack, where they could also be provided with addiction, mental health, or other support.

He added: “The next step is wrap-around care. If they are coming in every two days, we can use that as an intervention programme with a drug, alcohol or homeless [case] worker.”

Adam Godden, a specialist adviser for business crime with local councils, said the current judicial system was “not working.”

He said: “We are looking forward to seeing how this will impact on offenders’ behaviour and what other intelligence can be gained from this monitoring and, most importantly, what relief it will give to businesses that these offenders have relentlessly been targeting.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Starmer’s AI drive ‘will lead to hosepipe bans’
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer’s artificial intelligence (AI) plans risk leading to hosepipe bans and could drive up water bills, a government advisory body has warned.

A report from the Government Digital Sustainability Alliance (GDSA) said that placing major data centres in water-stressed areas could lead to “increased water bills [and] rationing”, adding it risked “potentially exacerbating social conflicts”.

Last week, the Prime Minister trumpeted a series of promised investments in AI data centres, including the construction of Britain’s most powerful AI supercomputer and a new “AI growth zone” in Northumberland.

The string of investments by US tech giants, which coincided with Donald Trump’s state visit, led to predictions of a “big bang” for the British AI industry.

However, the GDSA, a group set up by the Department for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs, claimed that Britain’s water supplies were not adequately prepared for a boom in data centres, which can require large quantities of water to cool computer systems.

It warned that the tech projects were a “blind spot” in official water forecasting, and that utilities were not planning for a surge in data centre infrastructure.

“The siting of water-intensive infrastructure in already water-stressed areas, particularly those with existing socio-economic vulnerabilities, raises significant environmental justice concerns,” it said.

“This practice could disproportionately impact local communities, potentially leading to increased water bills, rationing or reduced environmental flows.”

It warned there was a conflict between the Government’s AI dreams and Britain’s ability to manage water supplies.

“The rapid expansion of AI presents a profound challenge to the UK’s water security and environmental sustainability,” it said.

“The evidence demonstrates that AI’s substantial water footprint, primarily driven by data centre cooling and energy generation, is escalating at an alarming rate and is largely unaccounted for in current national water resource planning.

“This critical oversight, coupled with existing and projected water deficits in the UK, creates a significant risk of exacerbating water stress, leading to potential social and environmental conflicts.”

Source of conflict

The country is already facing pressure on its future water supply. England will require an extra five billion litres of water a day by 2050, according to the Environment Agency.

The GDSA, which is made up of business executives, academics and industry groups, exists to give the Government advice when developing new policies. Its members views do not necessarily reflect those of the Government.

The report by Chris Kenny, the co-chair of one of the GDSA’s panel groups, said measures recently proposed by water companies to improve Britain’s water supplies by 2050 did not account for the growth in data centres.

Data centres are crucial to AI and use large amounts of energy. Last week, Microsoft pledged to invest £22bn in Britain, the majority of which will go towards new infrastructure.

Thames Water warned that its water supplies risked being stressed by more than 100 new data centres planned in the regions it covers.

The tech industry has disputed that data centres risk adding to Britain’s water problems. Industry body techUK said in a report last month that the majority of new data centres used so-called closed loop-cooling systems that do not require any external water supplies.

Plans to support the AI industry have led to other conflicts within the Government.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, has championed a proposed giant data centre on Teesside that would conflict with a proposed hydrogen plant believed to be supported by Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary.

A government spokesman said: “Data centres are part of the vital infrastructure behind the digital technology that powers our businesses and everyday lives, and we need substantial investment to harness AI’s transformative potential.

“The planning system takes into account water scarcity and local plans themselves should take into account the long-term implications for water supply. We’re also unlocking £104bn in water infrastructure investment, which includes improving water supplies around data centres.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Innocent pensioners face fraud investigations under new bank account snooping laws
data.description:
Innocent pensioners will be dragged into lengthy fraud investigations by new Government snooping powers, charities have warned.

The House of Lords is debating new rules that will allow the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) to demand data on benefit claimants’ accounts from banks and directly claw back overpayments in a clampdown on fraud and error.

But charities have warned millions of retirees on pension credit could be wrongly dragged into the crackdown and forced to go through a “lengthy appeals process” to get their money back.

In an open letter to Pat McFadden, the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions, charities including Age UK and Silver Voices said new rules would constitute an “unprecedented level of population-wide mass surveillance”. The letter added that the new powers would leave members of the public at “risk of algorithmic errors, wrongful investigations and burdensome appeals”.

It cited benefit claimants who said they had previously been investigated by the DWP after being wrongly flagged as fraudulent claimants.

The powers are due to be granted under the Public Authorities (Fraud, Error and Recovery) Bill, currently in the House of Lords. It means the DWP will be able to demand that banks share data on those receiving a number of benefits.

This information will be examined to find evidence of fraud and government error, and money could be reclaimed directly from “relevant” accounts, using recovery powers expanded by the legislation.

An estimated £9.5bn was lost last year due to benefit overpayments and fraudulent applications, the equivalent of 3.3pc of the total benefits bill.

The Government previously insisted to The Telegraph that it would not have access to bank accounts and that decisions about benefit entitlement would be taken by a human.

Instead, these powers will enable the DWP to order banks to recoup overpaid money directly from accounts, even without a court order.

The letter, also addressed to Baroness Sherlock, a minister in the DWP, was signed by 11 campaign groups and charities, including Disability Rights UK, and Big Brother Watch.

It quoted pensioners concerned about the surveillance powers. One pensioner claiming pension credit said: “I occasionally need to pay quite large bills at one time thus depleting my savings. I am concerned that this movement in my account may trigger the algorithm to flag up a defraud.”

Another said they’d stopped buying lottery tickets in case a win was flagged as unusual, saying: “I genuinely won’t know if I do something wrong by mistake and end up losing the roof over my head as a result.

“Because of this I’ve stopped doing the lottery just in case it takes me £10 over what I’m supposed to be entitled to, and I’m paranoid about subscribing to anything in case it makes my bank account flag up to them.”

Others said they had previously been investigated by the DWP after being wrongfully flagged. One said: “I had to get letters from each of these banks to clear my name as none of the banks answered the DWP. The mental anguish this has caused me is unbearable and nearly sent me under.”

‘Suspects by default’

Jasleen Chaggar, of campaign group Big Brother Watch, said vulnerable people would be treated as “suspects by default” and that the legislation would not tackle “serious fraudsters”.

“It will be disabled people, the elderly, carers and those living in poverty who are most likely to be wrongfully flagged by the algorithm, investigated and forced through lengthy, draining appeals.”

Trade bodies, including UK Finance, which represents large banks, have also stated their opposition to the new powers, raising concerns about operational challenges and the risk of unintended harm to vulnerable customers.

In 2024-25, just 6pc of DWP regional fraud investigations led to convictions, according to government data. In all fraud investigations, 2,191 had their benefit corrected, while 8,075 cases resulted in no charge.

A DWP spokesman said: “All powers in the Fraud, Error and Recovery Bill are underpinned by a principle of fairness and proportionality, with numerous safeguards and independent oversight in place.

“In cases of fraud and error, a human will always make any decisions that affect benefit entitlement, and DWP will not have access to benefit claimants’ bank accounts.

“We have a duty to the taxpayer, and this bill is set to save £1.5bn over the next five years, which together with wider reforms will save £9.6bn by 2030, according to OBR estimates.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: HS2 in turmoil after steel beam hits cage carrying two workers
data.description:
HS2 has been left in turmoil after the cage that two workers were standing inside was hit by a steel beam during construction work in London.

The workers, who were inside a cherrypicker’s cage, narrowly escaped being crushed by a steel beam swinging across a tunnel ventilation shaft, insiders said.

All HS2 construction work in London has now been halted pending a review of safety procedures, the company confirmed.

Everything except for essential maintenance has been “brought to a safe stop” as a result of the safety incident, an HS2 spokesman said.

A full shutdown of London construction will not only add to those delays but damage even further the railway line’s already-dented reputation.

The incident took place on September 29, when a gantry crane was being dismantled at the Green Park Way ventilation shaft in west London.

Four giant tunnel boring machines (TBMs) are currently being dismantled there and removed from the tunnels.

A steel beam from a crane being used to dismantle one of the TBMs struck a cherrypicker basket containing two workers at height.

Although nobody was injured, the incident posed a “serious risk to those involved”, HS2 acknowledged.

Halt comes amid ongoing ‘comprehensive reset’

Works across London are being carried out by a joint venture company called Skanska Costain STRABAG, which decided to stop work after discussions with HS2 bosses.

News of the halt to building works comes amid an ongoing “comprehensive reset” of the repeatedly delayed and over-budget project by Mark Wild, its chief executive.

Mr Wild has been tasked by the Labour Government to break HS2’s “cycle of cost increases and delays”.

He is expected to report back to Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, with recommendations on how to achieve that, early next year.

In a report completed shortly after he was appointed HS2’s chief executive last December, Mr Wild also indicated that the project will not be completed until between 2036 and 2039.

The HS2 boss told the Department for Transport (DfT) that the cost of completing HS2 has soared to £81bn in 2019 prices, Rail magazine reported.

With inflation taken into account, taxpayers will be picking up a final bill widely expected to exceed £100bn for the new railway line.

In its current form, HS2 will link London to Birmingham, shaving about ten minutes off the current journey time.

Originally, HS2 would have stretched well into northern England, but a series of cuts to the project thanks to vast cost overruns have seen its scope drastically reduced.

The project was originally conceived to add extra rail capacity in parallel to the West Coast Main Line, which has no space for future expansion of rail services.

Mr Wild told The Telegraph: “Safety is our number one priority at HS2. Due to a serious incident, works have been safely halted across the London tunnels section of the route.

“While no workers were physically harmed, our contractor, Skanska Costain STRABAG joint venture, is undertaking a comprehensive review of safety procedures and leadership on their sites before a safe restart can take place.”

data.title: Charity criticises ‘disturbing’ TfL video that encourages bringing cats on Tube
data.description:
A charity has criticised Transport for London (TfL) over a “disturbing” video in which it encouraged people to bring their cats on the Tube.

Cats Protection asked TfL to remove the social media video that showed cats being taken on the London Underground, on leads and released from their carrying baskets, with a caption that read: “This is your sign to start travelling with your pet around London.”

The post also showed the cats playing with toys inside a carriage, with a voiceover telling owners they can “let them relax on the floor”.

The charity called the idea “shocking”, and said that “busy stations and crowded underground platforms” would be “hugely stressful” for cats.

Nicky Trevorrow, senior behaviour manager at Cats Protection, said: “It is disturbing to see Transport for London apparently normalising passengers taking cats on the underground with a social media reel aimed at young people.

“Busy stations and crowded underground platforms with loud and sudden noises, such as trains arriving, announcements, and crowds, should be off limits for cats.

“It is shocking to think that anyone would travel on the Underground with their cat on a lead or draped on their shoulders. It is not only poor welfare, it’s unsafe.”

The TfL video advised cat owners to follow four basic rules for travelling with their pets on public transport, including going equipped with pet carriers and escape-proof harnesses, treats, water and toys.

It also told owners not to allow their cats to sit on passenger seats, but to place them inside their carriers or on the floor if kept on a lead.

The charity said it has formally contacted Transport for London asking for the video to be removed from the company’s social media channels.

Sarah Elliott, central veterinary officer at Cats Protection, added: “Restraining a cat on a lead or taking it on unnecessary journeys in backpacks can cause significant stress and inhibit a cat’s natural behaviours.

“Cats are territorial and like to roam freely, rather than go on leisure outings away from their familiar home. As well as the obvious safety and welfare concerns, cat harnesses prevent the flight response, and feeling trapped can significantly escalate stress in cats.”

A spokesman for Cats Protection said: “Promotion of cats travelling on the Tube risks encouraging more people to take an action which can be hugely stressful for cats.

“The charity advises, when a cat needs transporting, such as for a vet visit, a safe and sturdy carrier with a secure door should be used, rather than a cat backpack or collar and lead.”

TfL allows assistance dogs and “any other dog or inoffensive animal” to be taken on public transport, adding that owners “must keep it under control on a lead or in a suitable container and must not allow it on a seat”.

A TfL spokesman said: “We permit animals to travel on our network and anyone doing so should do so safely and in line with our conditions.”
